topics.title: Familiarization of system calls (fork, exec, getpid, exit, wait, close, stat etc) in operating system and demonstration of creating partition and installing operating system
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of system calls (fork, exec, getpid, exit, wait, close, stat etc) in operating system and demonstration of creating partition and installing operating system

# 🖥️ Module Experiment 2 — System Calls & OS Installation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **System Call** is the controlled interface through which a user-mode process requests a privileged service from the **kernel** of the operating system. It acts as the only legitimate gateway between **user space** (where applications run with restricted privileges) and **kernel space** (where OS code executes with full hardware access).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for Operating Systems Lab (PCITL408), Experiment 2 expects the student to:
1. Write C programs that invoke core POSIX system calls: `fork()`, `exec()`, `getpid()`, `getppid()`, `exit()`, `wait()`, `close()`, `stat()`.
2. Demonstrate the working of an actual operating system install — which conceptually relies on the *same* underlying system calls (disk I/O, filesystem creation, bootloader installation).

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Government Office"

Think of the **Operating System Kernel** as a strict government office that handles passports, licenses, and land records. As a citizen (user program), you cannot walk in and stamp your own passport — that would be chaos. Instead, you must:

1. Fill a form (the system call).
2. Submit it at the official counter (the system call interface / trap).
3. The officer (kernel) processes it.
4. You receive a stamped receipt (the return value).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Every privileged operation — creating a process, opening a file, reading from disk, allocating memory — MUST go through a system call. There is **no other legal way** for user code to access hardware.

### 1.2 Categories of System Calls (as per Silberschatz / KTU syllabus)

| Category | Examples | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **Process Control** | `fork()`, `exec()`, `wait()`, `exit()`, `getpid()` | Create, run, and terminate processes |
| **File Management** | `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `close()`, `stat()` | Manipulate files & directories |
| **Device Management** | `ioctl()`, `read()`, `write()` | Interact with I/O devices |
| **Information Maintenance** | `getpid()`, `time()`, `uname()` | Query system/OS data |
| **Communication** | `pipe()`, `shmget()`, `socket()` | Inter-process & network communication |
| **Memory Management** | `mmap()`, `brk()` | Allocate/release virtual memory |

### 1.3 The Eight System Calls in Focus

| # | System Call | Header | Purpose |
|---|-------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | `fork()` | `<unistd.h>` | Creates an exact duplicate (child) of the calling process |
| 2 | `exec()` family | `<unistd.h>` | Replaces current process image with a new program |
| 3 | `getpid()` | `<unistd.h>` | Returns the **PID** of the calling process |
| 4 | `getppid()` | `<unistd.h>` | Returns the **parent PID** |
| 5 | `exit()` | `<stdlib.h>` | Terminates the calling process; sends status to parent |
| 6 | `wait()` | `<sys/wait.h>` | Parent blocks until child terminates |
| 7 | `close()` | `<unistd.h>` | Closes an open file descriptor |
| 8 | `stat()` | `<sys/stat.h>` | Retrieves file metadata (size, permissions, timestamps) |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for KTU Viva:** "PID = Process ID. PPID = Parent PID. The very first process in a UNIX/Linux system is `init` (or `systemd`) with **PID = 1**."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 `fork()` — The Process Creator

`fork()` is the **only** way (besides `clone()` in Linux) to create a new process in UNIX. It creates a nearly identical copy of the calling process.

**Prototype:**
```c
pid_t fork(void);
```

**Return Values — THE CRITICAL POINT:**

| Return Value | Meaning |
|--------------|---------|
| **Negative (`-1`)** | Error — child could not be created (e.g., process table full) |
| **Zero (`0`)** | Returned in the **child** process |
| **Positive (>0)** | Returned in the **parent** — value is the **PID of the child** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After `fork()`, **both** parent and child continue execution from the **same instruction** following the `fork()` call. Their memory spaces are **independent** (copy-on-write in modern Linux).

### 2.2 `exec()` Family — The Process Transformer

`exec()` does **not** create a new process; it **replaces** the current process image with a new program. The PID stays the same.

Common variants:

| Function | Path Search? | Argument Style |
|----------|--------------|----------------|
| `execl(path, arg0, ..., NULL)` | No | List |
| `execlp(file, arg0, ..., NULL)` | Yes (uses `PATH`) | List |
| `execv(path, argv[])` | No | Vector |
| `execvp(file, argv[])` | Yes | Vector |

### 2.3 `getpid()` and `getppid()`

```c
pid_t getpid(void);   // returns PID of current process
pid_t getppid(void);  // returns PID of parent process
```

- PIDs are unique **per boot session** on UNIX.
- Maximum PID is typically `32768` (Linux default, configurable via `/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max`).

### 2.4 `exit()` vs `return` in `main()`

| Aspect | `return 0;` (in `main`) | `exit(status);` |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Cleans up stdio buffers | Yes | Yes |
| Calls `atexit()` handlers | Yes | Yes |
| Closes all `fd` via `_exit()` | Yes | Yes |
| Can be called from any function | **No** | **Yes** |
| Returns control to caller | Yes | No (terminates process) |

### 2.5 `wait()` and `waitpid()`

```c
pid_t wait(int *status);
pid_t waitpid(pid_t pid, int *status, int options);
```

- `wait()` **blocks** the parent until *any* child terminates.
- `waitpid()` can wait for a *specific* child or use `WNOHANG` for non-blocking check.
- `WIFEXITED(status)` and `WEXITSTATUS(status)` macros extract the exit code.

### 2.6 `close()`

```c
int close(int fd);
```
- Closes the file descriptor `fd`.
- Returns `0` on success, `-1` on error.
- Releases the kernel file table entry. **FD limits**: default `1024` per process (check with `ulimit -n`).

### 2.7 `stat()`

```c
int stat(const char *path, struct stat *buf);
```
- Populates a `struct stat` containing:
  - `st_mode` — file type and permissions
  - `st_size` — file size in bytes
  - `st_uid`, `st_gid` — owner
  - `st_atime`, `st_mtime`, `st_ctime` — access, modify, change times
  - `st_ino` — inode number
  - `st_nlink` — number of hard links

### 2.8 Demonstration: Creating Partitions & Installing an OS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Note:** Although Experiment 2 is a C-programming lab, the syllabus title also mandates a "**demonstration**" of partition creation and OS installation. This is typically shown via:
> - Tools: `fdisk`, `gdisk`, `parted`, `GParted` (GUI)
> - Creating **MBR** (Master Boot Record) or **GPT** (GUID Partition Table) partitions
> - Steps: Download ISO → Make bootable USB (using `Rufus` / `dd` / `Etcher`) → Boot → Choose partition → Install bootloader (GRUB) → Install OS

**Partition Table Comparison (High-Yield for KTU):**

| Feature | **MBR** | **GPT** |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Max Disk Size | **2 TB** | **9.4 ZB** (effectively unlimited) |
| Max Partitions | **4** primary | **128** primary |
| Boot Mode | Legacy BIOS | UEFI |
| Backup Table | No | Yes (at end of disk) |

**Standard Linux Partition Layout:**

| Mount Point | Typical Size | Filesystem | Purpose |
|-------------|--------------|------------|---------|
| `/boot` | **512 MB – 1 GB** | `ext4` | Kernel + GRUB |
| `/` (root) | **20 – 50 GB** | `ext4` | OS files |
| `swap` | **Equal to RAM** (or 2× RAM if RAM < 4 GB) | `swap` | Virtual memory |
| `/home` | Remaining space | `ext4` | User data |

### 2.9 KTU Reference Sheet

| Concept | Key Value / Formula |
|---------|---------------------|
| PID range (Linux default) | `1` to `32768` |
| Default FD limit per process | `1024` |
| `fork()` returns in child | `0` |
| `fork()` returns in parent | Child's PID |
| `fork()` returns on failure | `-1` |
| MBR max disk | `2 TB` |
| GPT max partitions | `128` |
| Standard `ext4` inode ratio | 1 inode per **4 KB** block (tunable) |
| Process states | New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Terminated |
| `wait()` return value | PID of terminated child (or `-1`) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Walkthroughs, Sample Programs & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Program 1: Demonstrating `fork()`, `getpid()`, `getppid()`

```c
/* program1_fork.c */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>

int main(void) {
    pid_t pid;

    printf("Before fork: PID = %d, PPID = %d\n", getpid(), getppid());

    pid = fork();                          /* STEP 1: create child */

    if (pid < 0) {                         /* STEP 2: error check */
        perror("fork failed");
        return 1;
    }
    else if (pid == 0) {                   /* STEP 3: child branch */
        printf("[CHILD]  PID = %d, PPID = %d, fork() returned %d\n",
               getpid(), getppid(), pid);
    }
    else {                                 /* STEP 4: parent branch */
        printf("[PARENT] PID = %d, Child PID = %d, fork() returned %d\n",
               getpid(), pid, pid);
    }

    printf("Common line: This runs in BOTH parent and child. PID = %d\n",
           getpid());

    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output (sample):**
```
Before fork: PID = 2540, PPID = 1890
[PARENT] PID = 2540, Child PID = 2541, fork() returned 2541
Common line: This runs in BOTH parent and child. PID = 2540
[CHILD]  PID = 2541, PPID = 2540, fork() returned 0
Common line: This runs in BOTH parent and child. PID = 2541
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **order** of PARENT/CHILD output lines is **non-deterministic** — it depends on the scheduler. This is a classic KTU viva question!

### 3.2 Program 2: `wait()` Synchronization

```c
/* program2_wait.c */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>

int main(void) {
    pid_t pid = fork();

    if (pid == 0) {                          /* child */
        printf("Child: starting work, PID = %d\n", getpid());
        sleep(2);                            /* simulate work */
        printf("Child: exiting with code 7\n");
        exit(7);                             /* STEP: child terminates */
    }
    else if (pid > 0) {                      /* parent */
        int status;
        printf("Parent: waiting for child %d...\n", pid);
        wait(&status);                       /* STEP: block here */
        if (WIFEXITED(status)) {
            printf("Parent: child exited normally with code %d\n",
                   WEXITSTATUS(status));
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Key Analytical Points:**

1. Without `wait()`: parent may exit **before** child → child becomes an **orphan** and is adopted by `init` (PID 1).
2. Without `fork()` cleanup: child becomes a **zombie** (defunct process in `ps`).
3. The `status` integer is a bit-packed field — the **low 8 bits** (after right shift) hold the exit code.

### 3.3 Program 3: `exec()` — Replacing Process Image

```c
/* program3_exec.c */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(void) {
    pid_t pid = fork();

    if (pid == 0) {
        /* CHILD: replace itself with `ls -l /tmp` */
        printf("Child: about to exec ls\n");
        execlp("ls", "ls", "-l", "/tmp", (char *)NULL);

        /* If execlp returns, it FAILED */
        perror("execlp failed");
        exit(1);
    }
    else if (pid > 0) {
        wait(NULL);
        printf("Parent: child has finished executing ls\n");
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Analytical Reasoning:**

$$\text{PID}_{\text{after exec}} = \text{PID}_{\text{before exec}}$$

The PID does **not** change, but the **code, stack, heap, and data segments are completely overwritten**. The new program starts at its `main()` function.

### 3.4 Program 4: `close()` and `stat()`

```c
/* program4_close_stat.c */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>

int main(void) {
    int fd = open("test.txt", O_RDONLY);
    if (fd < 0) { perror("open"); return 1; }

    struct stat st;
    if (stat("test.txt", &st) < 0) { perror("stat"); return 1; }

    printf("File size       : %ld bytes\n", (long)st.st_size);
    printf("Inode number    : %ld\n",     (long)st.st_ino);
    printf("Permissions     : %o\n",       st.st_mode & 0777);
    printf("Hard link count : %ld\n",     (long)st.st_nlink);

    close(fd);
    printf("File descriptor %d closed.\n", fd);
    return 0;
}
```

**Compilation Command (KTU Lab Standard):**
```bash
gcc program4_close_stat.c -o prog4
./prog4
```

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Behavior of `fork()` + `exec()` + `wait()`

| Scenario | `fork()` called? | `exec()` called? | `wait()` called? | Resulting Process Tree |
|----------|------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Plain C program | No | No | No | Single process |
| Use `fork()` only | Yes | No | No | Parent may exit → orphan; child → zombie if parent doesn't wait |
| Use `fork()` + `wait()` | Yes | No | Yes | Clean parent-child sync; child reaped properly |
| Use `fork()` + `exec()` | Yes | Yes | Optional | Child becomes a different program |
| Use `fork()` + `exec()` + `wait()` | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Shell-like** behavior (this is how bash runs commands) |

### 3.6 Step-by-Step: Creating a Partition and Installing an OS

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **demonstration** half of the experiment. Below is the exact logical sequence a student would narrate during the KTU lab viva.

**Step 1 — Identify the Disk**
```bash
lsblk                # list block devices
sudo fdisk -l /dev/sda
```

**Step 2 — Create Partitions Using `fdisk` (MBR style)**

| Sub-step | Command | Action |
|----------|---------|--------|
| 1 | `sudo fdisk /dev/sda` | Open partitioning tool |
| 2 | `o` | Create a new empty **DOS partition table** |
| 3 | `n` → `p` → `1` → `Enter` → `+512M` | Create 512 MB primary partition for `/boot` |
| 4 | `n` → `p` → `2` → `Enter` → `+20G` | Create 20 GB primary partition for `/` |
| 5 | `n` → `p` → `3` → `Enter` → `+2G` | Create 2 GB primary partition for `swap` |
| 6 | `t` → `3` → `82` | Set partition 3 type to **Linux swap** |
| 7 | `w` | Write table to disk and exit |

**Step 3 — Format the Partitions**
```bash
sudo mkfs.ext4  /dev/sda1      # /boot
sudo mkfs.ext4  /dev/sda2      # /
sudo mkswap     /dev/sda3      # swap
sudo swapon     /dev/sda3
```

**Step 4 — Mount and Install (Ubuntu/Debian debootstrap example)**
```bash
sudo mount /dev/sda2 /mnt
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/boot
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/boot
sudo debootstrap focal /mnt http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/
```

**Step 5 — Install the Bootloader (GRUB)**
```bash
sudo grub-install --target=x86_64-efi --efi-directory=/boot/efi --bootloader-id=ubuntu /dev/sda
sudo update-grub
```

**Step 6 — Reboot and Verify**
```bash
sudo reboot
uname -a    # verify installed kernel
```

**Verification Formula (sanity check):**

$$\text{Number of partitions created} + \text{bootloader installed} + \text{filesystem mounted} = \text{Successful Install}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 User Space vs Kernel Space — The System Call Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["User Application printf fork open"] --> B["Wrapper Function glibc fork"]
    B --> C["System Call Interface Trap to Kernel"]
    C --> D["Kernel Mode Privileged Execution"]
    D --> E["Hardware Disk CPU Memory"]
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> F["Return Value / errno"]
    F --> A
```

### 4.2 Process Creation Flow — `fork()` + `exec()` + `wait()`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Parent Process PID 100"] --> F["fork call"]
    F --> C1["Child Process PID 101"]
    F --> C2["Parent continues after fork"]
    C1 --> E["execlp replaces process image"]
    E --> N["New Program running as PID 101"]
    N --> EX["child exits with status code"]
    EX --> W["wait blocks parent"]
    W --> P2["Parent resumes and reaps child"]
```

### 4.3 File Descriptor Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OF["open returns fd 3"] --> U["read write lseek using fd 3"]
    U --> CL["close fd 3"]
    CL --> FR["File table entry released"]
    CL --> Z["fd 3 is now free for reuse"]
```

### 4.4 Disk Partitioning & OS Installation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DISK["Raw Disk /dev/sda"] --> PT["Partition Table MBR or GPT"]
    PT --> P1["sda1 /boot 512 MB ext4"]
    PT --> P2["sda2 / 20 GB ext4"]
    PT --> P3["sda3 swap 2 GB"]
    P1 --> BS["Bootloader GRUB installed in sda1"]
    P2 --> K["Kernel vmlinuz placed in /boot"]
    P2 --> FS["Root filesystem populated by installer"]
    BS --> R["Reboot"]
    K --> R
    FS --> R
    R --> OS["Running Linux OS"]
```

### 4.5 Memory Layout of a Process (Why `exec()` is a "Replacement")

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L["High Address 0xFFFFFFFF"] --> A["Stack grows downward"]
    A --> H["Heap grows upward"]
    H --> D["Data Segment globals and static"]
    D --> T["Text Segment code readonly"]
    T --> Z["Low Address 0x00000000"]
    EX["execlp call"] --> N["Entire address space wiped and replaced with new program"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** What is a system call? List any four categories of system calls with one example each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *system call* is the programmatic interface through which a user process requests a service from the OS kernel. It is the **only legal mechanism** for user-mode code to access kernel-mode services.
>
> Four categories:
> 1. **Process Control** — `fork()`, `exit()`
> 2. **File Management** — `open()`, `close()`
> 3. **Information Maintenance** — `getpid()`, `time()`
> 4. **Communication** — `pipe()`, `socket()`

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between `fork()` and `exec()` system calls.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | `fork()` | `exec()` |
> |--------|----------|----------|
> | New process created? | **Yes** | **No** (replaces current) |
> | PID changes? | **Yes** (child gets new PID) | **No** |
> | Code changes? | No (duplicate of parent) | **Yes** (new program loaded) |
> | Return value | 0 in child, child PID in parent | Returns only on **failure** |

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE – Module 2, 14-Mark Choice Question]**

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the working of the `fork()` system call with a suitable C program. Discuss the possible return values and what they indicate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program that creates a child process using `fork()`. The child should execute the `ls -l` command using `execlp()`, and the parent should wait for the child to finish using `wait()`. Print the exit status of the child. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Working of `fork()` (7 marks):**

`fork()` creates a **child process** that is an almost exact duplicate of the parent. The two processes run **concurrently** from the instruction immediately following the `fork()` call.

**Return Values:**
- **Negative (`-1`):** Error — child not created (e.g., process table full, reached user process limit).
- **Zero (`0`):** Returned in the **child** process.
- **Positive (>0):** Returned in the **parent** — value is the **PID of the newly created child**.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>

int main(void) {
    pid_t pid;

    printf("Before fork: PID = %d\n", getpid());
    pid = fork();

    if (pid < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "fork failed\n");
        return 1;
    }
    else if (pid == 0) {
        /* Child branch */
        printf("CHILD : PID = %d, PPID = %d, fork returned %d\n",
               getpid(), getppid(), pid);
    }
    else {
        /* Parent branch */
        printf("PARENT: PID = %d, Child PID = %d, fork returned %d\n",
               getpid(), pid, pid);
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation (for full marks):**
- Before `fork()`: single process prints its PID.
- After `fork()`: **two** processes exist.
- The `if-else` distinguishes them using the return value.
- Both processes execute the `return 0;` line independently.
- The `pid` variable has **different values** in parent and child due to separate address spaces (Copy-on-Write).

---

**(b) `fork` + `execlp` + `wait` Program (7 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>

int main(void) {
    pid_t pid = fork();

    if (pid < 0) {
        perror("fork");
        return 1;
    }
    else if (pid == 0) {
        /* Child: replace image with ls -l */
        printf("Child: running ls -l\n");
        execlp("ls", "ls", "-l", (char *)NULL);
        /* If we reach here, exec failed */
        perror("execlp");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }
    else {
        /* Parent: wait for child */
        int status;
        printf("Parent: waiting for child (PID %d)\n", pid);
        wait(&status);

        if (WIFEXITED(status))
            printf("Parent: child exited with code %d\n",
                   WEXITSTATUS(status));
        else
            printf("Parent: child did not exit normally\n");
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Output (sample):**
```
Child: running ls -l
Parent: waiting for child (PID 4521)
total 12
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user  380 May 10 10:20 file1.c
drwxr-xr-x 2 user user 4096 May 10 10:21 dirA
Parent: child exited with code 0
```

**Mark-by-mark breakdown:**
- Correct header inclusions — **1 mark**
- `fork()` call with return-value handling — **2 marks**
- `execlp()` with correct argument list and NULL terminator — **2 marks**
- `wait()` with `WIFEXITED` / `WEXITSTATUS` macros — **2 marks**

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the `stat()` system call. Write a C program that uses `stat()` to display the size, inode number, and permissions of a file named `"input.txt"`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the steps involved in creating disk partitions and installing a Linux operating system. Compare MBR and GPT partitioning schemes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) `stat()` System Call (7 marks):**

`stat()` retrieves metadata about a file (or directory, symlink, etc.) and stores it in a `struct stat`.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>

int main(void) {
    struct stat st;

    if (stat("input.txt", &st) < 0) {
        perror("stat");
        return 1;
    }

    printf("File size        : %ld bytes\n", (long)st.st_size);
    printf("Inode number     : %ld\n",       (long)st.st_ino);
    printf("Permissions      : %o\n",        st.st_mode & 0777);
    printf("Hard link count  : %ld\n",       (long)st.st_nlink);
    printf("Owner UID        : %d\n",        st.st_uid);
    printf("Last modified    : %ld\n",       (long)st.st_mtime);

    return 0;
}
```

**Output (sample):**
```
File size        : 1024 bytes
Inode number     : 524301
Permissions      : 644
Hard link count  : 1
Owner UID        : 1000
Last modified    : 1715234582
```

**Mark-by-mark breakdown:**
- Correct struct definition and `stat()` call — **2 marks**
- Use of `st_size`, `st_ino`, `st_mode` — **2 marks**
- Error handling with `perror()` — **1 mark**
- Compilation and correct output — **2 marks**

---

**(b) Partitioning & OS Installation (7 marks):**

**Steps to Create Partitions and Install Linux:**

1. **Identify target disk** using `lsblk` or `fdisk -l`.
2. **Partition the disk** using `fdisk` (MBR) or `gdisk` (GPT):
   - `/boot` — 512 MB
   - `/` — 20 GB
   - `swap` — 2 GB (or equal to RAM)
3. **Format partitions** with filesystems:
   - `mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda1`
   - `mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda2`
   - `mkswap /dev/sda3`
4. **Mount** target partitions at `/mnt`.
5. **Install OS** via installer, `debootstrap`, or by copying live files.
6. **Install bootloader** (GRUB) into the disk's MBR/EFI partition.
7. **Reboot** and verify with `uname -a`.

**MBR vs GPT Comparison (3 marks reserved in this sub-question):**

| Feature | **MBR** | **GPT** |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Max Disk Size | **2 TB** | **9.4 ZB** (theoretical) |
| Max Partitions | **4** primary | **128** primary |
| Boot Mode | Legacy BIOS | UEFI |
| Redundancy | Single table | Primary + backup table |
| Sector Size | 512 bytes | 512 or **4096** bytes |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `fork()` with `exec()`** — students often say "`exec()` creates a new process". It does NOT. `exec()` *replaces* the current process image.
> 2. **Forgetting the NULL terminator in `execlp()`** — this causes undefined behavior; the kernel may interpret garbage as additional arguments.
> 3. **Not checking `fork()` return for `-1`** — losing 1 mark on error handling.
> 4. **Writing `wait()` without a status variable** — `wait(NULL)` works but the examiner may deduct if the question explicitly asks for exit status.
> 5. **Confusing `close()` with `unlink()`** — `close()` releases the file descriptor; `unlink()` deletes the directory entry.
> 6. **Partition table confusion** — saying MBR supports 128 partitions (that's GPT).
> 7. **Missing `#include <sys/wait.h>`** — `wait()` will not compile without it.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 A **system call** is the **only legal interface** between user programs and the kernel.
- 🔑 `fork()` returns **3 possible values**: `-1` (error), `0` (child), `>0` (parent, child PID).
- 🔑 `exec()` **replaces** the process image — PID is **preserved**, code is **overwritten**.
- 🔑 `wait()` makes the parent **block** until any child terminates; it also **reaps** the child (prevents zombies).
- 🔑 `getpid()` → own PID; `getppid()` → parent's PID; first process = `init`/PID `1`.
- 🔑 `close(fd)` releases the file descriptor; default limit = `1024` per process.
- 🔑 `stat(path, &buf)` fills a `struct stat` with file metadata (size, inode, mode, timestamps).
- 🔑 **MBR**: max `2 TB`, `4` primary partitions, legacy BIOS.
- 🔑 **GPT**: up to `128` partitions, no realistic size limit, UEFI-based.
- 🔑 Standard Linux layout: `/boot` (512 MB) + `/` (20 GB) + `swap` (≈ RAM) + `/home` (rest).
- 🔑 Bootloader **GRUB** is installed after filesystems are populated.
- 🔑 Always compile with `gcc file.c -o output` and run with `./output`.
- 🔑 Order of output after `fork()` is **non-deterministic** (scheduler-dependent).
- 🔑 **Orphan** = parent exits first; **Zombie** = child exits but parent didn't `wait()`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of process and thread (life cycle of process): (i) process creation and termination, (ii) thread creation and termination
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of process and thread (life cycle of process): (i) process creation and termination, (ii) thread creation and termination

# Experiment 2: Process and Thread Implementation in Linux

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Process — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** A **process** is a program in execution. It is the fundamental unit of work in an operating system and consists of an executing program code (text section), its current activity (program counter, processor registers), a process stack (temporary data such as function parameters, return addresses, local variables), a data section (global variables), and a heap (memory dynamically allocated during run time). Each process is represented in the kernel by a **Process Control Block (PCB)** — a data structure containing all bookkeeping information: process ID, state, program counter, CPU registers, memory management info, accounting info, and I/O status.

### 1.2 Thread — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution. It is a lightweight subprocess that shares the **code section, data section, and OS resources** (open files, signals) with its peer threads belonging to the same process, but maintains its **own thread ID, program counter, register set, and stack**. A process with at least one thread is called a **multithreaded process**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **large software company (the Process)**:

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | OS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Process** | The entire company building a product | A running program in memory |
| **Thread** | A worker inside the company | Independent flow of control |
| **Process Creation** | A parent company spinning off a startup | `fork()` system call |
| **Process Termination** | The startup shutting down | `exit()` system call |
| **Thread Creation** | Hiring a new worker in the same company | `pthread_create()` |
| **Shared Resources** | Shared office space, internet, printers | Code, data, heap, open files |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for exam:** *Process = heavy container (has its own everything). Thread = light backpack inside the container (shares most stuff, carries only its own register state).*

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (POSIX/Linux)

- **`PID`** — Process Identifier; a unique positive integer assigned by the kernel. Default range: **2 to 32768** on 32-bit systems, much larger on 64-bit.
- **`PPID`** — Parent Process Identifier.
- **`TID`** — Thread Identifier; in Linux, threads are exposed as tasks with their own `TID`; the main thread's `TID == PID`.
- **`getpid()` / `getppid()`** — POSIX calls to fetch the calling process's PID and parent's PID.
- **`pthread_self()`** — POSIX call to fetch the calling thread's TID.
- **`ps -ef`** — Linux command to list all running processes (essential for verifying orphan/zombie behavior in lab).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Life Cycle of a Process — State Diagram Theory

A process moves through a well-defined set of **states**. The KTU 2024 module specifically names the *life cycle of a process* — meaning you must draw and explain the **5-state process model**.

### The Five Process States

1. **New** — Process is being created; PCB is being constructed; not yet admitted to the ready queue.
2. **Ready** — Process is waiting to be assigned to a processor. It has all resources except the CPU.
3. **Running** — Instructions are being executed by the CPU. Exactly one process per CPU core can be in this state.
4. **Blocked / Waiting** — Process is waiting for some event to occur (I/O completion, signal, child termination). Cannot run even if the CPU is free.
5. **Terminated / Exit** — Process has finished execution. OS is reaping its PCB.

### State Transition Triggers (KTU High-Yield)

| From → To | Transition Name | Trigger Event |
|---|---|---|
| New → Ready | Admit | Long-term scheduler admits the process |
| Ready → Running | Dispatch / Schedule | Short-term scheduler picks the process |
| Running → Ready | Preempt / Time-out | Time quantum expired (or higher-priority process arrived) |
| Running → Blocked | Block / I/O wait | Process requests I/O or waits on a system resource |
| Blocked → Ready | Wake-up | The waited event occurs (I/O completes) |
| Running → Terminated | Exit | Process calls `exit()` or is killed by signal |

## 2.2 Process Creation — The `fork()` Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanics of `fork()`:** When a process invokes `fork()`, the kernel creates an almost-exact duplicate of the calling process. The new process is called the **child process**. The original is the **parent process**. Both continue execution from the point immediately after the `fork()` call, but with **divergent return values**:
> - **Parent** receives the **child's PID** (a positive integer).
> - **Child** receives **0**.
> - On failure, the parent receives **-1** and no child is created (no child process is created).

### Header File & Function Signature

```c
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>

pid_t fork(void);
```

- `pid_t` is a POSIX type alias for `int` (or `long` on some systems).
- `fork()` is declared in `<unistd.h>` and `<sys/types.h>`.

### What `fork()` Duplicates vs. Shares

| Resource | Behaviour After `fork()` |
|---|---|
| Code (text) section | **Duplicated** (copy-on-write, lazy) |
| Data, BSS, heap | **Duplicated** (copy-on-write) |
| Stack | **Duplicated** (copy-on-write) |
| Open file descriptors | **Shared** (same underlying file description) |
| Signal handlers | **Inherited** |
| Environment, argv | **Inherited** |
| Current working directory | **Inherited** |
| Nice value, scheduling policy | **Inherited** |
| Pending signals | **NOT inherited** (cleared in child) |

## 2.3 Process Termination — Two Pathways

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Termination:** The process calls `exit()` (C library wrapper) → invokes `_exit()` (system call) → kernel frees most resources, marks zombie, sends `SIGCHLD` to parent.

| Exit Mechanism | Description |
|---|---|
| `return` from `main()` | Implicitly calls `exit(main_return_value)` |
| `exit(status)` | C library call: flushes stdio buffers, runs `atexit()` handlers, then calls `_exit()` |
| `_exit(status)` | Direct system call: terminates immediately, no cleanup |
| `abort()` | Raises `SIGABRT`, terminates with core dump |
| Received uncatchable signal (e.g., `SIGKILL`, `SIGSEGV`) | Abnormal termination |

> [!WARNING]
> **Zombie vs. Orphan — KTU favourite question:**
> - **Zombie process:** Child has terminated but parent has not yet called `wait()` / `waitpid()`. The PCB remains in the kernel, the child is "dead but not reaped". A long-lived zombie is a resource leak.
> - **Orphan process:** Parent has terminated before the child. The orphan is **adopted by `init` (PID = 1)**, which will eventually `wait()` on it. Orphans are not leaks — they are normal.

## 2.4 The `exec()` Family — Replacing a Process Image

After `fork()`, the child typically calls `exec()` to replace its memory image with a new program. The six members of the `exec` family are listed below — **KTU expects you to know at least `execl`, `execv`, `execlp`, and `execvp`**.

| Function | Path Argument | Argument Style | Search `PATH`? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `execl(path, arg0, ..., NULL)` | Full path required | List (variadic) | No |
| `execlp(file, arg0, ..., NULL)` | Filename allowed | List (variadic) | **Yes** |
| `execle(path, arg0, ..., NULL, envp)` | Full path required | List + env | No |
| `execv(path, argv[])` | Full path required | Vector (`char *argv[]`) | No |
| `execvp(file, argv[])` | Filename allowed | Vector (`char *argv[]`) | **Yes** |
| `execve(path, argv[], envp[])` | Full path required | Vector + env | No (raw syscall) |

## 2.5 The `wait()` System Call — Reaping a Child

```c
#include <sys/wait.h>
pid_t wait(int *status);
pid_t waitpid(pid_t pid, int *status, int options);
```

- `wait()` blocks the parent until **any one child** terminates.
- `waitpid(-1, ...)` is equivalent to `wait()`.
- `WIFEXITED(status)` → true if child terminated normally.
- `WEXITSTATUS(status)` → extracts the low 8 bits of the exit code.
- `WIFSIGNALED(status)` → true if child terminated by an uncaught signal.
- `WTERMSIG(status)` → returns the signal number.

## 2.6 Thread Creation — The POSIX Threads API

> [!NOTE]
> **Header file:** `#include <pthread.h>`
> **Linker flag:** Compile with `-lpthread` (or `-pthread`).

### Core Pthread Functions (KTU High-Yield)

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `pthread_create(thread, attr, start_routine, arg)` | Create a new thread |
| `pthread_exit(value)` | Terminate the calling thread, return `value` |
| `pthread_join(thread, retval)` | Block until `thread` terminates, capture return value |
| `pthread_self()` | Return TID of calling thread |
| `pthread_equal(t1, t2)` | Compare two TIDs (cannot use `==` portably) |
| `pthread_cancel(thread)` | Send cancellation request to a thread |

### `pthread_create` Signature

```c
int pthread_create(pthread_t *thread,
                   const pthread_attr_t *attr,
                   void *(*start_routine)(void *),
                   void *arg);
```

- `thread` — output: stores the new thread's TID.
- `attr` — input: thread attributes (NULL = default).
- `start_routine` — input: function pointer where the new thread begins execution.
- `arg` — input: single `void *` argument passed to `start_routine`.

## 2.7 Process vs. Thread — The Comparison Table (Always Asked)

| Property | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | **Independent** | **Shared** with peer threads |
| Creation cost | **Heavy** (`fork`, copy-on-write) | **Light** (`pthread_create`) |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Shared globals (race-condition risk) |
| Termination | `exit()` / signal | `pthread_exit()` / return from start_routine |
| Context-switch cost | **High** (TLB flush, page tables) | **Low** (same page tables) |
| Crash isolation | One process crash does NOT kill others | One thread crash can kill entire process |
| Identifier | `PID` (`getpid()`) | `TID` (`pthread_self()`) |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web servers (Apache, Nginx)** use a thread pool to handle thousands of concurrent client requests within a single process — a single `fork()` per request would never scale.
- **Database engines (PostgreSQL)** spawn one process per connection but use threads internally for parallel query execution.
- **GUI applications** keep the UI responsive by running long computations on a worker thread while the main thread handles the event loop.
- **Chrome browser** famously uses one process per tab for crash isolation, with multiple threads per tab for rendering, networking, and JavaScript execution.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / API | Meaning | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|
| `fork()` | Create child process | Returns `pid_t` (0/child_PID/-1) |
| `getpid()` | Get current process ID | Returns `pid_t` |
| `getppid()` | Get parent process ID | Returns `pid_t` |
| `exit(n)` | Terminate with status `n` | `n ∈ [0, 255]` |
| `_exit(n)` | Immediate kernel exit (no cleanup) | `n ∈ [0, 255]` |
| `wait(&s)` | Wait for any child | `s` is int holding status |
| `execvp(file, argv)` | Replace image with `file` | Returns int (-1 on err) |
| `pthread_create(&t,...)` | Create thread | Returns int (0 on success) |
| `pthread_exit(v)` | Exit thread with value `v` | `v` is `void *` |
| `pthread_join(t, &r)` | Wait for thread `t` | `r` receives `void *` |
| `pthread_self()` | Get current thread ID | Returns `pthread_t` |
| N total processes after N `fork()`s | $2^N$ | (excluding failures) |

> **Critical rule for KTU exams:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value to keep the table intact. Never use the bare `|` pipe character.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Programs & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Exam Convention (KTU 2024):** Every program must be presented with **(a) Aim, (b) Algorithm, (c) Program, (d) Sample Output, (e) Result**. Examiner marks depend on each section being clearly labeled.

## 3.1 Experiment 2(a) — Process Creation, Termination & Life-Cycle Observation

### 3.1.1 Aim

To write a C program that creates a child process using the `fork()` system call, demonstrates the divergent return values in parent and child, observes the **life cycle states** (New → Ready → Running → Terminated), illustrates **zombie** and **orphan** scenarios, and terminates cleanly using `exit()` and `wait()`.

### 3.1.2 Algorithm

**Step 1.** Start the program — this creates the **parent process** (initially in Running state).

**Step 2.** Invoke `fork()`. The kernel creates a **new process** (enters New state, then immediately Ready, then eligible to run). Both parent and child resume execution from the line after `fork()`.

**Step 3.** Test the return value of `fork()`:

- If `pid < 0` → `fork()` failed; print error; terminate.
- If `pid == 0` → this is the **child branch**. Print `getpid()`, `getppid()`. Sleep for a configurable time. Demonstrate two sub-scenarios:
  - **Zombie scenario:** child terminates, parent does **not** call `wait()` — child becomes zombie.
  - **Orphan scenario:** parent terminates *before* child — child becomes orphan, adopted by `init` (PID 1). Use `sleep()` in the parent before its own termination.
- If `pid > 0` → this is the **parent branch**. Print `getpid()` and the child's PID returned by `fork()`. Call `wait(&status)` to **block** until the child terminates. Use `WIFEXITED` / `WEXITSTATUS` to extract the exit code.

**Step 4.** Parent calls `exit(0)`. Kernel reaps the parent PCB.

### 3.1.3 Program — Master Demonstration (Linux, GCC)

```c
/* exp2a_process_lifecycle.c
 * KTU PCITL408 - Experiment 2(a)
 * Demonstrates process creation, zombie, orphan, and life cycle.
 * Compile: gcc exp2a_process_lifecycle.c -o exp2a
 */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>

int main(void)
{
    pid_t pid;
    int   status;

    printf("[BEFORE FORK] PID=%d  PPID=%d\n", getpid(), getppid());

    pid = fork();

    if (pid < 0) {
        perror("fork failed");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }
    else if (pid == 0) {
        /* ----------------- CHILD ----------------- */
        printf("\n[CHILD]  PID=%d  PPID=%d  fork() returned %d\n",
               getpid(), getppid(), pid);
        printf("[CHILD]  I will sleep 2 seconds, then exit.\n");
        sleep(2);
        printf("[CHILD]  Exiting now with status 42.\n");
        exit(42);                          /* Normal termination */
    }
    else {
        /* ----------------- PARENT ---------------- */
        printf("\n[PARENT] PID=%d  Child PID=%d  fork() returned %d\n",
               getpid(), pid, pid);

        printf("[PARENT] Sleeping 5 seconds. Run 'ps -ef | grep exp2a' "
               "during this window to see the child as ZOMBIE (Z).\n");
        sleep(5);                          /* Child will be zombie here */

        printf("[PARENT] Calling wait() to reap child...\n");
        wait(&status);

        if (WIFEXITED(status))
            printf("[PARENT] Child exited normally with code %d.\n",
                   WEXITSTATUS(status));
        else if (WIFSIGNALED(status))
            printf("[PARENT] Child killed by signal %d.\n",
                   WTERMSIG(status));

        printf("[PARENT] Now spawning an ORPHAN demo...\n");

        pid_t orphan = fork();
        if (orphan == 0) {
            /* Grand-child sleeps; its parent (the middle process)
               will exit, leaving this one orphaned.                */
            printf("[ORPHAN-CHILD] PID=%d  PPID=%d  sleeping 4s...\n",
                   getpid(), getppid());
            sleep(4);
            printf("[ORPHAN-CHILD] Awake. My new PPID is %d "
                   "(should be 1 = init).\n", getppid());
            exit(0);
        }

        /* Middle process exits immediately, abandoning orphan-child */
        printf("[PARENT] Exiting without waiting for orphan. Bye.\n");
        exit(0);
    }
}
```

### 3.1.4 Sample Output (Annotated)

```
[BEFORE FORK] PID=10234  PPID=5678

[PARENT] PID=10234  Child PID=10235  fork() returned 10235
[PARENT] Sleeping 5 seconds. Run 'ps -ef | grep exp2a' ...

[CHILD]  PID=10235  PPID=10234  fork() returned 0
[CHILD]  I will sleep 2 seconds, then exit.
[CHILD]  Exiting now with status 42.

--- At t = 2s, run in another terminal:  ps -ef | grep exp2a ---
UID  PID  PPID  C  STIME  TTY  STAT  TIME  CMD
user 10234 5678  0  10:00  pts/0 S     0:00  ./exp2a
user 10235 10234 0  10:00  pts/0 Z     0:00  [exp2a]   <defunct>
--- STAT column = Z -> ZOMBIE ---

[PARENT] Calling wait() to reap child...
[PARENT] Child exited normally with code 42.
[PARENT] Now spawning an ORPHAN demo...
[ORPHAN-CHILD] PID=10236  PPID=10234  sleeping 4s...
[PARENT] Exiting without waiting for orphan. Bye.
[ORPHAN-CHILD] Awake. My new PPID is 1 (should be 1 = init).
```

### 3.1.5 Result

The program demonstrates:
1. **Process creation** — `fork()` produces a child with a unique PID and PPID = parent's PID.
2. **Divergent return values** — parent receives child's PID; child receives 0.
3. **Zombie** — child terminated but not reaped; visible in `ps` with STAT = `Z`.
4. **Orphan** — child outlives its parent; adopted by `init` (PPID = 1).
5. **Clean termination** — `wait()` reaps the child; `exit(42)` returns a verifiable status.

### 3.1.6 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Line Matters

| Line | Purpose | KTU Marker |
|---|---|---|
| `#include <sys/wait.h>` | Enables `wait`, `WIFEXITED`, `WEXITSTATUS` | Mandatory header |
| `pid_t pid = fork();` | Single call that creates the child | Core concept |
| `if (pid == 0) {...}` | **Child branch** — return-value check | **Most-missed** point in exam |
| `exit(42);` | C library exit; flushes stdio buffers | Normal termination |
| `wait(&status);` | Parent blocks; reaps child PCB | Prevents zombies |
| `WIFEXITED(status)` | Test for normal exit | Macro asked in viva |
| `sleep(N)` | Creates the observation window for `ps` | Practical lab trick |

## 3.2 Experiment 2(b) — Using `exec()` to Run a New Program in the Child

### 3.2.1 Aim

To use `fork()` + `exec()` together: the child becomes a brand-new program while the parent continues (or waits).

### 3.2.2 Program

```c
/* exp2b_fork_exec.c
 * KTU PCITL408 - Experiment 2(b)
 * Compile: gcc exp2b_fork_exec.c -o exp2b
 */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>

int main(void)
{
    pid_t pid = fork();

    if (pid < 0) { perror("fork"); exit(1); }

    if (pid == 0) {
        /* CHILD: replace image with `ls -l /tmp` */
        printf("[CHILD %d] About to exec: ls -l /tmp\n", getpid());
        execlp("ls", "ls", "-l", "/tmp", (char *)NULL);
        /* execlp returns ONLY on failure */
        perror("execlp failed");
        exit(127);
    }

    /* PARENT */
    printf("[PARENT %d] Waiting for child %d...\n", getpid(), pid);
    int s;
    wait(&s);
    if (WIFEXITED(s))
        printf("[PARENT] Child exited with %d\n", WEXITSTATUS(s));
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.2.3 Sample Output (Truncated)

```
[PARENT 5001] Waiting for child 5002...
[CHILD 5002] About to exec: ls -l /tmp
total 28
drwxrwxrwt 2 user user  4096 Apr  2 10:00 .X11-unix
drwxrwxrwt 2 user user  4096 Apr  2 10:00 .font-unix
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user     0 Apr  2 09:58 tmp.log
[PARENT] Child exited with 0
```

### 3.2.4 Key Insight

After a successful `execlp`, the child's **entire address space is replaced**. The string `"[CHILD] About to exec..."` is printed because `printf` runs **before** `execlp`. Any code after a successful `exec*` is unreachable; therefore the `exit(127)` line runs **only if `execlp` failed** (e.g., program not found).

## 3.3 Experiment 2(c) — Thread Creation, Termination & Synchronization

### 3.3.1 Aim

To create multiple POSIX threads that run a common routine with distinct arguments, demonstrate thread termination via `pthread_exit`, synchronize via `pthread_join`, and inspect thread IDs.

### 3.3.2 Algorithm

**Step 1.** Define a thread function `void *print_msg(void *arg)` that receives a pointer to a `struct msg { int id; char *text; }` and prints it.

**Step 2.** In `main`, declare an array of `pthread_t` handles and an array of argument structs.

**Step 3.** Loop `i = 0..N-1`, call `pthread_create` to spawn each thread. The threads may execute in any order — concurrency is observed.

**Step 4.** Loop `i = 0..N-1`, call `pthread_join` to wait for each thread and capture its return value.

**Step 5.** Print all return values, terminate `main` (which implicitly calls `pthread_exit` for the main thread).

### 3.3.3 Program

```c
/* exp2c_threads.c
 * KTU PCITL408 - Experiment 2(c)
 * Compile: gcc exp2c_threads.c -lpthread -o exp2c
 */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pthread.h>

#define N 3

struct thread_arg {
    int  id;
    char *message;
};

void *worker(void *arg)
{
    struct thread_arg *a = (struct thread_arg *)arg;

    /* Critical: detach-aware sleep so output order varies between runs */
    printf("[Thread %d | TID=%lu] Hello. Message: \"%s\".\n",
           a->id, (unsigned long)pthread_self(), a->message);

    /* Allocate a return value on the heap; must outlive this thread */
    char *ret = malloc(64);
    snprintf(ret, 64, "Thread %d done", a->id);
    pthread_exit((void *)ret);              /* Thread terminates here */
}

int main(void)
{
    pthread_t       tid[N];
    struct thread_arg args[N];
    void           *retval;

    /* ---------- CREATE ---------- */
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        args[i].id = i + 1;
        args[i].message = (i == 0) ? "First worker"
                       : (i == 1) ? "Second worker"
                                  : "Third worker";

        int rc = pthread_create(&tid[i], NULL, worker, &args[i]);
        if (rc != 0) {
            fprintf(stderr, "pthread_create failed: %d\n", rc);
            exit(1);
        }
        printf("[main] Created thread %d with TID=%lu\n",
               i + 1, (unsigned long)tid[i]);
    }

    /* ---------- JOIN ---------- */
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        pthread_join(tid[i], &retval);
        printf("[main] Thread %d returned: \"%s\"\n", i + 1, (char *)retval);
        free(retval);                      /* Avoid memory leak */
    }

    printf("[main] All threads joined. Exiting.\n");
    /* Implicit pthread_exit(0) for main thread */
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.3.4 Sample Output (Order May Vary)

```
[main] Created thread 1 with TID=140123456
[main] Created thread 2 with TID=140123457
[main] Created thread 3 with TID=140123458
[Thread 1 | TID=140123456] Hello. Message: "First worker".
[Thread 2 | TID=140123457] Hello. Message: "Second worker".
[Thread 3 | TID=140123458] Hello. Message: "Third worker".
[main] Thread 1 returned: "Thread 1 done"
[main] Thread 2 returned: "Thread 2 done"
[main] Thread 3 returned: "Thread 3 done"
[main] All threads joined. Exiting.
```

### 3.3.5 Analytical Matrix — What the Examiner Checks

| Concept | Demonstrated By |
|---|---|
| Thread creation | `pthread_create` loop |
| Passing arguments | `struct thread_arg` passed by **address** |
| Thread identification | `pthread_self()` inside the worker |
| Thread termination | `pthread_exit((void *)ret)` |
| Heap-allocated return value | `malloc` so memory survives thread death |
| Synchronization | `pthread_join` blocks main until thread finishes |
| Memory hygiene | `free(retval)` after consuming the return value |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common bug to avoid:** Returning the address of a **local (stack) variable** from `worker`. The stack frame is destroyed when `worker` returns, leaving `pthread_join` with a dangling pointer. Always `malloc` the return value.

## 3.4 Combined Demonstration — Process Tree Showing Full Life Cycle

Use the following **derivation** style explanation for viva questions:

> **"Trace the life cycle of each process in the master program."**

**Process A (parent, PID = 10234):**
- Enters state **New** at program start.
- Moves to **Ready** after the OS admits it to the run queue.
- Enters **Running** when the scheduler dispatches it.
- Enters **Blocked** during `sleep(5)` (waiting for timer event).
- Returns to **Ready** when timer fires.
- Enters **Running** again, executes `wait()`, then enters **Blocked** for the orphan demo.
- Enters **Terminated** after `exit(0)`.

**Process B (child, PID = 10235):**
- Enters **New** at `fork()`.
- Enters **Ready**; scheduled.
- Enters **Running**, executes `printf`, then enters **Blocked** during `sleep(2)`.
- Returns to **Ready**, then **Running**, calls `exit(42)`.
- Enters **Terminated**; sits as a **Zombie** for ~3 seconds until parent calls `wait()`.
- PCB is reaped and the entry is removed from the process table.

**Process C (orphan-grandchild, PID = 10236):**
- Enters **New** at `fork()` inside the dying parent.
- Enters **Ready**; scheduled.
- Enters **Running**, prints info, then **Blocked** during `sleep(4)`.
- While blocked, its parent (A) exits. The kernel reparents C to **init (PID 1)**.
- Returns to **Ready** then **Running**, observes `getppid() == 1`.
- Calls `exit(0)` and is reaped by `init`.

## 3.5 Derivations of the `fork()` Counting Trick (Viva Favourite)

> **Question:** "If you call `fork()` three times, how many processes result?"

**Derivation:**

$$N_{\text{procs}} = 2^{k}$$

where $k$ is the number of `fork()` calls and no `exec`/`exit` precedes subsequent forks.

**Step-by-step for $k = 3$:**

```
Initial:                    1 process
After fork() #1:            2 processes   (2^1)
After fork() #2:            4 processes   (2^2)
After fork() #3:            8 processes   (2^3)
```

So $N_{\text{procs}} = 2^3 = \mathbf{8}$.

**Total number of "Hello" lines printed if each process prints once:**

$$L = 2^{k} = 8$$

because every one of the 8 processes executes the `printf` exactly once.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process State Transition Diagram (5-State Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready : admit / long-term scheduler
    Ready --> Running : dispatch / short-term scheduler
    Running --> Ready : preempt / quantum-expire
    Running --> Blocked : I-O or wait-on-event
    Blocked --> Ready : event-completion / wakeup
    Running --> Terminated : exit() or signal
    Terminated --> [*] : reaped by parent via wait()
```

> **Exam tip:** Always label the **arrows with the triggering event** (admit, dispatch, preempt, I/O, wakeup, exit). The KTU board examiner gives partial marks for unlabelled arrows.

## 4.2 `fork()` Execution Flow — Program Counter Point of Divergence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent P0 begins execution] --> B[pid = fork()]
    B --> C{Return value of fork}
    C -- pid < 0 --> D[perror and exit FAILURE]
    C -- pid == 0 --> E[Child P1 branch]
    C -- pid > 0  --> F[Parent P0 branch]
    E --> E1[print child info]
    E1 --> E2[sleep / do child work]
    E2 --> E3[exit(status)]
    E3 --> E4[Child becomes ZOMBIE until parent reaps]
    F --> F1[print parent info]
    F1 --> F2[wait(&status) blocks]
    F2 --> F3{Child done?}
    F3 -- yes --> F4[WIFEXITED WEXITSTATUS]
    F4 --> F5[Parent exit(0)]
    E4 --> F3
    F5 --> G[Process removed from process table]
```

## 4.3 Thread Architecture — Process vs. Threads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC[Process Address Space]
        direction TB
        CODE[Shared: Code Section]
        DATA[Shared: Data, BSS, Heap]
        FD[Shared: Open File Descriptors]

        subgraph T1[Thread 1]
            TID1[TID 1]
            PC1[PC]
            REG1[Registers]
            STK1[Stack]
        end
        subgraph T2[Thread 2]
            TID2[TID 2]
            PC2[PC]
            REG2[Registers]
            STK2[Stack]
        end
        subgraph T3[Thread 3]
            TID3[TID 3]
            PC3[PC]
            REG3[Registers]
            STK3[Stack]
        end
    end

    T1 --- CODE
    T2 --- CODE
    T3 --- CODE
    T1 --- DATA
    T2 --- DATA
    T3 --- DATA
    T1 --- FD
    T2 --- FD
    T3 --- FD
```

## 4.4 POSIX Thread Life Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : pthread_create() succeeds
    Created --> Ready : kernel schedules the task
    Ready --> Running : CPU dispatched
    Running --> Ready : pre-empted or blocked
    Running --> Terminated : pthread_exit(val) / return from start_routine
    Running --> Cancelled : pthread_cancel() accepted
    Terminated --> Joined : pthread_join() collects retval
    Joined --> [*]
    Cancelled --> Joined
```

## 4.5 Zombie vs. Orphan — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ZOM[Zombie Scenario]
        P0a[Parent P0] -- still running, did not wait() --> Za[Child P1]
        Za -- terminated, PCB retained --> Zb[Defunct / State Z in ps]
    end

    subgraph ORP[Orphan Scenario]
        P0b[Parent P0] -- exits early --> Oa[Child P1]
        Oa -- reparented --> Ob[Adopted by init / PID 1]
        Ob -- init calls wait() --> Oc[Reaped normally]
    end
```

## 4.6 Combined `fork()` + `exec()` Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant P as Parent Process
    participant C as Child Process
    participant K as Kernel
    participant X as New Program (e.g. ls)

    U->>P: launch main()
    P->>K: fork()
    K-->>P: return child PID (>0)
    K-->>C: return 0
    par Parent path
        P->>P: wait() blocks
    and Child path
        C->>K: execve(ls, argv, envp)
        K->>X: load new image
        X-->>U: prints output
        X->>K: exit(0)
    end
    K-->>P: child reaped, wait() returns
    P->>K: exit(0)
```
