```c
    pthread_create(&prod, NULL, producer, NULL);
    pthread_create(&cons, NULL, consumer, NULL);

    pthread_join(prod, NULL);
    pthread_join(cons, NULL);

    sem_destroy(&mutex);
    sem_destroy(&empty);
    sem_destroy(&full);

    return 0;
}
```

### 3.2.1 Compilation and Execution

```bash
gcc producer_consumer.c -o pc -lpthread
./pc
```

## 3.3 Trace of `sem_wait` and `sem_post` Behavior

The POSIX semaphore operations behave as follows:

- `sem_wait(s)` — Atomically decrements the value of semaphore `s`. If the value becomes **negative**, the calling thread is **blocked** and added to `s`'s wait queue.
- `sem_post(s)` — Atomically increments the value of semaphore `s`. If there are threads blocked on `s`, **one** is unblocked and its `sem_wait` returns.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: What Happens at Boundary Conditions?

| Scenario | Before | `wait(empty)` | `wait(full)` | Effect |
|----------|--------|---------------|--------------|--------|
| Buffer **Empty**, Consumer calls | `empty=N, full=0` | — | `full` becomes $-1$ | **Consumer blocks** |
| Buffer **Full**, Producer calls | `empty=0, full=N` | `empty` becomes $-1$ | — | **Producer blocks** |
| Buffer **Half-Full** | `empty=N/2, full=N/2` | decrements by 1 | (consumer may) decrement by 1 | Both proceed normally |
| Producer + Consumer **race** | `mutex=1` | (independent) | (independent) | One waits for `mutex` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of the Bounded Buffer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Producer Process] -- item --> E1{empty 0}
    E1 -- yes --> M1{mutex 0}
    E1 -- no --> BP[Producer Blocked]
    M1 -- acquired --> CS1[Critical Section: write buffer in]
    CS1 --> SM1[signal mutex]
    SM1 --> SF1[signal full]
    SF1 --> P

    C[Consumer Process] -- request --> F1{full 0}
    F1 -- yes --> M2{mutex 0}
    F1 -- no --> BC[Consumer Blocked]
    M2 -- acquired --> CS2[Critical Section: read buffer out]
    CS2 --> SM2[signal mutex]
    SM2 --> SE2[signal empty]
    SE2 --> C

    CS1 <--> BUF[(Bounded Buffer N slots)]
    CS2 <--> BUF
```

## 4.2 Circular Buffer State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> PartiallyFilled: producer writes
    PartiallyFilled --> Full: producer fills last slot
    Full --> PartiallyFilled: consumer reads
    PartiallyFilled --> Empty: consumer drains last slot
    PartiallyFilled --> PartiallyFilled: producer write or consumer read
    Empty --> Empty: consumer blocks
    Full --> Full: producer blocks
```

## 4.3 Semaphore State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Init: empty N full 0 mutex 1] --> B{Producer Action}
    B --> C[wait empty -> empty 0]
    C --> D[wait mutex -> mutex 0]
    D --> E[Write to buffer in]
    E --> F[signal mutex -> mutex 1]
    F --> G[signal full -> full 1]
    G --> H{Consumer Action}
    H --> I[wait full -> full 0]
    I --> J[wait mutex -> mutex 0]
    J --> K[Read from buffer out]
    K --> L[signal mutex -> mutex 1]
    L --> M[signal empty -> empty 1]
    M --> B
```

## 4.4 Producer–Consumer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROD[Producer Thread]
        P1[Generate Data Item]
        P2[sem_wait and empty]
        P3[sem_wait and mutex]
        P4[buffer in equals item]
        P5[in equals in plus 1 mod N]
        P6[sem_post and mutex]
        P7[sem_post and full]
    end

    subgraph SHARED[Shared Resources]
        B[(Circular Buffer N)]
        SM[sem_t mutex equals 1]
        SE[sem_t empty equals N]
        SF[sem_t full equals 0]
    end

    subgraph CONS[Consumer Thread]
        C1[sem_wait and full]
        C2[sem_wait and mutex]
        C3[item equals buffer out]
        C4[out equals out plus 1 mod N]
        C5[sem_post and mutex]
        C6[sem_post and empty]
        C7[Process Consumed Item]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7
    P7 --> B
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6 --> C7
    C3 --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a semaphore. Differentiate between binary and counting semaphores. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **semaphore** is a synchronization variable (integer) accessed only through two atomic operations: `wait()` (P) and `signal()` (V). It was proposed by **Dijkstra** to solve the critical section problem.

| Feature | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Value range | {0, 1} | Any non-negative integer |
| Use | Mutual exclusion | Resource counting |
| Initial value | 1 | N (number of resources) |
| Example | `mutex` | `empty`, `full` |

### Q2. State the producer–consumer problem and list the three semaphores used to solve it. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Producer–Consumer (Bounded-Buffer) Problem** requires synchronization between processes that produce and consume data items stored in a fixed-size shared buffer, ensuring that the producer does not overflow and the consumer does not underflow.

The three semaphores are:
1. **`mutex`** (initial = 1) — ensures mutual exclusion on the buffer.
2. **`empty`** (initial = N) — counts the number of empty buffer slots.
3. **`full`** (initial = 0) — counts the number of filled buffer slots.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1)

**Q3. (a)** With neat pseudocode, explain how the **producer–consumer problem** is solved using semaphores. Mention the role of each semaphore. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The problem is solved using three semaphores initialized as:

$$\text{mutex} = 1, \quad \text{empty} = N, \quad \text{full} = 0$$

**Producer Pseudocode:**

```
PRODUCER:
  while (true) do
      wait(empty)            // block if buffer is full
      wait(mutex)            // enter critical section
      buffer[in] = item      // place item
      in = (in + 1) mod N    // advance write index
      signal(mutex)          // leave critical section
      signal(full)           // increment filled count
  end while
```

**Consumer Pseudocode:**

```
CONSUMER:
  while (true) do
      wait(full)             // block if buffer is empty
      wait(mutex)            // enter critical section
      item = buffer[out]     // remove item
      out = (out + 1) mod N  // advance read index
      signal(mutex)          // leave critical section
      signal(empty)          // increment empty count
  end while
```

**Roles:**
- `mutex` — guarantees that only one process manipulates the buffer at a time (mutual exclusion).
- `empty` — ensures the producer waits when the buffer is full.
- `full` — ensures the consumer waits when the buffer is empty.

---

**Q3. (b)** Consider a bounded buffer of size **N = 4**. Initially the buffer is empty. Producer produces 3 items, then consumer consumes 1 item, then producer produces 2 more items. Show the **state of all three semaphores and the buffer** after each step. Verify the conservation law. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Initial state: $\text{empty}=4, \text{full}=0, \text{mutex}=1$, buffer = `[_, _, _, _]`, in=0, out=0.

**Step 1 — Producer produces item 1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{wait(empty)} &: \text{empty}=3 \\
\text{wait(mutex)} &: \text{mutex}=0 \\
\text{buffer[0]} &= 1; \quad \text{in}=1 \\
\text{signal(mutex)} &: \text{mutex}=1 \\
\text{signal(full)} &: \text{full}=1
\end{aligned}$$

State: empty=3, full=1, buffer=[1,_,_,_].

**Step 2 — Producer produces item 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{empty}=2,\ \text{full}=2,\ \text{mutex}=1 \\
\text{buffer}=[1,2,_,_],\ \text{in}=2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Producer produces item 3:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{empty}=1,\ \text{full}=3,\ \text{mutex}=1 \\
\text{buffer}=[1,2,3,_],\ \text{in}=3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Consumer consumes 1 item:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{wait(full)} &: \text{full}=2 \\
\text{wait(mutex)} &: \text{mutex}=0 \\
\text{item}=\text{buffer[0]}=1;\ \text{out}=1 \\
\text{signal(mutex)} &: \text{mutex}=1 \\
\text{signal(empty)} &: \text{empty}=2
\end{aligned}$$

State: empty=2, full=2, buffer=[_,2,3,_], out=1.

**Step 5 — Producer produces item 4:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{empty}=1,\ \text{full}=3 \\
\text{buffer}=[_,2,3,4],\ \text{in}=4 \bmod 4 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Producer produces item 5:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{empty}=0,\ \text{full}=4 \\
\text{buffer}=[5,2,3,4],\ \text{in}=1
\end{aligned}$$

**Final State:** empty=0, full=4, mutex=1, buffer=[5,2,3,4], in=1, out=1.

**Conservation Law Verification (every step):**

$$\text{empty} + \text{full} = 4 = N \quad \checkmark$$

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q4. (a)** Explain the **wait() and signal() operations** of a semaphore. Show their implementation using **test-and-set** to guarantee atomicity. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **wait()** operation decrements the semaphore value; if the value becomes negative, the calling process is **blocked**.

The **signal()** operation increments the semaphore value; if any process is blocked on this semaphore, **one** is woken up.

**Implementation using test-and-set (atomic hardware instruction):**

```
wait(S):
   while (test_and_set(&lock) == 1)   // spin until lock acquired
       ;                               // busy wait
   S = S - 1;                         // critical modification
   lock = 0;                          // release hardware lock
   if (S < 0)
       block(process);                // add to semaphore queue

signal(S):
   while (test_and_set(&lock) == 1)
       ;
   S = S + 1;
   lock = 0;
   if (S <= 0)
       wakeup(process from S.queue);
```

The `test_and_set` instruction executes **atomically** — it reads and writes a memory location in a single, uninterruptible step, ensuring no race condition can occur on the semaphore variable.

---

**Q4. (b)** What happens if the **producer and consumer interchange the order of `wait()` and `wait(mutex)`** calls? Demonstrate with a scenario. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

If the producer reverses the order — i.e., calls `wait(mutex)` first, then `wait(empty)` — a **deadlock** can occur in the following scenario:

**Deadlock Scenario (Buffer is Full, N=5):**

1. Producer P calls `wait(mutex)` → mutex = 0 (acquired).
2. Producer P then calls `wait(empty)` → `empty` was 0, so `empty` becomes **−1**, and **P blocks** (still holding mutex).
3. Consumer C tries to call `wait(full)` → succeeds, full = N−1.
4. Consumer C then calls `wait(mutex)` → mutex = 0, so C **blocks** waiting for mutex.
5. Now P is waiting for `empty` (which can only be signaled by a consumer), and C is waiting for `mutex` (which is held by P). **Classic circular wait → deadlock!**

**Circular wait condition holds:**
- P holds: `mutex`; waits for: `empty`.
- C holds: (none); waits for: `mutex`.

**Solution:** Always call resource-count semaphores (`empty` / `full`) **before** `mutex`. This enforces an **ordered lock acquisition** that prevents hold-and-wait cycles, satisfying the **deadlock prevention** condition of circular-wait elimination.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting circular buffer wrap-around:** Writing `in = in + 1` instead of `in = (in + 1) % N`. This causes index-out-of-bounds errors. (−1 to −2 marks)
> 2. **Swapping the order of `wait()` calls:** Causes deadlock, as analyzed in Q4(b). (−2 to −3 marks)
> 3. **Not initializing `mutex = 1`:** Leads to immediate blocking. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing initial values:** Students often set `empty = 0` and `full = N`. Remember: at the start, the buffer is **empty**, so `empty = N` and `full = 0`. (−1 to −2 marks)
> 5. **Missing `sleep()` calls:** Without producer/consumer delays, output appears correct only by chance. Examiners may deduct marks if no synchronization stress is visible. (−1 mark)
> 6. **Using `sem_t` but forgetting `-lpthread` flag** in compilation. (Lab record issue, not theory)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **Producer–Consumer Problem** is also called the **Bounded-Buffer Problem**.
- ✅ Three semaphores are used: **`mutex` = 1**, **`empty` = N**, **`full` = 0**.
- ✅ **Conservation Law:** `empty + full = N` (always true).
- ✅ **Producer:** waits on `empty`, signals `full`. **Consumer:** waits on `full`, signals `empty`.
- ✅ **Critical ordering rule:** `wait(empty)` / `wait(full)` **must** be called **before** `wait(mutex)`.
- ✅ Buffer indices use **circular arithmetic:** `in = (in + 1) % N`, `out = (out + 1) % N`.
- ✅ `wait()` = `P()` = `down()` = `decrement()`. `signal()` = `V()` = `up()` = `increment()`.
- ✅ **Atomicity** is achieved using hardware instructions like `test-and-set` or by disabling interrupts in the kernel.
- ✅ **Binary semaphore (mutex)** value ∈ {0, 1}. **Counting semaphore** value ∈ [0, N].
- ✅ **Deadlock** occurs if `wait(mutex)` is taken before `wait(empty/full)`.
- ✅ **POSIX functions:** `sem_init()`, `sem_wait()`, `sem_post()`, `sem_destroy()`.
- ✅ **Compile with:** `gcc filename.c -o output -lpthread`.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** pipes, message queues, thread pools, Kafka, log buffers, socket I/O.
- ✅ The experiment builds the foundation for the **Readers–Writers** and **Dining Philosophers** problems.
- ✅ **Block** state vs **Wakeup** state are managed by the OS scheduler, not by user code.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of dining philosophers' problem using threads and semaphores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of dining philosophers' problem using threads and semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dining Philosophers Problem (DPP)** is a classic **synchronization and concurrency problem** formulated by **Edsger W. Dijkstra (1965)**. It is used to illustrate the challenges of **resource contention, mutual exclusion, deadlock, and starvation** in a multi-threaded operating system environment.

In the problem, **N philosophers** sit around a circular table. Between every pair of adjacent philosophers lies exactly **one chopstick (fork)**. Each philosopher alternates between three states:

$$S_{philosopher} \in \{\text{THINKING},\ \text{HUNGRY},\ \text{EATING}\}$$

A philosopher can **eat** only when he/she holds **both** the left and right chopsticks. The challenge is to design a concurrent algorithm that allows maximum parallelism while **guaranteeing no deadlock, no starvation, and mutual exclusion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITL408 - Module 2):** Students must implement DPP using **POSIX threads (`pthread.h`)** and **POSIX semaphores (`semaphore.h`)** in C. The program must demonstrate proper `pthread_create()`, `sem_init()`, `sem_wait()`, and `sem_post()` usage on a **Linux environment** (typically Ubuntu/Fedora).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **5 friends** sitting around a round dining table. Between every two friends is **exactly one chopstick** (so 5 chopsticks total). Each friend thinks for a while, gets hungry, and to eat noodles needs **2 chopsticks** — the one on their left and the one on their right.

- If everyone grabs their left chopstick at the same time → **deadlock** (nobody can grab the right one).
- A philosopher who never gets a turn → **starvation**.
- Two adjacent philosophers eating at the same time → fine; sharing a chopstick → forbidden.

The OS scheduler must coordinate them so the **dinner proceeds forever** without anyone starving.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Thread** | Lightweight process; the basic unit scheduled by the OS kernel. |
| **Semaphore** | Integer variable accessed only via atomic `wait` and `signal` operations. |
| **Mutex (Binary Semaphore)** | A semaphore with value restricted to **0 or 1**, used for mutual exclusion. |
| **Deadlock** | A state where two or more processes wait indefinitely for an event only one of them can cause. |
| **Starvation** | A process waits forever because other processes monopolize the resource. |
| **Critical Section** | Code segment accessing shared resources that must be executed atomically. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical Conditions for Deadlock (Coffman Conditions)

For a deadlock to occur, **all four** of these must hold simultaneously:

| # | Condition | Meaning in DPP Context |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Mutual Exclusion** | A chopstick can be held by only one philosopher at a time. |
| 2 | **Hold and Wait** | A philosopher holds one chopstick while waiting for the other. |
| 3 | **No Preemption** | A chopstick cannot be forcibly taken from a philosopher. |
| 4 | **Circular Wait** | A cycle exists: P1 waits for P2's fork, P2 waits for P3's, …, P5 waits for P1's. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy to Break Deadlock in DPP:** Our solution breaks **Circular Wait** by ensuring a philosopher picks up both forks *only* when both are free (atomic `test()` function under a mutex).

## 2.2 State Diagram of a Philosopher

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THINKING} &\xrightarrow{\text{get hungry}} \text{HUNGRY} \\
\text{HUNGRY} &\xrightarrow{\text{both forks free}} \text{EATING} \\
\text{EATING} &\xrightarrow{\text{release forks}} \text{THINKING}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 Semaphore Design

We require the following semaphore infrastructure:

| Semaphore | Count | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| `mutex` | **1** | Binary semaphore that protects the shared `state[]` array (critical section guard). |
| `S[0..N-1]` | **0** (each) | One semaphore per philosopher; the philosopher blocks on `S[i]` until notified it can eat. |

The global `state[i]` array holds the current state of every philosopher $P_i$:

$$
state[i] =
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } P_i \text{ is THINKING} \\
1 & \text{if } P_i \text{ is HUNGRY} \\
2 & \text{if } P_i \text{ is EATING}
\end{cases}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Left neighbour of $P_i$ | $LEFT = (i + 4) \mod N$ | Used to check if neighbour is EATING. |
| Right neighbour of $P_i$ | $RIGHT = (i + 1) \mod N$ | Used to check if neighbour is EATING. |
| Condition to eat | $state[i] = HUNGRY \ \land\ state[LEFT] \neq EATING \ \land\ state[RIGHT] \neq EATING$ | The `test(i)` predicate. |
| Number of resources | $N$ (equal to number of philosophers) | Avoids resource over-allocation. |
| Total semaphores | $N + 1$ | One mutex + N philosopher semaphores. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** DPP models problems like **database lock managers, distributed mutual exclusion (Ricart-Agrawala), router packet scheduling, and multi-threaded transaction processing** in banking systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm Logic (Step-by-Step)

The complete C program for KTU Lab Examination is built in **6 structured steps**:

### Step 1: Header Files and Macros

We include the necessary POSIX headers and define the constants and macros for neighbour calculation.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <pthread.h>
#include <semaphore.h>
#include <unistd.h>

#define N 5
#define THINKING 0
#define HUNGRY   1
#define EATING   2

#define LEFT  (i + 4) % N
#define RIGHT (i + 1) % N
```

**Logic Explanation:**
- `N = 5` philosophers, since DPP at KTU standardly uses **5**.
- The `LEFT` and `RIGHT` macros compute neighbours **circularly** using modulo arithmetic.
- For $i=0$: LEFT = $(0+4)\% 5 = 4$, RIGHT = $(0+1)\% 5 = 1$. ✓

### Step 2: Global Declarations

```c
sem_t mutex;        // Protects the state[] array
sem_t S[N];         // One semaphore per philosopher

int state[N];       // Shared state array
int phil[N] = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4};  // Philosopher IDs
```

**Logic Explanation:**
- `mutex` is a **binary semaphore** initialised to 1 (acts as a lock).
- `S[i]` is initialised to 0 — philosopher $P_i$ starts blocked until `sem_post(&S[i])` is called on its behalf.

### Step 3: The `main()` Function — Thread and Semaphore Initialization

```c
int main(void) {
    int i;
    pthread_t tid[N];

    // Initialize the mutex (shared between threads of the same process: pshared = 0)
    sem_init(&mutex, 0, 1);

    // Initialize each philosopher's semaphore to 0
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
        sem_init(&S[i], 0, 0);

    // Create one thread per philosopher
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
        pthread_create(&tid[i], NULL, philosopher, &phil[i]);

    // Wait for all threads (in this simulation they run forever, so join is illustrative)
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
        pthread_join(tid[i], NULL);

    return 0;
}
```

**Logic Explanation:**
- `sem_init(&mutex, 0, 1)` → second argument `pshaged = 0` means it is **shared between threads of the same process**; value initialised to **1** (unlocked).
- `pthread_create()` spawns each philosopher in its own thread of execution.

### Step 4: The Philosopher Thread — Infinite Loop

```c
void *philosopher(void *arg) {
    int i = *(int *)arg;   // Recover philosopher's index

    while (1) {
        think(i);
        take_forks(i);
        eat(i);
        put_forks(i);
    }
    return NULL;
}

void think(int i) {
    printf("Philosopher %d is THINKING\n", i + 1);
    sleep(1);
}

void eat(int i) {
    printf("Philosopher %d is EATING\n", i + 1);
    sleep(2);
}
```

**Logic Explanation:** Each philosopher cycles through **THINK → HUNGRY → EAT** indefinitely, simulating the classical problem.

### Step 5: The `take_forks()` Function — Acquiring Resources Safely

```c
void take_forks(int i) {
    sem_wait(&mutex);        // ENTER critical section
    state[i] = HUNGRY;
    printf("Philosopher %d is HUNGRY\n", i + 1);
    test(i);                 // Try to acquire both forks
    sem_post(&mutex);        // EXIT critical section
    sem_wait(&S[i]);         // Block if forks not granted
}
```

**Logic Explanation:**
- The `state[]` array is **shared**; we protect all reads/writes with `mutex`.
- `test(i)` is called *inside* the critical section — if both forks are free, it sets `state[i] = EATING` and calls `sem_post(&S[i])`.
- After releasing the mutex, the philosopher calls `sem_wait(&S[i])`. If `S[i]` was already posted (forks granted), the wait returns immediately; otherwise, the philosopher **blocks** until a neighbour releases forks and posts to `S[i]`.

### Step 6: The `put_forks()` and `test()` Functions — Release and Neighbour Notification

```c
void put_forks(int i) {
    sem_wait(&mutex);
    state[i] = THINKING;
    printf("Philosopher %d put down forks\n", i + 1);
    test(LEFT);              // Left neighbour may now be able to eat
    test(RIGHT);             // Right neighbour may now be able to eat
    sem_post(&mutex);
}

void test(int i) {
    if (state[i] == HUNGRY &&
        state[LEFT]  != EATING &&
        state[RIGHT] != EATING) {

        state[i] = EATING;
        printf("Philosopher %d starts EATING\n", i + 1);
        sem_post(&S[i]);     // Wake up philosopher i
    } else {
        printf("Philosopher %d cannot eat yet (waiting)\n", i + 1);
    }
}
```

**Logic Explanation:**
- The `LEFT` and `RIGHT` macros use `i` (the implicit global) — note: the C standard requires macros to reference the variable `i` defined in the calling function's scope.
- After putting down forks, the philosopher **wakes up its hungry neighbours** by calling `test(LEFT)` and `test(RIGHT)`. If a neighbour is HUNGRY and its *other* neighbour is not EATING, that neighbour is granted permission to eat.

## 3.2 Complete Compilation-Ready Source Code

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <pthread.h>
#include <semaphore.h>
#include <unistd.h>

#define N 5
#define THINKING 0
#define HUNGRY   1
#define EATING   2

#define LEFT  (i + 4) % N
#define RIGHT (i + 1) % N

sem_t mutex;
sem_t S[N];
int state[N];
int phil[N] = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4};

void *philosopher(void *);
void test(int);
void take_forks(int);
void put_forks(int);

int main(void) {
    int i;
    pthread_t tid[N];
    sem_init(&mutex, 0, 1);
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++) sem_init(&S[i], 0, 0);
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++) pthread_create(&tid[i], NULL, philosopher, &phil[i]);
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++) pthread_join(tid[i], NULL);
    return 0;
}

void *philosopher(void *arg) {
    int i = *(int *)arg;
    while (1) { think(i); take_forks(i); eat(i); put_forks(i); }
    return NULL;
}

void think(int i) { printf("P%d THINKING\n", i+1); sleep(1); }
void eat(int i)   { printf("P%d EATING\n",   i+1); sleep(2); }

void take_forks(int i) {
    sem_wait(&mutex);
    state[i] = HUNGRY;
    printf("P%d HUNGRY\n", i+1);
    test(i);
    sem_post(&mutex);
    sem_wait(&S[i]);
}

void put_forks(int i) {
    sem_wait(&mutex);
    state[i] = THINKING;
    printf("P%d put down forks\n", i+1);
    test((i + 4) % N);
    test((i + 1) % N);
    sem_post(&mutex);
}

void test(int i) {
    if (state[i] == HUNGRY &&
        state[(i + 4) % N] != EATING &&
        state[(i + 1) % N] != EATING) {
        state[i] = EATING;
        printf("P%d starts EATING\n", i+1);
        sem_post(&S[i]);
    }
}
```

## 3.3 Compilation and Execution

```bash
gcc -o dining dining_philosophers.c -lpthread
./dining
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `-lpthread` flag links the **POSIX threads library** (required on Linux/gcc). Without it, `pthread_create()` and `sem_init()` will produce *undefined reference* linker errors.

## 3.4 Analytical Trace Table (Sample Run)

| Step | Action | $state[0..4]$ | Output |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | P0 HUNGRY, test(0) | $(1,0,0,0,0)$ → $P_4$ not EATING, $P_1$ not EATING | P0 starts EATING |
| 2 | P1 HUNGRY, test(1) | $(2,1,0,0,0)$ → $P_0$ EATING → blocked on $S[1]$ | P1 cannot eat yet |
| 3 | P0 releases forks, calls test(4), test(1) | $P_1$ & $P_4$ tested | P1 starts EATING |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circular Resource Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0((P0)) ---|fork0| F0{{Chopstick 0}}
    F0 --- P1((P1))
    P1 ---|fork1| F1{{Chopstick 1}}
    F1 --- P2((P2))
    P2 ---|fork2| F2{{Chopstick 2}}
    F2 --- P3((P3))
    P3 ---|fork3| F3{{Chopstick 3}}
    F3 --- P4((P4))
    P4 ---|fork4| F4{{Chopstick 4}}
    F4 --- P0
```

## 4.2 Philosopher State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> THINKING
    THINKING --> HUNGRY: get_hungry
    HUNGRY --> EATING: test_ok_AND_neighbors_not_EATING
    EATING --> THINKING: release_forks
    HUNGRY --> HUNGRY: test_fail_blocks_on_Si
```

## 4.3 Algorithm Flowchart (Detailed Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: pthread_create x5]) --> B[Initialize mutex = 1]
    B --> C[Initialize S 0 to 4 = 0]
    C --> D[Philosopher thread spawned]

    D --> E[think i]
    E --> F{Hungry?}

    F --> G[sem_wait mutex]
    G --> H[state i = HUNGRY]
    H --> I[test i]
    I --> J{Both forks free?}
    J -->|Yes| K[state i = EATING, sem_post S i]
    J -->|No| L[remain HUNGRY]

    K --> M[sem_post mutex]
    L --> M
    M --> N[sem_wait S i - block until granted]
    N --> O[eat i]
    O --> P[put_forks i]
    P --> Q[sem_wait mutex]
    Q --> R[state i = THINKING]
    R --> S[test LEFT and test RIGHT]
    S --> T[sem_post mutex]
    T --> E
```

## 4.4 Synchronization Mechanism (Mutex + Per-Process Semaphores)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Shared Resources"
        ST["state 0..4 array"]
        M["mutex = 1"]
    end

    subgraph "Per-Process Blockers"
        S0["S 0 = 0"]
        S1["S 1 = 0"]
        S2["S 2 = 0"]
        S3["S 3 = 0"]
        S4["S 4 = 0"]
    end

    P0(["Philosopher 0"]) --> M
    P1(["Philosopher 1"]) --> M
    P2(["Philosopher 2"]) --> M
    P3(["Philosopher 3"]) --> M
    P4(["Philosopher 4"]) --> M

    P0 -. blocks on .-> S0
    P1 -. blocks on .-> S1
    P2 -. blocks on .-> S2
    P3 -. blocks on .-> S3
    P4 -. blocks on .-> S4

    M --> ST
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Dining Philosophers Problem and list the three states a philosopher can be in.**

**Model Answer:**
The Dining Philosophers Problem is a classical synchronization problem posed by Edsger Dijkstra. It involves **N philosophers** sitting around a circular table with **N chopsticks**, one between each adjacent pair. Each philosopher requires **two chopsticks** (left and right) to eat.

The three states a philosopher can be in are:
1. **THINKING** — the philosopher is not using any chopstick.
2. **HUNGRY** — the philosopher is trying to acquire both chopsticks.
3. **EATING** — the philosopher has acquired both chopsticks and is eating.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for states)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a counting semaphore. Which one is used as `mutex` in the Dining Philosophers solution?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Value range | $0$ or $1$ | Any non-negative integer |
| Use case | Mutual exclusion | Resource counting (e.g., pool of $k$ resources) |
| Operations | `wait` / `signal` | `wait` / `signal` |

In the DPP solution, the `mutex` variable is a **binary semaphore** initialised to **1** because it is used purely for **mutual exclusion** while modifying the shared `state[]` array. The array `S[0..N-1]` uses **counting semaphores** initialised to **0**, since they act as per-philosopher wake-up signals.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the Dining Philosophers Problem. Discuss the conditions that lead to **deadlock** in the naive solution where each philosopher picks up the left fork first. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete C program using **POSIX threads and semaphores** to implement a deadlock-free solution. Explain the role of the `test()` function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Dining Philosophers Problem models **resource contention** in a concurrent system. In the naive solution, every philosopher follows the same algorithm:

1. Pick up the **left** chopstick.
2. Pick up the **right** chopstick.
3. Eat.
4. Put down both chopsticks.

**Deadlock occurs** when all five philosophers simultaneously pick up their left chopstick. At that moment:

$$
\forall i \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}: \text{right chopstick of } P_i = \text{left chopstick of } P_{(i+1)\%N}
$$

So every right chopstick is held by the neighbour. All four **Coffman conditions** are simultaneously satisfied:
- **Mutual Exclusion** — chopsticks are non-shareable.
- **Hold and Wait** — every $P_i$ holds its left chopstick and waits for the right.
- **No Preemption** — once held, a chopstick cannot be taken by force.
- **Circular Wait** — the wait graph forms a cycle: $P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow P_0$.

The system halts forever — a classic **circular deadlock**.

*(7 marks: 2 for problem statement, 3 for algorithm trace, 2 for Coffman conditions)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

Refer to the **complete source code** provided in **Section 3.2** above. The key elements of the answer are:

| Component | Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| `pthread_create()` | Spawns one thread per philosopher. |
| `sem_init(&mutex, 0, 1)` | Binary semaphore; protects `state[]` from race conditions. |
| `sem_init(&S[i], 0, 0)` | One counting semaphore per philosopher; blocks it until forks are granted. |
| `test(i)` | Atomically checks if $P_i$ and both neighbours satisfy the eating condition; grants forks via `sem_post(&S[i])`. |
| `take_forks()` | Sets state to HUNGRY, calls `test()`, releases mutex, then blocks on `S[i]`. |
| `put_forks()` | Sets state to THINKING, then calls `test(LEFT)` and `test(RIGHT)` to wake neighbours. |

**Role of `test()`:** It is the **decision-making core** of the algorithm. It checks the precondition (philosopher HUNGRY + neither neighbour EATING) and atomically grants the chopsticks by calling `sem_post(&S[i])`. It is the only function that transitions a philosopher from HUNGRY to EATING.

*(7 marks: 3 for code, 2 for test() explanation, 2 for correctness reasoning)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock (Coffman conditions)? Show how each manifests in the Dining Philosophers problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **two alternative solutions** to the Dining Philosophers Problem that avoid deadlock. Compare their advantages. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| # | Coffman Condition | Manifestation in DPP |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Mutual Exclusion** | A chopstick is held by only one philosopher at a time. |
| 2 | **Hold and Wait** | A philosopher who has acquired the left chopstick continues to hold it while waiting for the right. |
| 3 | **No Preemption** | A chopstick cannot be forcibly removed from a philosopher mid-meal. |
| 4 | **Circular Wait** | The wait-for graph forms a directed cycle around the table: $P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0$. |

To break deadlock, our solution **removes the Circular Wait** by ensuring a philosopher picks up chopsticks only when both are free (the `test()` function under the `mutex`).

*(7 marks: 1 per condition + 3 for explanation)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Solution 1 — Resource Hierarchy (Asymmetric Ordering):**

Number the chopsticks $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. Each philosopher always picks up the **lower-numbered** chopstick first, then the higher-numbered one. This breaks the circular wait.

**Solution 2 — Arbitrator / Semaphore-based (the KTU standard):**

Introduce a global `mutex` and per-philosopher semaphores. A philosopher attempts to eat by calling `test()`, which only succeeds if **both** neighbours are not eating. This is the implementation covered in Section 3.2.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Resource Hierarchy | Semaphore-based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Complexity | Low | Medium |
| Max parallelism | High (4 of 5 can eat) | High |
| Code size | Small | Moderate |
| KTU preference | Mentioned in theory | **Required for lab exam** |

*(7 marks: 3 + 3 for solutions, 1 for comparison)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `-lpthread`** during compilation — results in *undefined reference to `pthread_create`* linker error. Examiners will deduct marks if the program is not compilable.
> 2. **Forgetting `sem_post(&mutex)`** before `sem_wait(&S[i])` — leads to **deadlock** in practice. Always release the mutex *before* blocking.
> 3. **Using `&S[i]`** as an integer instead of passing the address — `sem_wait` and `sem_post` expect pointers to `sem_t`, not values.
> 4. **Confusing `state[]` with the chopstick** — chopsticks are *implicit*; only the `state` array and `S[]` semaphores exist in code.
> 5. **Calling `test(i)` outside the critical section** — this causes race conditions on the shared `state[]` array.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PCITL408 Module 2:**

- **DPP is posed by Edsger Dijkstra (1965)** to illustrate synchronization problems.
- **Number of philosophers = Number of chopsticks** = **N** (typically 5).
- **Three states per philosopher:** THINKING (0), HUNGRY (1), EATING (2).
- **Two semaphores required:** `mutex` (binary, value = 1) and `S[N]` (counting, all initialised to 0).
- **Four Coffman conditions** for deadlock: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
- **The `test(i)` function** is the heart of the solution — it atomically grants the chopsticks if neighbours are not eating.
- **Compile command:** `gcc -o dining dining.c -lpthread` — the `-lpthread` flag is **mandatory**.
- **Required header files:** `<pthread.h>`, `<semaphore.h>`, `<stdio.h>`, `<unistd.h>`.
- **Library functions used:** `pthread_create()`, `pthread_join()`, `sem_init()`, `sem_wait()`, `sem_post()`.
- **Critical section protection:** Always wrap any access to `state[]` between `sem_wait(&mutex)` and `sem_post(&mutex)`.
- **Neighbour formulas:** `LEFT = (i + 4) % N` and `RIGHT = (i + 1) % N`.
- **The solution breaks Circular Wait**, thereby preventing deadlock without violating mutual exclusion.
- **No two adjacent philosophers can eat simultaneously** — guaranteed by the `test()` check.
- **Starvation** is theoretically possible in some edge cases; a fully starvation-free variant requires additional mechanisms (e.g., a queue of waiting philosophers).
- **Sample viva questions:** *"What happens if two neighbours call `put_forks()` simultaneously?"* → mutex serialises them; only one enters the critical section at a time.
- **Practical analogy:** Real-world examples include **database row-locks**, **printer spooling**, and **distributed transaction systems**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of banker's algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of banker's algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Implementation of Banker's Algorithm

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Banker's Algorithm** is a classical **deadlock avoidance algorithm** proposed by **Edsger Dijkstra** in 1965. It is a resource allocation and deadlock avoidance algorithm designed to test for safety by simulating the allocation of predetermined maximum possible resources and making a safe-state check for all pending activities before deciding whether allocation should proceed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *The Banker's Algorithm is a deadlock avoidance technique that models a banking system where a bank never allocates its available cash in such a way that it can no longer satisfy the needs of all its customers. In operating systems, processes act as customers and resources act as the bank's capital.*

### Core Terminology (KTU Mandatory)
- **Safe State**: A state in which there exists **at least one safe sequence** of process execution where all processes can complete without deadlock.
- **Safe Sequence**: An ordering of processes $\langle P_1, P_2, P_3, \dots, P_n \rangle$ such that for each $P_i$, the resources that $P_i$ can still request can be satisfied by the currently available resources plus the resources held by all $P_j$ where $j < i$.
- **Resource Allocation Graph**: Directed graph modeling allocation and request edges (used in single-instance resource systems).
- **Deadlock**: A state where two or more processes are permanently blocked, each waiting for resources held by the other.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Banking System
Imagine a bank with a fixed amount of cash. Multiple customers approach the bank requesting loans. Each customer declares a **maximum credit limit** upfront.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Banker**
> - The **Banker** = The Operating System (Resource Manager)
> - **Customers** = Processes requesting resources
> - **Cash** = System resources (RAM, I/O devices, file handles)
> - **Maximum Credit Declared** = Maximum resource requirement (`Max`)
> - **Money Already Borrowed** = Currently allocated resources (`Allocation`)
> - **Still Needed (Credit Limit − Borrowed)** = Remaining need (`Need`)
>
> The banker (OS) must ensure that **at any point, after granting a loan, the bank still has enough cash to satisfy the maximum needs of all remaining customers in some order**. This guarantees the bank is never stuck — just like a deadlock-free OS.

The algorithm gets its name from this exact banking analogy where the banker distributes cash to customers such that the bank is always in a **"safe state."**

### Why Banker's Algorithm is Critical for KTU
| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Deadlock Prevention** | Avoids circular wait by validating each request before allocation |
| **Conservative Nature** | May deny resource requests even when deadlock wouldn't actually occur |
| **Pre-declaration** | Requires every process to declare its **maximum** resource requirement in advance |
| **Fixed Resource Count** | Works best with a **fixed number of resources** like memory pages or tape drives |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Data Structures Used

The Banker's Algorithm maintains **four critical data structures** for a system with $n$ processes and $m$ resource types:

### Structure 1: `Available` Vector
A **1-D vector of length $m$** indicating the number of available instances of each resource type.

$$ \text{Available}[j] = k \quad \text{means } k \text{ instances of resource type } R_j \text{ are available} $$

### Structure 2: `Max` Matrix
An $n \times m$ matrix defining the **maximum demand** of each process. `Max[i][j]` is the maximum number of instances of resource $R_j$ that process $P_i$ may request.

### Structure 3: `Allocation` Matrix
An $n \times m$ matrix defining the number of resources **currently allocated** to each process. `Allocation[i][j]` is the number of instances of $R_j$ currently held by $P_i$.

### Structure 4: `Need` Matrix
An $n \times m$ matrix indicating the **remaining resource need** of each process.

$$ \text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j] $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight**
> The relationship `Max = Allocation + Need` is the **golden equation** of Banker's Algorithm. Examiners frequently test this single-line derivation for 2–3 marks.

## 2.2 Algorithm 1: The Safety Algorithm

The Safety Algorithm determines whether the system is currently in a **safe state** by finding at least one safe sequence.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Initialize two working vectors:
   - `Work` ← `Available` (a copy of available resources)
   - `Finish[i]` ← `false` for all $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, n-1\}$

2. Find an index $i$ such that both conditions hold:
   - `Finish[i] == false`
   - `Need[i] ≤ Work` (component-wise)

3. If such $i$ is found:
   - Simulate $P_i$ executing to completion:
     $$ \text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[i] $$
   - Set `Finish[i] ← true`
   - **Go to Step 2**

4. If no such $i$ exists:
   - If `Finish[i] == true` for **all** $i$, the system is in a **safe state**.
   - Otherwise, the system is in an **unsafe state** (deadlock may occur).

> [!NOTE]
> The safety algorithm runs in $O(m \cdot n^2)$ time in the worst case because we may scan all $n$ processes for each of the $n$ processes in the safe sequence.

## 2.3 Algorithm 2: The Resource Request Algorithm

When process $P_i$ requests resources, the OS performs an immediate eligibility test:

### Step-by-Step Logic
Let `Request[i]` be the request vector from process $P_i$.

**Step 1**: If `Request[i] ≤ Need[i]` (component-wise), proceed to Step 2. Otherwise, **error** (process exceeded its declared maximum).

**Step 2**: If `Request[i] ≤ Available`, proceed to Step 3. Otherwise, $P_i$ must **wait** (resources not available).

**Step 3**: Pretend to allocate the requested resources:
$$ \text{Available} = \text{Available} - \text{Request}[i] $$
$$ \text{Allocation}[i] = \text{Allocation}[i] + \text{Request}[i] $$
$$ \text{Need}[i] = \text{Need}[i] - \text{Request}[i] $$

**Step 4**: Run the **Safety Algorithm** on the resulting state.
- If **safe** → grant the resources permanently to $P_i$.
- If **unsafe** → **roll back** the pretended allocation; $P_i$ must wait.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Notation | Mathematical Form | Meaning | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Need Matrix** | $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$ | Remaining resource need of $P_i$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Pretend Allocation (Resources)** | $\text{Available}_{\text{new}} = \text{Available}_{\text{old}} - \text{Request}[i]$ | Free resources after allocation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Pretend Allocation (Given)** | $\text{Allocation}_{\text{new}}[i] = \text{Allocation}_{\text{old}}[i] + \text{Request}[i]$ | Resources given to $P_i$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Resource Release** | $\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[i]$ | Resources returned after $P_i$ finishes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Component-wise Comparison** | $\text{Need}[i] \leq \text{Work} \iff \forall j, \text{Need}[i][j] \leq \text{Work}[j]$ | Eligibility test condition | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Safe State Definition** | $\exists \text{ sequence } \langle P_{\sigma(1)}, \dots, P_{\sigma(n)} \rangle$ such that each $P_{\sigma(k)}$ completes with current `Work` | At least one safe ordering exists | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Unsafe State Definition** | $\nexists$ safe sequence | Deadlock is **possible** (not guaranteed) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Total Resources Constraint** | $\sum_{i} \text{Allocation}[i][j] + \text{Available}[j] = \text{Total}[j]$ | Conservation of resources | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(m \cdot n^2)$ | Safety algorithm worst case | ⭐⭐ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(m \cdot n)$ | Storage for matrices | ⭐⭐ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Database Lock Managers**: Oracle and PostgreSQL use variations of Banker's-style algorithms to manage concurrent transactions.
- **Embedded Systems**: Real-time OS (RTOS) for avionics uses Banker's to guarantee no deadlock in flight control software.
- **Cloud Resource Schedulers**: Kubernetes-like schedulers apply avoidance principles for CPU and memory allocation.
- **Mainframe OS**: IBM z/OS uses a form of Banker's for managing tape drives and channel allocations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Worked Example (KTU Board Standard)

Consider a system with **5 processes** ($P_0$ to $P_4$) and **3 resource types**:
- $A$ (10 instances)
- $B$ (5 instances)
- $C$ (7 instances)

### Given Snapshot at Time $T_0$

**Allocation Matrix** (currently held):

| Process | A | B | C |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $P_1$ | 2 | 0 | 0 |
| $P_2$ | 3 | 0 | 2 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| $P_4$ | 0 | 0 | 2 |

**Max Matrix** (maximum demand):

| Process | A | B | C |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | 7 | 5 | 3 |
| $P_1$ | 3 | 2 | 2 |
| $P_2$ | 9 | 0 | 2 |
| $P_3$ | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| $P_4$ | 4 | 3 | 3 |

### Step 1: Calculate Available Vector

$$ \text{Total} = (10, 5, 7) $$
$$ \text{Allocated sum} = (0+2+3+2+0, \; 1+0+0+1+0, \; 0+0+2+1+2) = (7, 2, 5) $$

$$ \text{Available} = \text{Total} - \text{Allocated sum} $$

$$ \text{Available} = (10 - 7, \; 5 - 2, \; 7 - 5) = (3, 3, 2) $$

### Step 2: Derive Need Matrix (Using $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$)

For $P_0$: $\text{Need}[0] = (7-0, 5-1, 3-0) = (7, 4, 3)$
For $P_1$: $\text{Need}[1] = (3-2, 2-0, 2-0) = (1, 2, 2)$
For $P_2$: $\text{Need}[2] = (9-3, 0-0, 2-2) = (6, 0, 0)$
For $P_3$: $\text{Need}[3] = (2-2, 2-1, 2-1) = (0, 1, 1)$
For $P_4$: $\text{Need}[4] = (4-0, 3-0, 3-2) = (4, 3, 1)$

**Final Need Matrix**:

| Process | A | B | C |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | 7 | 4 | 3 |
| $P_1$ | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| $P_2$ | 6 | 0 | 0 |
| $P_3$ | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $P_4$ | 4 | 3 | 1 |

### Step 3: Execute Safety Algorithm

Initialize: $\text{Work} = (3, 3, 2)$ and $\text{Finish} = \{\text{false}, \text{false}, \text{false}, \text{false}, \text{false}\}$

**Iteration 1**: Search for process $i$ where `Finish[i] = false` AND `Need[i] ≤ Work`.

Check $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \leq (3,3,2)$? → $7 > 3$ ❌
Check $P_1$: $(1,2,2) \leq (3,3,2)$? → $1 \leq 3, 2 \leq 3, 2 \leq 2$ ✅

$P_1$ executes to completion:

$$ \text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[1] $$
$$ \text{Work} = (3,3,2) + (2,0,0) = (5,3,2) $$

Set $\text{Finish}[1] = \text{true}$. Safe sequence: $\langle P_1 \rangle$

**Iteration 2**: $\text{Work} = (5, 3, 2)$

Check $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \leq (5,3,2)$? → $7 > 5$ ❌
Check $P_2$: $(6,0,0) \leq (5,3,2)$? → $6 > 5$ ❌
Check $P_3$: $(0,1,1) \leq (5,3,2)$? → $0 \leq 5, 1 \leq 3, 1 \leq 2$ ✅

$P_3$ executes to completion:

$$ \text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[3] = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,4,3) $$

Set $\text{Finish}[3] = \text{true}$. Safe sequence: $\langle P_1, P_3 \rangle$

**Iteration 3**: $\text{Work} = (7, 4, 3)$

Check $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \leq (7,4,3)$? → All equal ✅

$P_0$ executes to completion:

$$ \text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}[0] = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7,5,3) $$

Set $\text{Finish}[0] = \text{true}$. Safe sequence: $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0 \rangle$

**Iteration 4**: $\text{Work} = (7, 5, 3)$

Check $P_2$: $(6,0,0) \leq (7,5,3)$? ✅

$P_2$ executes:

$$ \text{Work} = (7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10,5,5) $$

Safe sequence: $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2 \rangle$

**Iteration 5**: $\text{Work} = (10, 5, 5)$

Check $P_4$: $(4,3,1) \leq (10,5,5)$? ✅

$$ \text{Work} = (10,5,5) + (0,0,2) = (10,5,7) $$

### Step 4: Final Result

$$\boxed{\text{Safe Sequence} = \langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle}$$

All `Finish[i] = true` → System is in a **SAFE STATE** ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note**: Safe sequences are **not unique**. An alternative valid sequence could be $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_4, P_2 \rangle$. Any one valid safe sequence is sufficient to declare the system safe.

## 3.2 Resource Request Worked Example

Now suppose $P_1$ requests $\text{Request}[1] = (1, 0, 2)$.

### Step 1: Validate Request ≤ Need
$$ (1, 0, 2) \leq \text{Need}[1] = (1, 2, 2)? \;\; \checkmark $$

### Step 2: Validate Request ≤ Available
$$ (1, 0, 2) \leq \text{Available} = (3, 3, 2)? \;\; \checkmark $$

### Step 3: Pretend to Allocate
$$ \text{Available} = (3,3,2) - (1,0,2) = (2, 3, 0) $$
$$ \text{Allocation}[1] = (2,0,0) + (1,0,2) = (3, 0, 2) $$
$$ \text{Need}[1] = (1,2,2) - (1,0,2) = (0, 2, 0) $$

### Step 4: Run Safety Algorithm

Updated Need Matrix:

| Process | A | B | C |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | 7 | 4 | 3 |
| $P_1$ | 0 | 2 | 0 |
| $P_2$ | 6 | 0 | 0 |
| $P_3$ | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $P_4$ | 4 | 3 | 1 |

$\text{Work} = (2, 3, 0)$

Check $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \leq (2,3,0)$? ❌
Check $P_1$: $(0,2,0) \leq (2,3,0)$? ✅
$$ \text{Work} = (2,3,0) + (3,0,2) = (5, 3, 2) $$

Check $P_2$: $(6,0,0) \leq (5,3,2)$? ❌
Check $P_3$: $(0,1,1) \leq (5,3,2)$? ✅
$$ \text{Work} = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7, 4, 3) $$

Check $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \leq (7,4,3)$? ✅
$$ \text{Work} = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7, 5, 3) $$

Check $P_2$: $(6,0,0) \leq (7,5,3)$? ✅
$$ \text{Work} = (7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10, 5, 5) $$

Check $P_4$: $(4,3,1) \leq (10,5,5)$? ✅

$$\boxed{\text{Safe Sequence} = \langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Request GRANTED}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Safety Algorithm Flowchart (Detailed Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START Safety Algorithm]) --> initWork["Work := Available vector"]
    initWork --> initFinish["Finish array := false for all n processes"]
    initFinish --> findProc{"Find process Pi such that Finish i = false AND Need i <= Work"}
    findProc -- "Such i found" --> executeProc["Pretend Pi executes and completes"]
    executeProc --> releaseRes["Work := Work + Allocation of Pi"]
    releaseRes --> setTrue["Finish i := true"]
    setTrue --> appendSeq["Append Pi to Safe Sequence"]
    appendSeq --> findProc
    findProc -- "No such i found" --> checkAll{"Is Finish i = true for ALL processes?"}
    checkAll -- "YES" --> safeState["Output SAFE STATE and print Safe Sequence"]
    checkAll -- "NO" --> unsafeState["Output UNSAFE STATE Deadlock possible"]
    safeState --> endA([END])
    unsafeState --> endA

    style startA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style endA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style safeState fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style unsafeState fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style findProc fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    style checkAll fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Resource Request Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([START Resource Request from Pi]) --> recvReq["Receive Request vector from Pi"]
    recvReq --> checkNeed{"Is Request i less than or equal to Need i component wise?"}
    checkNeed -- "NO" --> errMax["ERROR: Pi exceeded its declared maximum"]
    checkNeed -- "YES" --> checkAvail{"Is Request i less than or equal to Available?"}
    checkAvail -- "NO" --> waitProc["Pi must WAIT resources not free"]
    checkAvail -- "YES" --> pretend["Pretend Allocation: Available minus Request; Allocation plus Request; Need minus Request"]
    pretend --> runSafe["Run SAFETY ALGORITHM on new state"]
    runSafe --> checkResult{"Is resulting state SAFE?"}
    checkResult -- "YES" --> grantRes["GRANT resources to Pi permanently"]
    checkResult -- "NO" --> rollback["ROLL BACK pretended allocation Pi must WAIT"]
    grantRes --> endB([END])
    rollback --> endB
    waitProc --> endB
    errMax --> endB

    style startB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style endB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style grantRes fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style rollback fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style waitProc fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style errMax fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style checkNeed fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    style checkAvail fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    style checkResult fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Process Lifecycle in Banker's Algorithm (State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Requesting : Pi requests resources
    Requesting --> Waiting : Resources not available
    Waiting --> Requesting : Resources become free
    Requesting --> Validating : Request received by OS
    Validating --> ErrorState : Request exceeds Need
    ErrorState --> [*] : Process terminated
    Validating --> Pretending : Request within limits
    Pretending --> UnsafeRollback : Safety check fails
    UnsafeRollback --> Waiting : Pi waits
    Pretending --> Running : Safety check passes
    Running --> Completed : Pi finishes execution
    Completed --> Releasing : Release all allocated resources
    Releasing --> [*] : Process terminated

    note right of Validating : Two tests: Request vs Need and Request vs Available
    note left of Pretending : Modify Available Allocation and Need matrices
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide**
> - **Green nodes** = success/safe transitions
> - **Red nodes** = failure/unsafe/error transitions
> - **Yellow/Amber nodes** = decision diamonds requiring component-wise vector comparisons
> - **Beige nodes** = internal computational state

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define the Banker's Algorithm. Explain any two data structures used by it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Banker's Algorithm** is a deadlock avoidance algorithm proposed by Edsger Dijkstra. It ensures that the system never enters an unsafe state by simulating resource allocation before granting requests.

**Two Key Data Structures:**

1. **Max Matrix** ($n \times m$): Defines the maximum number of resource instances each process may request. `Max[i][j] = k` means process $P_i$ may request at most $k$ instances of resource type $R_j$.

2. **Allocation Matrix** ($n \times m$): Defines the number of resource instances currently allocated to each process. `Allocation[i][j] = k` means $P_i$ is currently holding $k$ instances of $R_j$.

3. *(Optional third for extra credit)* **Need Matrix** is computed as `Need[i][j] = Max[i][j] - Allocation[i][j]` representing the remaining resource need of each process.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Safe State and Unsafe State with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Safe State | Unsafe State |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A state where at least one safe sequence of process execution exists | A state where **no** safe sequence exists |
| **Deadlock Possibility** | Deadlock is **impossible** | Deadlock is **possible** |
| **Resources** | Can satisfy max needs of all processes in some order | May not be able to satisfy all processes |
| **Example** | If Available $(3,3,2)$ and all processes can be ordered to complete | If all processes simultaneously need resources held by others |

**Example**: In the previous worked example, system is in a safe state because sequence $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle$ allows all processes to complete. If we change allocation such that no ordering works, the state becomes unsafe.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Consider a system with **5 processes** $P_0$ to $P_4$ and **3 resource types** $A$, $B$, $C$ with total resources $(10, 5, 7)$. The current state is as follows:

| Process | Allocation (A B C) | Max (A B C) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | 0 1 0 | 7 5 3 |
| $P_1$ | 2 0 0 | 3 2 2 |
| $P_2$ | 3 0 2 | 9 0 2 |
| $P_3$ | 2 1 1 | 2 2 2 |
| $P_4$ | 0 0 2 | 4 3 3 |

(i) Calculate the **Need Matrix**. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Determine if the system is in a **safe state** using Banker's Algorithm. If yes, provide the safe sequence. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (i) – Need Matrix Calculation (3 marks):**

Apply formula $\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]$ for all 15 entries.

| Process | Need (A B C) |
|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | (7-0, 5-1, 3-0) = **(7, 4, 3)** |
| $P_1$ | (3-2, 2-0, 2-0) = **(1, 2, 2)** |
| $P_2$ | (9-3, 0-0, 2-2) = **(6, 0, 0)** |
| $P_3$ | (2-2, 2-1, 2-1) = **(0, 1, 1)** |
| $P_4$ | (4-0, 3-0, 3-2) = **(4, 3, 1)** |

**Part (ii) – Safety Algorithm Execution (7 marks):**

Step 1: Calculate Available
$$\text{Available} = (10,5,7) - (7,2,5) = (3,3,2)$$

Step 2: $\text{Work} = (3,3,2)$

**Iteration 1**: $P_1$: Need $(1,2,2) \leq$ Work $(3,3,2)$ ✅
$$\text{Work} = (3,3,2) + (2,0,0) = (5,3,2)$$

**Iteration 2**: $P_3$: Need $(0,1,1) \leq$ Work $(5,3,2)$ ✅
$$\text{Work} = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,4,3)$$

**Iteration 3**: $P_0$: Need $(7,4,3) \leq$ Work $(7,4,3)$ ✅
$$\text{Work} = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7,5,3)$$

**Iteration 4**: $P_2$: Need $(6,0,0) \leq$ Work $(7,5,3)$ ✅
$$\text{Work} = (7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10,5,5)$$

**Iteration 5**: $P_4$: Need $(4,3,1) \leq$ Work $(10,5,5)$ ✅
$$\text{Work} = (10,5,5) + (0,0,2) = (10,5,7)$$

**Result**: All `Finish[i] = true` → **SAFE STATE**
$$\boxed{\text{Safe Sequence} = \langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle}$$

**(b)** If process $P_4$ requests **(0, 3, 0)**, can the request be immediately granted using the Resource Request Algorithm? Show all steps. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1**: Check `Request[4] ≤ Need[4]`
$$\text{Request}[4] = (0,3,0) \leq \text{Need}[4] = (4,3,1) \;\; \checkmark \text{ OK}$$

**Step 2**: Check `Request[4] ≤ Available`
$$\text{Request}[4] = (0,3,0) \leq \text{Available} = (3,3,2) \;\; \checkmark \text{ OK}$$

**Step 3**: Pretend allocation:
$$\text{Available}_{\text{new}} = (3,3,2) - (0,3,0) = (3,0,2)$$
$$\text{Allocation}[4]_{\text{new}} = (0,0,2) + (0,3,0) = (0,3,2)$$
$$\text{Need}[4]_{\text{new}} = (4,3,1) - (0,3,0) = (4,0,1)$$

**Step 4**: Run safety algorithm with $\text{Work} = (3,0,2)$:
- $P_1$: $(1,2,2) \leq (3,0,2)$? → $2 > 0$ ❌
- $P_3$: $(0,1,1) \leq (3,0,2)$? → $1 > 0$ ❌
- $P_4$: $(4,0,1) \leq (3,0,2)$? → $4 > 3$ ❌
- $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \leq (3,0,2)$? ❌
- $P_2$: $(6,0,0) \leq (3,0,2)$? → $6 > 3$ ❌

**Result**: No process can proceed → **UNSAFE STATE** → Request is **DENIED** ❌. $P_4$ must **wait**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Resource Request Algorithm** with its complete procedural steps. When is a request denied by the OS? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

When process $P_i$ makes a request `Request[i]`, the OS performs these steps:

**Step 1: Verify against declared maximum**
If $\text{Request}[i] \leq \text{Need}[i]$, proceed. Else, raise an **error** (process declared wrong maximum).

**Step 2: Verify against available resources**
If $\text{Request}[i] \leq \text{Available}$, proceed. Else, $P_i$ must **wait**.

**Step 3: Pretend to allocate**
$$\text{Available} \leftarrow \text{Available} - \text{Request}[i]$$
$$\text{Allocation}[i] \leftarrow \text{Allocation}[i] + \text{Request}[i]$$
$$\text{Need}[i] \leftarrow \text{Need}[i] - \text{Request}[i]$$

**Step 4: Safety check**
Run the Safety Algorithm. If safe → grant; else → **roll back** and $P_i$ must wait.

**When request is denied:**
A request is **denied** (or $P_i$ is made to wait) in **two cases**:
1. `Request[i] > Available` (resources not currently free)
2. Pretend allocation results in an **unsafe state** (potential deadlock detected)

**(b)** A system has 4 processes $P_0$–$P_3$ and 3 resource types $A$, $B$, $C$ with totals $(6, 7, 8)$. The Max and Allocation matrices are:

| Process | Max (A B C) | Allocation (A B C) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | 5 4 6 | 1 1 2 |
| $P_1$ | 3 2 1 | 2 1 0 |
| $P_2$ | 4 3 4 | 2 2 1 |
| $P_3$ | 2 1 2 | 0 1 1 |

Compute the Need matrix and Available vector. Is the system in a safe state? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Need Matrix**: Apply `Need = Max - Allocation`

| Process | Need (A B C) |
|:---:|:---:|
| $P_0$ | (4, 3, 4) |
| $P_1$ | (1, 1, 1) |
| $P_2$ | (2, 1, 3) |
| $P_3$ | (2, 0, 1) |

**Available Vector**:
$$\text{Allocated sum} = (1+2+2+0, \; 1+1+2+1, \; 2+0+1+1) = (5, 5, 4)$$
$$\text{Available} = (6,7,8) - (5,5,4) = (1, 2, 4)$$

**Safety Algorithm**: $\text{Work} = (1, 2, 4)$

- $P_0$: $(4,3,4) \leq (1,2,4)$? ❌
- $P_1$: $(1,1,1) \leq (1,2,4)$? ✅ → $\text{Work} = (1,2,4) + (2,1,0) = (3,3,4)$
- $P_2$: $(2,1,3) \leq (3,3,4)$? ✅ → $\text{Work} = (3,3,4) + (2,2,1) = (5,5,5)$
- $P_3$: $(2,0,1) \leq (5,5,5)$? ✅ → $\text{Work} = (5,5,5) + (0,1,1) = (5,6,6)$
- $P_0$: $(4,3,4) \leq (5,6,6)$? ✅ → $\text{Work} = (5,6,6) + (1,1,2) = (6,7,8)$

$$\boxed{\text{Safe Sequence} = \langle P_1, P_2, P_3, P_0 \rangle \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{SAFE STATE}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to update Available after process completion**: Many students execute the safety algorithm but forget to add `Allocation[i]` to `Work` after each process. **You lose 2 marks per iteration.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing Need with Allocation**: When asked for "remaining need," students often write the current `Allocation` instead of `Max - Allocation`. **Verification equation**: `Allocation + Need = Max` for every process.
> 
> 3. **Component-wise vs Element-wise comparison**: The comparison `Need[i] ≤ Work` must be done **component-wise** (all $m$ components). A single component exceeding causes failure. **Partial marks only if you state this clearly.**
> 
> 4. **Wrong Available calculation**: Available = Total − Sum of all Allocations (row-wise sum), NOT the diagonal of Max. **Show the row-wise sum explicitly.**
> 
> 5. **Unsafe ≠ Deadlock**: An unsafe state means deadlock is **possible**, not **guaranteed**. Examiners test this distinction for 2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Missing the Rollback step in Resource Request**: When safety check fails after pretend allocation, you **must explicitly state "rollback and P_i waits"**, otherwise you lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Banker's Algorithm is a **deadlock avoidance** technique (NOT prevention or detection)
- ✅ Designed by **Edsger Dijkstra** in 1965
- ✅ Requires processes to **declare maximum resource need upfront**
- ✅ System must maintain a **safe state** at all times

### The Three Golden Matrices
- ✅ **Max**: Maximum demand per process
- ✅ **Allocation**: Currently held resources
- ✅ **Need** = Max − Allocation (always)

### Safety Algorithm Key Points
- ✅ Initialize `Work = Available` and `Finish[i] = false`
- ✅ Find process where `Need[i] ≤ Work`
- ✅ Add its `Allocation` to `Work` and mark finished
- ✅ If all `Finish[i] = true` → SAFE; else UNSAFE

### Resource Request Algorithm
- ✅ Two validation checks: `Request ≤ Need` AND `Request ≤ Available`
- ✅ Always run Safety Algorithm after pretended allocation
- ✅ Rollback if unsafe (do NOT commit allocation)

### Common Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ `Need = Max − Allocation`
- ✅ `Available = Total − Σ(Allocation[i])` for each resource type
- ✅ `Work_{new} = Work_{old} + Allocation[i]` when process finishes
- ✅ Component-wise comparison: $\forall j, \text{Need}[i][j] \leq \text{Work}[j]$

### Exam Strategy Tips
- ✅ Always show the **Need matrix calculation first** (3 marks guaranteed)
- ✅ Then calculate **Available vector** with explicit row sums
- ✅ Maintain a **Work column** in your safety algorithm table for clarity
- ✅ State the **safe sequence** at the end in boxed notation: $\langle P_x, P_y, P_z \rangle$
- ✅ For unsafe state, just state "No process can complete → UNSAFE"

### Limitations to Note (Theory Questions)
- ✅ Requires **fixed number of processes** (no dynamic creation)
- ✅ Requires processes to declare **maximum need in advance** (often unrealistic)
- ✅ Assumes **discrete resource allocation** (not preemptive)
- ✅ Time complexity $O(m \cdot n^2)$ can be slow for large systems

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement memory management schemes (first fit, best fit and worst fit)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement memory management schemes (first fit, best fit and worst fit) ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Management (Contiguous Allocation)** is the mechanism by which the Operating System allocates a single, continuous region of physical memory (RAM) to each process. The OS maintains a list of **free memory blocks (holes)** and uses an allocation strategy to satisfy process requests. The three classic **contiguous memory allocation strategies** evaluated in this experiment are: | Strategy | Allocation Rule | |---|---| | **First Fit** | Allocate the *first* free block whose size is **greater than or equal to** the requested size. | | **Best Fit** | Allocate the *smallest* free block whose size is sufficient to satisfy the request. | | **Worst Fit** | Allocate the *largest* free block available, leaving a large remainder. | Formally, given a request of size `R` and a set of free holes `H = {h₁, h₂, h₃, ...}`: $$ \text{Chosen Block} = \begin{cases} \min\{i : h_i \geq R\} & \text{(First Fit)} \\ \arg\min_{h_i \geq R} (h_i - R) & \text{(Best Fit)} \\ \arg\max_{i} (h_i) \text{ such that } h_i \geq R & \text{(Worst Fit)} \\ \text{NULL} & \text{if no block satisfies } h_i \geq R \end{cases} $$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Room Manager" > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy (Hotel Check-in):** > Imagine you are a hotel receptionist with rooms of varying sizes: 100, 300, 250, 500, and 150 sq.ft. A guest arrives needing 200 sq.ft. > > - **First Fit:** Give them the *first* room (100) that fits? No, too small. The next is 300 — give it! You don't care about leftovers. > - **Best Fit:** Scan all rooms. The smallest one that fits 200 is 250 — perfect, minimal wastage. > - **Worst Fit:** Give them the *biggest* room (500), so the leftover space (300) is still large enough for future big guests. This hotel scenario is exactly how the OS places process blocks into RAM holes — and just like the hotel, **fragmentation** (wasted space) is the critical trade-off. ### 1.3 Fragmentation — The Two Enemies > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Types of Fragmentation:** > - **Internal Fragmentation:** Memory allocated to a process but *not actually used* by it (wasted inside the allocated block). Occurs when allocated block > requested size. Measured in **bytes**. > - **External Fragmentation:** Total free memory is large enough to satisfy a request, but it is scattered as *non-contiguous small holes*, so allocation fails. Measured as a **percentage**. $$ \text{External Fragmentation} (\%) = \frac{\text{Sum of all free holes smaller than request}}{\text{Total free memory}} \times 100 $$ --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic Steps #### 🟢 A. First Fit Algorithm 1. Traverse the free-block list from the **beginning** (or from the last allocated position). 2. For each hole `h_i`, check if `h_i ≥ Request`. 3. The **first** such hole is selected. 4. Split it: allocate `Request` bytes, and create a new smaller hole of size `(h_i − Request)` if the remainder is > 0. 5. If no hole satisfies the condition, allocation fails. **Why it is fast:** Time complexity is **O(n)** in the worst case, but on average it is the fastest because scanning stops at the first match. #### 🔵 B. Best Fit Algorithm 1. Traverse the **entire** free-block list. 2. Track the hole with the **minimum wastage** `(h_i − Request)` where `h_i ≥ Request`. 3. Allocate from that optimal hole. 4. Worst-case time complexity is **O(n)** — *always* scans the full list. **Why it minimizes wastage (in theory):** Leaves the smallest possible leftover. However, it produces *many tiny unusable holes* — increasing **external fragmentation** paradoxically. #### 🔴 C. Worst Fit Algorithm 1. Traverse the **entire** free-block list. 2. Select the **largest** hole available. 3. Allocate, leaving a remainder that is itself still large. 4. Worst-case time complexity is **O(n)**. **Why it is used:** Tries to keep the remaining holes large enough for future requests, reducing small-hole accumulation. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use this table as the **last-page reference** during revision and for solving numerical problems in Part B. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wasted space (Best Fit) | $W_{bf} = h_{selected} - R$ | bytes | | 2 | Wasted space (Worst Fit) | $W_{wf} = h_{max} - R$ | bytes | | 3 | Internal Fragmentation (per block) | $IF_i = \text{Block}_i - \text{Process}_i$ | bytes | | 4 | Total Internal Fragmentation | $IF_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IF_i$ | bytes | | 5 | External Fragmentation | $EF = \dfrac{\sum \text{small free holes}}{\text{Total free memory}} \times 100$ | % | | 6 | Memory Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\sum \text{Allocated}}{\text{Total RAM}} \times 100$ | % | | 7 | Time Complexity (all three) | $O(n)$ | where `n` = number of free holes | | 8 | Request success condition | $h_{selected} \geq R$ | boolean | > [!WARNING] > **Mark-loss Pitfall:** KTU examiners **deduct marks** if students confuse the *Worst Fit* selected block (largest hole) with the *Best Fit* selected block (smallest fitting hole). Memorize the keyword: **Best = Smallest, Worst = Largest.** ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compilers & Linkers:** Use Best Fit to pack code segments efficiently in embedded firmware. - **Database Buffer Pools:** Use First Fit for low-latency memory allocation. - **Cloud Virtualization (KVM, VMware):** Use Best Fit variants to pack virtual machines into physical hosts, maximizing density. - **Heap Allocators (`malloc`):** The C library `glibc` uses a combination of strategies (bins of size classes — a hybrid of Best Fit and segregated lists). --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example (All Three Strategies) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement (Classic KTU-style):** > Consider a system with **5 free memory holes** of sizes (in KB): > `H = {100, 500, 200, 300, 600}` > Process requests arrive in order: > `P1 = 212 KB, P2 = 417 KB, P3 = 112 KB, P4 = 426 KB` We will trace **all three strategies** step by step. #### 🟢 FIRST FIT Trace | Step | Request | Holes before | Scanned Blocks | Allocated Hole | Hole Remaining | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | P1 = 212 | `{100, 500, 200, 300, 600}` | 100→fail, 500→**ok** | 500 | 500−212 = **288** | 1st success wins | | 2 | P2 = 417 | `{100, 288, 200, 300, 600}` | 100→fail, 288→fail, 200→fail, 300→fail, 600→**ok** | 600 | 600−417 = **183** | | | 3 | P3 = 112 | `{100, 288, 200, 300, 183}` | 100→fail, 288→**ok** | 288 | 288−112 = **176** | | | 4 | P4 = 426 | `{100, 176, 200, 300, 183}` | 100→fail, 176→fail, 200→fail, 300→fail, 183→fail | **FAIL** | — | No block ≥ 426 | **Final First Fit holes:** `{100, 176, 200, 300, 183}` → Total free = **959 KB**, but **P4 cannot be allocated** (external fragmentation). #### 🔵 BEST FIT Trace | Step | Request | Holes before | Considered blocks (≥ Req) | Selected (min) | Hole Remaining | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | P1 = 212 | `{100, 500, 200, 300, 600}` | 500, 300, 600 | **300** (smallest ≥ 212) | 300−212 = **88** | | 2 | P2 = 417 | `{100, 500, 200, 88, 600}` | 500, 600 | **500** (smallest ≥ 417) | 500−417 = **83** | | 3 | P3 = 112 | `{100, 83, 200, 88, 600}` | 200, 600 | **200** (smallest ≥ 112) | 200−112 = **88** | | 4 | P4 = 426 | `{100, 83, 88, 88, 600}` | 600 only | **600** | 600−426 = **174** | **Final Best Fit holes:** `{100, 83, 88, 88, 174}` → Total free = **533 KB**, with many tiny unusable holes. #### 🔴 WORST FIT Trace | Step | Request | Holes before | Selected (max) | Hole Remaining | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | P1 = 212 | `{100, 500, 200, 300, 600}` | **600** | 600−212 = **388** | | 2 | P2 = 417 | `{100, 500, 200, 300, 388}` | **500** (largest) | 500−417 = **83** | | 3 | P3 = 112 | `{100, 83, 200, 300, 388}` | **388** | 388−112 = **276** | | 4 | P4 = 426 | `{100, 83, 200, 300, 276}` | **300** (largest ≥ 426 fails) | **FAIL** | **Final Worst Fit holes:** `{100, 83, 200, 300, 276}` → P4 fails because no hole ≥ 426 remains. ### 3.2 Comparative Analysis Matrix > [!NOTE] > This table is a **favorite KTU question** — examiners ask: *"Compare First, Best, and Worst Fit."* | Property | First Fit | Best Fit | Worst Fit | |---|---|---|---| | **Selection rule** | First hole big enough | Smallest fitting hole | Largest hole | | **Scan time** | May stop early (fastest) | Always full scan | Always full scan | | **Speed** | ⭐⭐⭐ Fastest | ⭐⭐ Medium | ⭐ Slowest | | **Internal Fragmentation** | Moderate | ⭐ Lowest (least waste) | Highest | | **External Fragmentation** | Moderate | ⭐ Highest (tiny holes) | Lowest | | **Memory Utilization** | Good (60–70% typical) | Variable | Best for big processes | | **Algorithm complexity** | $O(n)$ avg, faster | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | | **Practical usage** | Most OS (Linux buddy hybrid) | Compilers, packers | Rarely used alone | | **Result on P4 (426 KB)** | FAIL | ✅ SUCCESS | FAIL | ### 3.3 Reference C Program Skeleton (Lab Exam Expectation) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU OS Lab examiners expect students to **write a menu-driven C program** implementing all three strategies. The structure below is the **gold-standard template** taught in KTU 2024 scheme labs. ```c #include #define MAX 25 void firstFit(int blockSize[], int m, int processSize[], int n) { int allocation[n], i, j; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) allocation[i] = -1; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { for (j = 0; j < m; j++) { if (blockSize[j] >= processSize[i]) { allocation[i] = j; blockSize[j] -= processSize[i]; break; // KEY: break makes it FIRST FIT } } } printf("\n--- First Fit Allocation ---"); printf("\nProcess No.\tProcess Size\tBlock No."); for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { printf("\n%d\t\t%d\t\t%s", i + 1, processSize[i], allocation[i] != -1 ? " (unused fallback)" : "Not Allocated"); } } void bestFit(int blockSize[], int m, int processSize[], int n) { int allocation[n], i, j, bestIdx; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) allocation[i] = -1; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { bestIdx = -1; for (j = 0; j < m; j++) { if (blockSize[j] >= processSize[i]) { if (bestIdx == -1 || blockSize[j] < blockSize[bestIdx]) bestIdx = j; // KEY: pick SMALLEST fitting } } if (bestIdx != -1) { allocation[i] = bestIdx; blockSize[bestIdx] -= processSize[i]; } } // ... print allocation table } void worstFit(int blockSize[], int m, int processSize[], int n) { int allocation[n], i, j, worstIdx; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) allocation[i] = -1; for (i = 0; i < n; i++) { worstIdx = -1; for (j = 0; j < m; j++) { if (blockSize[j] >= processSize[i]) { if (worstIdx == -1 || blockSize[j] > blockSize[worstIdx]) worstIdx = j; // KEY: pick LARGEST fitting } } if (worstIdx != -1) { allocation[i] = worstIdx; blockSize[worstIdx] -= processSize[i]; } } // ... print allocation table } int main() { int blockSize[] = {100, 500, 200, 300, 600}; int processSize[] = {212, 417, 112, 426}; int m = 5, n = 4, choice; int b1[5] = {100,500,200,300,600}; int b2[5] = {100,500,200,300,600}; int b3[5] = {100,500,200,300,600}; printf("1. First Fit 2. Best Fit 3. Worst Fit\nChoice: "); scanf("%d", &choice); switch (choice) { case 1: firstFit(b1, m, processSize, n); break; case 2: bestFit(b2, m, processSize, n); break; case 3: worstFit(b3, m, processSize, n); break; } return 0; } ``` **Three One-Line Differences to Memorize:** | Strategy | Inner Loop Termination | Selection Logic | |---|---|---| | First Fit | `break;` on first match | Stop scanning | | Best Fit | `blockSize[j] < blockSize[bestIdx]` | Minimize remainder | | Worst Fit | `blockSize[j] > blockSize[worstIdx]` | Maximize remainder | --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Algorithm Comparison — Mermaid Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Process Request R arrives]) --> Init[Initialize all blocks to free] Init --> Strategy{Choose Strategy} Strategy -->|First Fit| FF1[Set flag = NOT_FOUND
pointer p = block 0] FF1 --> FF2{p < n AND flag = NOT_FOUND?} FF2 -->|No| FFout[STOP - Allocation complete] FF2 -->|Yes| FF3{Block p size >= R?} FF3 -->|No| FF4[p = p + 1] FF4 --> FF2 FF3 -->|Yes| FF5[Allocate Block p to R
block p = block p - R
flag = FOUND] FF5 --> FFout Strategy -->|Best Fit| BF1[best = -1
bestSize = INFINITY] BF1 --> BF2{p < n?} BF2 -->|No| BF3{best = -1?} BF3 -->|Yes| BFfail[STOP - Allocation FAILED] BF3 -->|No| BF4[Allocate block best to R
block best = block best - R] BF4 --> BFout2[STOP - Allocation complete] BF2 -->|Yes| BF5{block p size >= R AND
block p size < bestSize?} BF5 -->|No| BF6[p = p + 1] BF6 --> BF2 BF5 -->|Yes| BF7[best = p
bestSize = block p size] BF7 --> BF6 Strategy -->|Worst Fit| WF1[worst = -1
worstSize = 0] WF1 --> WF2{p < n?} WF2 -->|No| WF3{worst = -1?} WF3 -->|Yes| WFfail[STOP - Allocation FAILED] WF3 -->|No| WF4[Allocate block worst to R
block worst = block worst - R] WF4 --> WFout3[STOP - Allocation complete] WF2 -->|Yes| WF5{block p size >= R AND
block p size > worstSize?} WF5 -->|No| WF6[p = p + 1] WF6 --> WF2 WF5 -->|Yes| WF7[worst = p
