worstSize = block p size] WF7 --> WF6 ``` ### 4.2 Memory State Transition Diagram (After Allocation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INITIAL["Initial Free Holes (KB)"] A1[Block1: 100] --- A2[Block2: 500] --- A3[Block3: 200] --- A4[Block4: 300] --- A5[Block5: 600] end subgraph FF["After First Fit"] B1[100 free] --- B2[288 free
was 500-212] --- B3[200 free] --- B4[300 free] --- B5[183 free
was 600-417] end subgraph BF["After Best Fit"] C1[100 free] --- C2[83 free
was 500-417] --- C3[88 free
was 200-112] --- C4[88 free
was 300-212] --- C5[174 free
was 600-426] end subgraph WF["After Worst Fit"] D1[100 free] --- D2[83 free
was 500-417] --- D3[200 free] --- D4[300 free] --- D5[276 free
was 388-112] end INITIAL --> FF INITIAL --> BF INITIAL --> WF ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Allocation Engine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"] I1[Memory Block Array] I2[Process Request Queue] end subgraph CTRL["Controller - menu driven main function"] C1[Strategy Selector] end subgraph ENGINES["Allocation Engines"] E1[First Fit Engine
break on first match] E2[Best Fit Engine
min waste search] E3[Worst Fit Engine
max remaining search] end subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"] O1[Allocation Table] O2[Updated Free Holes] O3[Fragmentation Stats] end I1 --> C1 I2 --> C1 C1 -->|choice 1| E1 C1 -->|choice 2| E2 C1 -->|choice 3| E3 E1 --> O1 E2 --> O1 E3 --> O1 E1 --> O2 E2 --> O2 E3 --> O2 E1 --> O3 E2 --> O3 E3 --> O3 ``` --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > *Differentiate between internal and external fragmentation. Which of the three contiguous allocation strategies (First, Best, Worst Fit) is most likely to suffer from external fragmentation, and why?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Fragmentation Type | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Internal** | Allocated memory that the process does **not** use (waste inside block) | Process requests 212 KB, gets 500 KB block → 288 KB wasted inside | | **External** | Free memory is sufficient in total, but scattered as **non-contiguous small holes** such that no single hole is large enough | Total free = 533 KB, but largest hole is 174 KB → cannot allocate 426 KB | **Strategy most prone to external fragmentation:** **Best Fit** ✅ **Reason (1 mark):** Best Fit always chooses the smallest sufficient hole, creating many *tiny leftover holes* that are too small for future requests. These accumulate over time, increasing external fragmentation. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *With a neat diagram, explain the working of the First Fit memory allocation algorithm. State one advantage and one disadvantage.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Working (2 marks):** The allocator scans the free-block list from the beginning and allocates the process to the **first** hole whose size is ≥ process size. The hole is split: the process gets the requested amount, and the remainder stays as a new (smaller) free hole. **Diagram (text representation — ½ mark):** ``` [ Proc1: 200 ][ Hole: 100 ][ Proc2: 300 ][ Hole: 175 ] ... ↑ First hole ≥ Request (P3 = 120) New: [Proc3: 120][Hole: 100][Hole: 55] ``` **Advantage (½ mark):** Fastest among the three — early termination on first match. **Disadvantage (½ mark):** Large holes at the end of the list get underutilized, increasing fragmentation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks) > **Module Choice: Implement memory management schemes (first fit, best fit and worst fit)** --- #### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Given the memory blocks of sizes `{300, 600, 350, 200, 750, 125}` KB and process requests of sizes `{115, 500, 358, 200, 375}` KB: (i) Simulate **First Fit** allocation and compute the total internal fragmentation. (ii) Simulate **Worst Fit** allocation and compute the total internal fragmentation. (iii) Which strategy yields better memory utilization? Justify with calculations. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write the C program logic (or pseudo-code) implementing the **Best Fit** algorithm. Explain how your code avoids the pitfalls of First Fit and Worst Fit. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]** **(i) First Fit Trace [3 marks]:** | Request | Block scan order | Allocated Block | Remaining hole | Internal Frag | |---|---|---|---|---| | 115 | 300→✅ | 300 | 185 | 0 | | 500 | 185→fail, 600→✅ | 600 | 100 | 0 | | 358 | 100→fail, 350→fail, 200→fail, 750→✅ | 750 | 392 | 0 | | 200 | 392→✅ | 392 | 192 | 0 | | 375 | 192→fail, 350→✅ | 350 | (350−375) = **−25 FAIL** | — | **Result:** 4 of 5 processes allocated. **Total Internal Fragmentation (First Fit) = 185 + 100 + 392 + 192 = 869 KB** (these are unallocated remainders *inside* allocated blocks). **(ii) Worst Fit Trace [3 marks]:** | Request | Allocated Block (largest) | Remaining | Internal Frag | |---|---|---|---| | 115 | 750 | 635 | 0 | | 500 | 635 (now max) | 135 | 0 | | 358 | 600 (now max) | 242 | 0 | | 200 | 635→wait, 350 (now max) | 150 | 0 | | 375 | 600? No, used. Next max unused: 300 | (300−375) FAIL | — | **Result:** 3 of 5 processes allocated. **Total Internal Frag (Worst Fit) = 635 + 135 + 242 + 150 = 1162 KB.** **(iii) Comparison [1 mark]:** $$ \text{Memory Utilization}_{FF} = \frac{115+500+358+200}{2325} \times 100 = \frac{1173}{2325} \times 100 \approx 50.45\% $$ $$ \text{Memory Utilization}_{WF} = \frac{115+500+358}{2325} \times 100 = \frac{973}{2325} \times 100 \approx 41.85\% $$ **First Fit is better** because it successfully allocated more processes (4 vs 3), giving higher memory utilization. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]** **Best Fit C Logic [5 marks]:** ```c void bestFit(int blockSize[], int m, int processSize[], int n) { int allocation[n]; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) allocation[i] = -1; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { int bestIdx = -1; for (int j = 0; j < m; j++) { if (blockSize[j] >= processSize[i]) { if (bestIdx == -1 || blockSize[j] < blockSize[bestIdx]) bestIdx = j; // smallest fitting block } } if (bestIdx != -1) { allocation[i] = bestIdx; blockSize[bestIdx] -= processSize[i]; } } } ``` **Avoiding pitfalls [2 marks]:** - **Avoids First Fit's drawback:** Does NOT `break` early — scans all blocks to find the *minimum* waste, not just the first acceptable block. - **Avoids Worst Fit's drawback:** Uses `<` (less than) comparison to pick the *smallest* fitting hole, not the *largest*. This minimizes leftover waste and improves internal packing. --- #### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the term **dynamic memory allocation**. With suitable examples, explain how the **Best Fit** algorithm allocates memory blocks to processes. Compute the total external fragmentation (as a percentage) after allocating processes of sizes `{120, 200, 50, 350}` KB from initial free blocks `{100, 300, 250, 400, 150}` KB using Best Fit. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast First Fit, Best Fit, and Worst Fit algorithms in terms of (i) time complexity, (ii) memory utilization, (iii) external fragmentation, and (iv) suitability for real-time OS. Recommend the best strategy for an embedded system with 512 KB RAM and justify. --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]** **Definition [1 mark]:** Dynamic memory allocation is the runtime assignment of memory blocks to processes whose sizes are not known at compile time. The OS maintains a list of free holes and allocates on-demand. **Best Fit Explanation [2 marks]:** Best Fit scans the *entire* free list and chooses the hole whose size is closest to (but ≥) the request. This minimizes the leftover waste. **Computation [4 marks]:** | Step | Request | Considered holes (≥ Req) | Selected (smallest) | Remaining | Holes state | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 120 | 300, 250, 400, 150 | **150** | 30 | {100, 300, 250, 400, 30} | | 2 | 200 | 300, 250, 400 | **250** | 50 | {100, 300, 50, 400, 30} | | 3 | 50 | 100, 300, 50, 400, 30 | **50** | 0 (delete hole) | {100, 300, 400, 30} | | 4 | 350 | 400 | **400** | 50 | {100, 300, 50, 30} | **Final free holes:** `{100, 300, 50, 30}` — Total = **480 KB**, but **largest single hole = 300 KB**. **External Fragmentation Calculation:** $$ EF(\%) = \frac{\text{Free holes smaller than next request (350 KB)}}{\text{Total free memory}} \times 100 $$ Holes < 350 KB = 100 + 50 + 30 = **180 KB** Total free memory = **480 KB** $$ EF = \frac{180}{480} \times 100 = 37.5\% $$ --- #### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]** **Comparison Table [4 marks]:** | Criterion | First Fit | Best Fit | Worst Fit | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Time Complexity | $O(n)$ average, faster (early stop) | $O(n)$ — full scan | $O(n)$ — full scan | | (ii) Memory Utilization | High (60–70%) | Variable (good short-term) | Poor (large leftovers wasted) | | (iii) External Fragmentation | Moderate | **Highest** (tiny holes) | Lowest | | (iv) Real-time OS Suitability | ✅ Good (predictable, fast) | ❌ Poor (unpredictable scan) | ❌ Poor (unpredictable scan) | **Embedded System Recommendation [3 marks]:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Recommendation: First Fit** ✅ > **Justification:** Embedded systems have **strict latency and determinism** requirements. > 1. **Speed:** First Fit's early termination gives the *fastest allocation time*, which is critical for ISR (Interrupt Service Routines) and real-time tasks. > 2. **Predictability:** Unlike Best Fit, First Fit's allocation time is bounded (best case O(1) if a hole is found at the start). > 3. **Simplicity:** With only 512 KB RAM, the free-list is small (a few holes), so First Fit's full-scan overhead is negligible. > 4. **Industry Practice:** Real-time OS like **FreeRTOS** and **VxWorks** use First Fit variants (`heap_4.c` in FreeRTOS). **Counter-argument:** Best Fit could pack processes more tightly, but the *unpredictable scan time* violates real-time deadlines. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losses** > 1. **Forgetting to update the hole after allocation** — examiners deduct **1 mark** if the remainder is not shown. > 2. **Confusing `break` vs full scan** — students often use `break` in Best Fit by mistake, making it equivalent to First Fit. **Avoid this.** > 3. **Skipping the "Allocation Failed" case** — always explicitly state when no block satisfies the request. > 4. **Using absolute value bars `\|x\|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks table rendering. Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. > 5. **Not showing fragmentation calculation** — KTU awards **dedicated marks** for the EF / IF formula. Always include the numerical answer with units. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - **Three strategies:** First Fit, Best Fit, Worst Fit — all have $O(n)$ time complexity. - **Selection keywords:** First = *first acceptable*, Best = *smallest fitting*, Worst = *largest hole*. - **Speed ranking:** First Fit (fastest) > Best Fit ≈ Worst Fit. - **Internal Fragmentation leader:** Worst Fit (most waste per allocation). - **External Fragmentation leader:** Best Fit (produces tiny holes). - **Memory Utilization leader:** First Fit (in most practical cases). - **Real-time OS choice:** **First Fit** — predictable and fast. - **C code distinction:** `break` = First Fit; `if (size < bestSize)` = Best Fit; `if (size > worstSize)` = Worst Fit. - **EF Formula:** $EF = \dfrac{\text{holes} < \text{request}}{\text{total free}} \times 100$. - **IF Formula:** $IF = \sum (\text{block allocated} - \text{process size})$. - **Linux `malloc` (glibc):** Uses a hybrid — segregated free lists (a form of Best Fit) plus mmap for large allocations. - **Examiner mantra:** "Show every step — hole list before AND after each allocation." - **Sample data to memorize:** Holes `{100, 500, 200, 300, 600}`; Requests `{212, 417, 112, 426}` — classic KTU problem. - **Practical implication:** Worst Fit is *rarely used in production*; First Fit and Best Fit dominate. ---

topics.title: Implement page replacement algorithms: FIFO, LRU and optimal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement page replacement algorithms: FIFO, LRU and optimal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Page Replacement Algorithm:** A memory management strategy used by the Operating System when a requested page is **not present** in the physical memory (RAM), causing a **page fault**. The OS must then decide *which existing page in memory to evict* to make room for the newly required page, with the goal of minimizing the number of page faults.

In a system using **Demand Paging**, processes are loaded into memory only when their pages are needed. When all frames are occupied and a new page is requested, the OS invokes a page replacement algorithm. The efficiency of the entire memory subsystem is heavily dependent on this choice — it directly influences the **Page Fault Rate** and consequently the system's **Effective Access Time (EAT)**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Page Fault** | A type of interrupt (trap) raised when a process tries to access a page that is **not mapped** in physical memory (RAM). |
| **Page Hit** | The requested page is already present in a physical frame — **zero disk I/O** required. |
| **Page Miss** | Synonym for Page Fault (older textbooks). |
| **Belady's Anomaly** | An unusual phenomenon where **increasing the number of frames can increase the page fault count** (observed in FIFO). |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Library Reading Room

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine a **small study table with only 3 slots** (your physical frames). You are revising from **20 different textbooks** (pages) one chapter at a time (reference string). When the table is full and a new textbook arrives, you must **remove one** to make space. Which one do you remove?
>
> - **FIFO** → Remove the one you placed on the table **earliest in time** (queue order).
> - **LRU (Least Recently Used)** → Remove the textbook you **haven't opened for the longest time** (recency heuristic).
> - **Optimal (OPT / Belady's Algorithm)** → Remove the textbook that you **will not need for the longest time in the future** (clairvoyant / oracle).
>
> The fewer times you have to shuffle textbooks, the **better your study efficiency** — analogous to lower page faults and faster execution.

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Database Buffer Pools:** MySQL InnoDB and PostgreSQL use variants of LRU (e.g., Clock-Sweep) to keep hot indexes in RAM.
- **Web Browser Caching:** Chrome & Firefox evict the least-recently-used tab from memory.
- **CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai):** Eviction policies on edge servers are direct descendants of these algorithms.
- **CPU Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB):** Even hardware caches use aging/recency schemes similar to LRU.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Flow (Common to All Three Algorithms)

1. Process reference string **R = r₁, r₂, r₃, …, rₙ** is generated.
2. Maintain a frame table (size = **m** frames), initially empty.
3. For each reference **rᵢ**:
   - **If rᵢ ∈ frames** → *Page Hit* (cost ≈ 1 memory access).
   - **Else if frames < m** → *Page Fault*, load **rᵢ** into an empty frame.
   - **Else frames == m** → *Page Fault*, evict one page using the chosen policy, then load **rᵢ**.

### 2.2 The Three Algorithms — Logic Breakdown

#### (A) FIFO (First-In, First-Out)
- Treats frames as a **circular queue**.
- The page that **arrived earliest** is replaced, regardless of how often it has been used.
- **Pros:** Trivially simple to implement (just an index counter).
- **Cons:** Suffers from **Belady's Anomaly** — more frames ⇒ more faults (counter-intuitive!).

#### (B) LRU (Least Recently Used)
- Uses the **recency** of use — replaces the page whose **last reference was the farthest in the past**.
- Based on the principle of **temporal locality**.
- **Pros:** No Belady's anomaly; very good practical hit ratio.
- **Cons:** Expensive hardware — requires a timestamp/counter on every reference or a stack.

#### (C) Optimal (OPT / Belady's Algorithm)
- A **theoretical oracle** — replaces the page that will **not be used for the longest time in the future**.
- **Pros:** Guarantees the **minimum possible page faults** for any given reference string.
- **Cons:** Requires future knowledge → **impossible to implement** in a real running system. Used only as a benchmark to measure other algorithms.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Page Fault Rate (PFR)** | $\text{PFR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Page Faults}}{\text{Total References}}$ | Range: 0 to 1 |
| **Page Hit Rate (PHR)** | $\text{PHR} = 1 - \text{PFR}$ | Useful for comparing algorithms |
| **Effective Access Time (EAT)** | $\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot \text{mem\_access} + p \cdot \text{page\_fault\_service}$ | $p$ = page fault rate |
| **Typical Page Fault Service Time** | $\approx 1\text{ to } 10$ **milliseconds** | Includes disk I/O |
| **Typical Memory Access Time** | $\approx 10\text{ to } 100$ **nanoseconds** | Crucial ratio difference |
| **Belady's Anomaly** | $\text{Faults}(m{+}1) > \text{Faults}(m)$ for some $m$ | Happens only with FIFO |
| **Stack Property** | $\text{Frames in OPT}(m) \subseteq \text{Frames in OPT}(m{+}1)$ | OPT & LRU satisfy this |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for KTU:** Always express Page Fault Rate as a **fraction** or **percentage**, never as a raw decimal alone. Example: *"Out of 20 references, 9 faults → PFR = 9/20 = 45%"*.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Why It Matters

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Cloud Computing** | AWS Lambda cold-start latency governed by cache replacement |
| **Mobile OS** | Android uses **LRU-style eviction** for app memory trimming |
| **Compiler Design** | Instruction cache prefetching uses similar heuristics |
| **Embedded Systems** | RTOS use simpler FIFO/Clock due to hardware constraints |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The "Classic" KTU Reference String

Let us solve using the textbook reference string:

**Reference String:** $R = 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1, 7, 0, 1$

**Number of Frames:** $m = 3$

**Total References:** $n = 20$

---

### 3.2 (A) FIFO — Full Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** Evict the page that was loaded *earliest*. We use a counter to track arrival order. The page with the smallest counter is replaced.

| Step | Ref | Frame 1 | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | Hit/Fault | Evicted |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **7** | 7 | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 2 | **0** | 7 | 0 | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 3 | **1** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | – |
| 4 | **2** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **7** (oldest) |
| 5 | **0** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 6 | **3** | 2 | 3 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **0** |
| 7 | **0** | 2 | 3 | 0 | ❌ Fault | **1** |
| 8 | **4** | 4 | 3 | 0 | ❌ Fault | **2** |
| 9 | **2** | 4 | 2 | 0 | ❌ Fault | **3** |
| 10 | **3** | 4 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **0** |
| 11 | **0** | 0 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **4** |
| 12 | **3** | 0 | 2 | 3 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 13 | **2** | 0 | 2 | 3 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 14 | **1** | 0 | 1 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **2** |
| 15 | **2** | 0 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **3** |
| 16 | **0** | 0 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 17 | **1** | 0 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 18 | **7** | 7 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **0** |
| 19 | **0** | 7 | 0 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **1** |
| 20 | **1** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **2** |

**Total Page Faults (FIFO, m=3):** $\text{PF}_{\text{FIFO}} = 15$

$$
\text{PFR}_{\text{FIFO}} = \frac{15}{20} = 0.75 = 75\%
$$

---

### 3.3 (B) LRU — Full Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** Evict the page whose **last reference was the farthest in the past**. Track a "recent-use timestamp" for each page in memory.

| Step | Ref | Frame 1 | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | Hit/Fault | Evicted (LRU) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **7** | 7 | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 2 | **0** | 7 | 0 | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 3 | **1** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | – |
| 4 | **2** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **7** (used at t=1) |
| 5 | **0** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit (refresh 0) | – |
| 6 | **3** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **1** (used at t=3) |
| 7 | **0** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Hit (refresh 0) | – |
| 8 | **4** | 4 | 0 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **2** (used at t=4) |
| 9 | **2** | 4 | 0 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **3** (used at t=6) |
| 10 | **3** | 4 | 3 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **0** (used at t=7) |
| 11 | **0** | 0 | 3 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **4** (used at t=8) |
| 12 | **3** | 0 | 3 | 2 | ✅ Hit (refresh 3) | – |
| 13 | **2** | 0 | 3 | 2 | ✅ Hit (refresh 2) | – |
| 14 | **1** | 1 | 3 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **0** (used at t=11) |
| 15 | **2** | 1 | 3 | 2 | ✅ Hit (refresh 2) | – |
| 16 | **0** | 1 | 0 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **3** (used at t=12) |
| 17 | **1** | 1 | 0 | 2 | ✅ Hit (refresh 1) | – |
| 18 | **7** | 7 | 0 | 2 | ❌ Fault | **2** (used at t=15) |
| 19 | **0** | 7 | 0 | 2 | ✅ Hit (refresh 0) | – |
| 20 | **1** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **2** (used at t=18) |

**Total Page Faults (LRU, m=3):** $\text{PF}_{\text{LRU}} = 12$

$$
\text{PFR}_{\text{LRU}} = \frac{12}{20} = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

---

### 3.4 (C) OPTIMAL (Belady's Algorithm) — Full Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** Evict the page whose **next use is farthest in the future** (or never used again). Requires looking ahead at the **entire remaining** reference string.

| Step | Ref | Frame 1 | Frame 2 | Frame 3 | Hit/Fault | Evicted (Optimal) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **7** | 7 | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 2 | **0** | 7 | 0 | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 3 | **1** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | – |
| 4 | **2** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **7** (next use at t=18) |
| 5 | **0** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 6 | **3** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **1** (next use at t=14) |
| 7 | **0** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 8 | **4** | 4 | 0 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **2** (next use at t=9) |
| 9 | **2** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ❌ Fault | **4** (never used again) |
| 10 | **3** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 11 | **0** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 12 | **3** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 13 | **2** | 2 | 0 | 3 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 14 | **1** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **3** (never used again) |
| 15 | **2** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 16 | **0** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 17 | **1** | 2 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 18 | **7** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Fault | **2** (never used again) |
| 19 | **0** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 20 | **1** | 7 | 0 | 1 | ✅ Hit | – |

**Total Page Faults (OPT, m=3):** $\text{PF}_{\text{OPT}} = 9$

$$
\text{PFR}_{\text{OPT}} = \frac{9}{20} = 0.45 = 45\%
$$

---

### 3.5 Comparative Summary Matrix

$$
\text{For } R = \{7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,2,1,2,0,1,7,0,1\},\; m = 3:
$$

| Algorithm | Total Page Faults | Page Fault Rate | Stack Property? | Belady-Safe? | Realizable? |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **FIFO** | 15 | **75%** | ❌ No | ❌ No (Anomaly possible) | ✅ Yes |
| **LRU** | 12 | **60%** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (costly) |
| **OPTIMAL** | 9 | **45%** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (oracle) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Optimality:** OPTIMAL always yields the **minimum possible page faults** for a given reference string. The inequality $\text{PF}_{\text{OPT}} \le \text{PF}_{\text{LRU}} \le \text{PF}_{\text{FIFO}}$ is **not always strict**, but in our example: $9 < 12 < 15$. ✔

---

### 3.6 Belady's Anomaly — Demonstration

Using a **shorter reference string** $R' = 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$:

**FIFO with $m = 3$ frames:**

$$
\text{Trace} \Rightarrow \text{Faults at } t = 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,9,10 \Rightarrow \boxed{\text{PF} = 9}
$$

**FIFO with $m = 4$ frames:**

$$
\text{Trace} \Rightarrow \text{Faults at } t = 1,2,3,4,6,7,8,9,10 \Rightarrow \boxed{\text{PF} = 10}
$$

**Result:** Adding a frame (3 → 4) **increased** faults (9 → 10). This counter-intuitive behavior is **Belady's Anomaly**.

$$
\exists R' \;\text{such that}\; \text{FIFO}_{m{+}1}(R') > \text{FIFO}_{m}(R')
$$

LRU and OPT do **not** suffer from this anomaly because they satisfy the **stack property** (a set of frames for $m{+}1$ is a superset of the set for $m$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Demand Paging with Replacement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU generates virtual address r_i"] --> B{"Page r_i in any frame?"}
    B -- "YES - Hit" --> C["Access physical memory directly"]
    B -- "NO - Fault" --> D{"All frames occupied?"}
    D -- "NO - Free frame" --> E["Load page r_i into free frame"]
    D -- "YES - All full" --> F["Invoke Replacement Algorithm"]
    F --> G1["FIFO: evict oldest"]
    F --> G2["LRU: evict least recently used"]
    F --> G3["OPT: evict farthest future use"]
    G1 --> H["Update frame table"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I["Load r_i into vacated frame"]
    I --> J["Resume process"]
    C --> K["Increment hit counter"]
    E --> L["Increment fault counter"]
    J --> L
    K --> M["Report PFR at end"]
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Comparative Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ALG["Page Replacement Policies"]
        direction TB
        P1["FIFO Policy"]
        P2["LRU Policy"]
        P3["OPT Policy"]
    end
    P1 --- D1["Evict page with smallest arrival timestamp"]
    P2 --- D2["Evict page with oldest last-use timestamp"]
    P3 --- D3["Evict page with farthest future reference"]
    ALG --> OUT["Output: Frame table after each reference + Total Faults"]
```

### 4.3 State-Transition Diagram of a Frame Slot

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty
    Empty --> Occupied: Page loaded on Fault
    Occupied --> Occupied: Hit refresh timestamp
    Occupied --> Empty: Evicted by FIFO or LRU or OPT
    Empty --> Occupied: New page loaded
    Occupied --> [*]: Process termination
```

### 4.4 C Implementation Skeleton (Reference for Lab Viva)

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
    int frames, n, i, j, k, page_faults = 0, found, oldest, pos, farthest, future_use;
    int reference_string[] = {7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3,
                              0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1, 7, 0, 1};
    int frame[10], counter[10];      /* counter holds timestamp for LRU */
    int use_future[10];              /* holds next-use distance for OPT */

    printf("Enter number of frames: ");
    scanf("%d", &frames);

    for (i = 0; i < frames; i++) {
        frame[i]   = -1;            /* empty */
        counter[i] = 0;
    }

    n = sizeof(reference_string) / sizeof(reference_string[0]);

    /* ----------- FIFO LOGIC ----------- */
    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        found = 0;
        for (j = 0; j < frames; j++)
            if (frame[j] == reference_string[i]) { found = 1; break; }

        if (!found) {
            /* find empty slot or oldest */
            pos = -1;
            for (j = 0; j < frames; j++)
                if (frame[j] == -1) { pos = j; break; }
            if (pos == -1) {
                pos = 0;
                for (j = 1; j < frames; j++)
                    if (counter[j] < counter[pos]) pos = j;
            }
            frame[pos]   = reference_string[i];
            counter[pos] = i;        /* arrival order = i */
            page_faults++;
        }
        printf("Step %2d -> ", i + 1);
        for (k = 0; k < frames; k++) printf("%2d ", frame[k]);
        printf(found ? " (Hit)\n" : " (Fault)\n");
    }
    printf("\nTotal Page Faults (FIFO) = %d\n", page_faults);
    return 0;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation for Lab Records:** Replace the FIFO block with two analogous blocks for **LRU** (use `i` as access timestamp directly) and **OPT** (loop through `reference_string[i+1..n-1]` to compute next-use distance). Always show **a hand-traced table** in the record alongside the code output for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a **page fault**. What is the role of a page replacement algorithm when a page fault occurs?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **page fault** is a hardware interrupt (trap) generated by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) when a process references a page that is **not currently resident** in any physical frame of RAM.
>
> When a page fault occurs, the OS kernel is invoked. If a **free frame** is available, the page is simply loaded from secondary storage. However, if **all frames are occupied**, the OS must call a **page replacement algorithm** to decide *which existing page to evict (swap out to disk)* in order to make room for the newly demanded page. The replacement algorithm directly affects the **page fault rate** and overall system throughput.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks role)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **FIFO** and **LRU** page replacement algorithms. State **one advantage** and **one disadvantage** of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | FIFO | LRU |
|---|---|---|
| **Eviction Policy** | Replace page loaded earliest | Replace page unused for the longest time |
| **Hardware Support** | Simple counter/queue | Expensive timestamps or stack |
| **Advantage** | Easy to implement; minimal overhead | No Belady's anomaly; good hit ratio |
| **Disadvantage** | Suffers from Belady's Anomaly | High implementation cost |

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark distinction + 1 mark FIFO points + 1 mark LRU points)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both choices below are independent modules from the same experiment.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified for Lab Module 2]**

**(a)** Consider the reference string: $R = 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ with **3 page frames**. Compute the number of page faults for the **FIFO** algorithm and show the frame contents after each reference. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Repeat part (a) for the **LRU** algorithm. Compare the page fault counts and explain which algorithm performed better and why. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) FIFO with m = 3 (7 Marks)**

| Step | Ref | F1 | F2 | F3 | Hit/Fault | Replaced |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **1** | 1 | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 2 | **2** | 1 | 2 | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 3 | **3** | 1 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | – |
| 4 | **4** | 4 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 1 |
| 5 | **1** | 4 | 1 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 2 |
| 6 | **2** | 4 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 3 |
| 7 | **5** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 4 |
| 8 | **1** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 9 | **2** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 10 | **3** | 5 | 3 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 1 |
| 11 | **4** | 5 | 3 | 4 | ❌ Fault | 2 |
| 12 | **5** | 5 | 3 | 4 | ✅ Hit | – |

**Total Page Faults (FIFO):** $\boxed{9}$

---

**(b) LRU with m = 3 (7 Marks)**

| Step | Ref | F1 | F2 | F3 | Hit/Fault | Replaced (LRU) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **1** | 1 | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 2 | **2** | 1 | 2 | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 3 | **3** | 1 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | – |
| 4 | **4** | 4 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 1 (used at t=1) |
| 5 | **1** | 4 | 1 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 2 (used at t=2) |
| 6 | **2** | 4 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 3 (used at t=3) |
| 7 | **5** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 4 (used at t=4) |
| 8 | **1** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 9 | **2** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 10 | **3** | 3 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 5 (used at t=7) |
| 11 | **4** | 3 | 4 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 1 (used at t=8) |
| 12 | **5** | 3 | 4 | 5 | ❌ Fault | 2 (used at t=9) |

**Total Page Faults (LRU):** $\boxed{10}$

---

**Comparison (1 mark within part b):**
$$
\text{PF}_{\text{FIFO}} = 9 \;<\; \text{PF}_{\text{LRU}} = 10
$$
FIFO outperformed LRU here — a rare case showing FIFO is **not strictly worse** than LRU for *every* reference string. However, this string also demonstrates **Belady's Anomaly** (with 4 frames, FIFO gives 10 faults, worse than 3 frames). LRU remains more robust in general.

---

### 📘 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for Lab Module 2]**

**(a)** What is **Belady's Anomaly**? Demonstrate it using the reference string $R = 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ for **FIFO** with **3 frames** and **4 frames**. Show frame contents for both cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the same reference string $R = 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ and **3 frames**, compute the number of page faults using the **OPTIMAL** algorithm. Comment on whether the result satisfies the optimality property. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Belady's Anomaly — Definition + Demonstration (7 Marks)**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Belady's Anomaly is the phenomenon in which, for certain reference strings, **increasing the number of page frames causes an *increase* in the number of page faults** when using the FIFO algorithm.

**FIFO with m = 3 (already shown above) → PF = 9**

**FIFO with m = 4 (5 marks table):**

| Step | Ref | F1 | F2 | F3 | F4 | Hit/Fault | Replaced |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **1** | 1 | – | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 2 | **2** | 1 | 2 | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 3 | **3** | 1 | 2 | 3 | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 4 | **4** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | ❌ Fault | – |
| 5 | **1** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 6 | **2** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 7 | **5** | 5 | 2 | 3 | 4 | ❌ Fault | 1 |
| 8 | **1** | 5 | 1 | 3 | 4 | ❌ Fault | 2 |
| 9 | **2** | 5 | 1 | 2 | 4 | ❌ Fault | 3 |
| 10 | **3** | 5 | 1 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 4 |
| 11 | **4** | 4 | 1 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 5 |
| 12 | **5** | 4 | 5 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 1 |

**Total Page Faults (FIFO, m=4):** $\boxed{10}$

**Conclusion:** $m=3 \Rightarrow 9$ faults; $m=4 \Rightarrow 10$ faults. With **more frames**, **more page faults** occurred → this is **Belady's Anomaly**.

---

**(b) OPTIMAL with m = 3 (7 Marks)**

| Step | Ref | F1 | F2 | F3 | Hit/Fault | Replaced (Optimal) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **1** | 1 | – | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 2 | **2** | 1 | 2 | – | ❌ Fault | – |
| 3 | **3** | 1 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | – |
| 4 | **4** | 4 | 2 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 1 (next at t=5) |
| 5 | **1** | 4 | 1 | 3 | ❌ Fault | 2 (next at t=6) |
| 6 | **2** | 4 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 3 (next at t=10) |
| 7 | **5** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 4 (never again) |
| 8 | **1** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 9 | **2** | 5 | 1 | 2 | ✅ Hit | – |
| 10 | **3** | 3 | 1 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 5 (never again) |
| 11 | **4** | 3 | 4 | 2 | ❌ Fault | 1 (next at t=12) |
| 12 | **5** | 3 | 4 | 5 | ❌ Fault | 2 (never again) |

**Total Page Faults (OPT):** $\boxed{7}$

**Comment (2 marks):**
$$
\text{PF}_{\text{OPT}} = 7 \;\le\; \text{PF}_{\text{FIFO}} = 9 \;\le\; \text{PF}_{\text{LRU}} = 10
$$
The OPTIMAL algorithm yields the **fewest faults** for this reference string, confirming the **Optimality Property**:
$$
\forall R,\;\forall m,\;\text{PF}_{\text{OPT}}(R, m) = \min_{\pi \in \Pi} \text{PF}_{\pi}(R, m)
$$
where $\Pi$ is the set of all possible replacement policies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to mark Hits vs Faults in the trace table** → KTU examiner expects a Hit/Fault column with ❌/✅ marks. Omitting it = −1 mark.
> 2. **Misapplying LRU** → LRU evicts based on *last USE time*, **not** the page loaded *earliest*. Conflating it with FIFO = full −7 on part (b).
> 3. **Belady's Anomaly** → Only FIFO shows it. Writing that "LRU also shows anomaly" = 0 for definition.
> 4. **OPT's "next use"** → If a page is **never used again**, it is the immediate choice to evict. Students often skip this case.
> 5. **Page Fault Rate** → Always quote as `faults/total` or percentage. Just writing "75" without denominator = −1 mark.
> 6. **Empty frames on first load** → Don't count an empty frame as a "fault replacement" — it is just loading into a free slot. The first $m$ loads are mandatory faults.

---

### 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Re-read before every KTU exam**

- ✅ **Page Fault** = page not in RAM → causes disk I/O (~ms cost). **Page Hit** = in RAM → fast (~ns cost).
- ✅ The **Effective Access Time (EAT)** is **dominated** by page faults; even a 1% fault rate can slow the system by 10×.
- ✅ **FIFO** = evict the page that has been in memory the longest. Simple but suffers from **Belady's Anomaly**.
- ✅ **LRU** = evict the page unused for the longest time. Uses **temporal locality**. No anomaly, but hardware-expensive.
- ✅ **OPTIMAL** = evict the page not needed for the longest time in future. **Theoretical benchmark only** — never implementable.
- ✅ The **inequality** $\text{PF}_{\text{OPT}} \le \text{PF}_{\text{LRU}} \le \text{PF}_{\text{FIFO}}$ is generally observed (but NOT a strict universal law).
- ✅ For the **classic KTU string** $7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,2,1,2,0,1,7,0,1$ with **3 frames**:
  - FIFO = **15 faults**
  - LRU = **12 faults**
  - OPT = **9 faults**
- ✅ **Belady's Anomaly proof string:** $R = 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5$ → FIFO(3 frames)=9 faults, FIFO(4 frames)=10 faults.
- ✅ **Stack property** holds for LRU and OPT but **not** for FIFO. This is the formal reason for Belady's Anomaly.
- ✅ In **lab practicals**, always show: (i) **trace table**, (ii) **total faults**, (iii) **PFR**, (iv) **sample I/O**.
- ✅ **Real-world use:** Android, MySQL InnoDB, and CDN edge servers all use **LRU or clock-proximity** variants — never naive FIFO.
- ✅ Be ready to **write the C program** for any of the three algorithms under exam conditions — practice the optimal version's lookahead loop carefully.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of disk scheduling using FCFS, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK and SSTF algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of disk scheduling using FCFS, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK and SSTF algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 💽 Disk Scheduling Algorithms — Module Experiment 2

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Disk Scheduling** is the process of determining the order in which pending I/O requests for the disk are serviced. The OS uses a *disk controller* and a *disk-arm scheduling algorithm* to minimize the total **seek time** — the time taken by the read/write head to move from its current cylinder to the requested cylinder.

In KTU 2024 terminology, disk scheduling is a core **I/O management** function of the OS kernel. The goal is to **minimize mechanical arm movement**, since the physical rotation and radial translation of the disk arm dominate I/O latency. Six classical algorithms form the syllabus mandate:

1. **FCFS** — First-Come, First-Served
2. **SSTF** — Shortest Seek Time First
3. **SCAN** — Elevator Algorithm
4. **C-SCAN** — Circular SCAN
5. **LOOK**
6. **C-LOOK**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Assumption:** Disk cylinders are numbered linearly (e.g., **0 to 199**), and the *initial head position* is given. Total head movement (in cylinders) is the **performance metric**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Elevator & The Librarian

Imagine you are a **librarian** standing at aisle **53** of a long library with aisles 0–199. Eight patrons simultaneously shout their aisle numbers: *98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67*.

- **FCFS** → You service them in the exact order they shouted (ignoring distance).
- **SSTF** → You always run to the *nearest* shouting patron next.
- **SCAN** → You walk to one end of the library, servicing everyone along the way, then walk back. Like a **lift/elevator**.
- **C-SCAN** → You walk to the far end, then **teleport** back to the start and walk forward again. Fairer waiting times.
- **LOOK / C-LOOK** → The *smart* elevator — it doesn't crawl all the way to the walls if the last request is before the wall.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering parallel:** Modern SSDs (Solid-State Drives) do **not** need disk scheduling because they have *no mechanical arm*. The algorithms studied here apply to **HDDs, magnetic tapes, optical drives, and robotic warehouse arms**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Each Algorithm

### 🟢 FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)
- Services requests in the **exact order of arrival**.
- Pros: *Fair, starvation-free, simplest implementation*.
- Cons: *Wild head swings — worst average seek time*.
- **Starvation:** ❌ None.

### 🟡 SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)
- A **greedy, non-preemptive** shortest-distance-first policy.
- Picks the pending request with the **minimum absolute difference** from the current head.
- Pros: *Lower average seek time than FCFS*.
- Cons: *May cause starvation of far-away requests*.
- **Starvation:** ⚠️ Possible.

### 🔵 SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)
- Head moves in a **fixed direction** (e.g., towards higher cylinders), servicing all requests en route, until it hits the **last cylinder**.
- Then **reverses** direction.
- Pros: *No starvation, low variance in wait time*.
- Cons: *Requests just behind the head must wait for a full sweep*.

### 🟣 C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)
- Head moves towards the higher end, servicing requests, until the last cylinder.
- Then **jumps** back to cylinder 0 **without servicing** any request on the return path, and starts moving up again.
- Provides a **more uniform wait time** (treating cylinders as a circular list).

### 🟠 LOOK
- Like SCAN, but the head **does not travel all the way to the end of the disk**. It only goes as far as the **last request** in that direction, then reverses.
- Saves unnecessary traversal.

### 🔴 C-LOOK
- A hybrid: head moves in one direction servicing all requests up to the **last request** (not the disk end), then **jumps** to the **lowest pending request** and continues forward.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Boundary Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Rule** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Seek distance between two requests $r_i \rightarrow r_{i+1}$ | $d_i = \vert r_{i+1} - r_i \vert$ | cylinders |
| Total Seek Time (TST) | $TST = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \vert r_{i+1} - r_i \vert$ | cylinders |
| Average Seek Time (AST) | $AST = \frac{TST}{n}$ | cylinders/request |
| SCAN — end reached | $d_{end} = \vert C_{max} - r_{last} \vert$ where $C_{max} = 199$ | cylinders |
| C-SCAN — circular jump | Includes $\vert 199 - 0 \vert = 199$ once | cylinders |
| LOOK / C-LOOK | End point = last request in that direction (not $C_{max}$) | cylinders |
| SSTF — selection rule | $\min_{r \in Q} \vert r - \text{head}_{cur} \vert$ | cylinders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition for KTU 2024 labs:** Unless stated otherwise, the disk has **200 cylinders (0–199)** and the head *starts at the given initial position*. The head is assumed to move at **uniform speed** (linear model).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Systems:** DB2, Oracle, and PostgreSQL use variants of LOOK/SCAN for multi-disk request queuing.
- **Operating Systems:** Linux's default *Deadline scheduler* and CFQ use SCAN-like fairness.
- **Robotics & CNC machines:** LOOK is the literal movement model of gantry robots that pick components.
- **Magnetic tape drives:** Used in legacy backup systems — same head-movement problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Solution (Worked Example)

## 3.1 Problem Statement

> Service the request queue: **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**
> Initial head position: **53**
> Disk range: **0 – 199**
> Compute **Total Head Movement (THM)** for all six algorithms.

---

### 3.2 🟢 FCFS — Order is the arrival order

Sequence served: $53 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{FCFS} &= \vert 98-53 \vert + \vert 183-98 \vert + \vert 37-183 \vert + \vert 122-37 \vert \\
&\quad + \vert 14-122 \vert + \vert 124-14 \vert + \vert 65-124 \vert + \vert 67-65 \vert \\
&= 45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 \\
&= \mathbf{640 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 🟡 SSTF — Always pick nearest pending request

| **Step** | **Current Head** | **Pending Distances** | **Chosen Next** | **Movement** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 53 | 45, 130, 16, 69, 39, 71, 12, 14 | **65** | 12 |
| 2 | 65 | 33, 118, 28, 57, 59, **2**, 2 | **67** | 2 |
| 3 | 67 | 31, 116, 30, 55, 57, — , — | **37** | 30 |
| 4 | 37 | 61, 85, 85, 23, 87, — , — | **14** | 23 |
| 5 | 14 | 84, 108, 169, 110, 108, — , — | **98** | 84 |
| 6 | 98 | — , 85, 24, 24, 26, — , — | **122** | 24 |
| 7 | 122 | — , 61, 2, **2** | **124** | 2 |
| 8 | 124 | — , 59, — , — | **183** | 59 |

Served order: **65, 67, 37, 14, 98, 122, 124, 183**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{SSTF} &= 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\
&= \mathbf{236 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 🔵 SCAN (moving towards higher end first, then reversing)

Pending requests sorted: $\{14, 37, 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183\}$

**Phase 1 (head 53 → 199):** Service 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, then travel to 199
**Phase 2 (199 → 0):** Service 37, 14

Served order: **65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 199, 37, 14**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{SCAN} &= \vert 65-53 \vert + \vert 67-65 \vert + \vert 98-67 \vert + \vert 122-98 \vert + \vert 124-122 \vert \\
&\quad + \vert 183-124 \vert + \vert 199-183 \vert + \vert 199-37 \vert + \vert 37-14 \vert \\
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 \\
&= \mathbf{331 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 🟣 C-SCAN (towards higher end, circular jump to 0, then up again)

Served order: **65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 199, 0, 14, 37**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{C\text{-}SCAN} &= \vert 65-53 \vert + \vert 67-65 \vert + \vert 98-67 \vert + \vert 122-98 \vert + \vert 124-122 \vert \\
&\quad + \vert 183-124 \vert + \vert 199-183 \vert + \vert 199-0 \vert + \vert 14-0 \vert + \vert 37-14 \vert \\
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 199 + 14 + 23 \\
&= \mathbf{382 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **circular jump** term $\vert 199-0 \vert = 199$ — this is the defining extra cost of C-SCAN.

---

### 3.6 🟠 LOOK (mirror of SCAN, but stops at last request)

Served order: **65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 37, 14**
(Head reverses at 183 — not at 199 — since 183 is the last request in the upward direction.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{LOOK} &= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + \vert 37-183 \vert + 23 \\
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 146 + 23 \\
&= \mathbf{299 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> LOOK saves SCAN's wasteful $199 \rightarrow 183 = 16$ cylinder trip on the return phase. (Note: in this particular problem, the last request 183 equals 199 only conceptually; the savings appear on the *reverse* sweep.)

---

### 3.7 🔴 C-LOOK

Served order: **65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 14, 37**
(After serving 183, jump back to lowest pending request 14, then continue forward.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{THM}_{C\text{-}LOOK} &= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + \vert 14-183 \vert + \vert 37-14 \vert \\
&= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 169 + 23 \\
&= \mathbf{322 \text{ cylinders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.8 📊 Comparative Performance Matrix

| **Algorithm** | **Service Order** | **Total Head Movement (cylinders)** | **Starvation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| FCFS | 98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67 | **640** | None |
| SSTF | 65, 67, 37, 14, 98, 122, 124, 183 | **236** ✅ Best | Possible |
| SCAN | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 199, 37, 14 | **331** | None |
| C-SCAN | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 199, 0, 14, 37 | **382** | None |
| LOOK | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 37, 14 | **299** | None |
| C-LOOK | 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183, 14, 37 | **322** | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Lab Viva:** If a question states *"using initial head at 53 and disk range 0–199"*, the answer keys above are the **expected reference solutions**. Always show the *sorted request set* first.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Disk Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Issues I/O Request] --> B[Request Queue FIFO Buffer]
    B --> C{Scheduling Algorithm}
    C --> C1[FCFS Module]
    C --> C2[SSTF Module]
    C --> C3[SCAN Module]
    C --> C4[CSCAN Module]
    C --> C5[LOOK Module]
    C --> C6[CLOOK Module]
    C1 --> D[Sorted Service Sequence]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E[Disk Controller]
    E --> F[Disk Arm Physical Movement]
    F --> G[Total Seek Time Computation]
```

## 4.2 SCAN Algorithm — Head Movement Trace

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Cylinder 0] --- A14[14] --- A37[37] --- A53((Head Start 53)) --- A65[65] --- A67[67] --- A98[98] --- A122[122] --- A124[124] --- A183[183] --- A199[199]
    A53 -.->|move up| A65
    A65 -.->|2| A67
    A67 -.->|31| A98
    A98 -.->|24| A122
    A122 -.->|2| A124
    A124 -.->|59| A183
    A183 -.->|16| A199
    A199 -.->|reverse 162| A37
    A37 -.->|23| A14
```

## 4.3 C-LOOK Algorithm — Circular Trace

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A14[14] --- A37[37] --- A53((Head Start 53)) --- A65[65] --- A67[67] --- A98[98] --- A122[122] --- A124[124] --- A183[183]
    A53 -.->|12| A65
    A65 -.->|2| A67
    A67 -.->|31| A98
    A98 -.->|24| A122
    A122 -.->|2| A124
    A124 -.->|59| A183
    A183 -.->|circular jump 169| A14
    A14 -.->|23| A37
```

## 4.4 Comparative Flow of Algorithm Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Pending Request Queue] --> R{Fairness Priority?}
    R -->|Strict Order| FCFS[FCFS Algorithm]
    R -->|Performance| SSTF[SSTF Greedy Pick]
    SSTF --> S{Starvation Concern?}
    S -->|Yes| SCAN[SCAN Elevator Sweep]
    S -->|Uniform Wait| CSCAN[C-SCAN Circular]
    SCAN --> L{Need Edge Traversal?}
    L -->|No| LOOK[LOOK Reverse at Last Req]
    L -->|Yes| SCAN
    CSCAN --> CL{Need Edge Traversal?}
    CL -->|No| CLOOK[C-LOOK Jump to Lowest]
    CL -->|Yes| CSCAN
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is disk scheduling? Why is it necessary in operating systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Disk scheduling is the technique used by the OS to determine the order in which pending disk I/O requests are serviced, with the goal of minimizing total seek time.
- **Necessity (2 marks):** Since the read/write head is a *mechanical component*, its radial movement across platters dominates I/O latency. With multiple concurrent processes issuing requests, an optimal order **reduces arm movement**, thereby improving **throughput** and reducing **average response time**. Without scheduling, random ordering (FCFS) causes wild head swings and poor performance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between SCAN and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **SCAN** | **C-SCAN** |
|---|---|---|
| Return path | Services requests on the way back | Jumps to cylinder 0, services only while moving up |
| Wait time variance | Higher (cylinders near start wait longer) | Uniform (treats queue as circular) |
| Extra movement | Reverse sweep cost | $199 \rightarrow 0$ circular jump (199 cyl) |

*(1 mark for each valid difference; minimum two differences needed.)*

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 2]**
> *Given the disk request sequence for cylinders: **98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67**. The initial head position is at cylinder **53** and the disk has **200 cylinders (0–199)**.*

---

### 🅰️ Question A — 14 Marks

**(a) Compute the total head movement using the FCFS and SSTF algorithms. Show the order of service in each case. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**FCFS (3 marks):**
Served in arrival order: $53 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67$

$$
\text{THM}_{FCFS} = 45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 = \mathbf{640 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

**SSTF (4 marks):**
At head 53, closest is 65 (dist 12), then 67 (2), then 37 (30), then 14 (23), then 98 (84), then 122 (24), then 124 (2), then 183 (59).

$$
\text{THM}_{SSTF} = 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 = \mathbf{236 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

---

**(b) Compute the total head movement using SCAN and C-SCAN algorithms, assuming the head moves towards the higher-numbered cylinder first. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**SCAN (3.5 marks):**
Served: $53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 199 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14$

$$
\text{THM}_{SCAN} = 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 = \mathbf{331 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

**C-SCAN (3.5 marks):**
Served: $53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 199 \rightarrow 0 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 37$

$$
\text{THM}_{C\text{-}SCAN} = 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 199 + 14 + 23 = \mathbf{382 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) Compute the total head movement using the LOOK and C-LOOK algorithms for the same request sequence and initial head position. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**LOOK (3.5 marks):**
Served: $53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14$
(Note: head reverses at 183, not 199.)

$$
\text{THM}_{LOOK} = 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 146 + 23 = \mathbf{299 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

**C-LOOK (3.5 marks):**
Served: $53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 37$

$$
\text{THM}_{C\text{-}LOOK} = 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 169 + 23 = \mathbf{322 \text{ cylinders}}
$$

---

**(b) Tabulate the total head movement for all six algorithms and identify the best-performing algorithm. Justify. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

| **Algorithm** | **Total Head Movement (cyl)** |
|---|---|
| FCFS | 640 |
| SSTF | **236** ✅ |
| SCAN | 331 |
| C-SCAN | 382 |
| LOOK | 299 |
| C-LOOK | 322 |

**Best Performer: SSTF (4 marks for analysis):**
- SSTF yields the *minimum* head movement (236 cylinders) because it is a *greedy* algorithm that always picks the nearest pending request.
- **Caveat (3 marks):** SSTF is *susceptible to starvation* of far-away requests, and is *not* implementable in practice because it requires continuous knowledge of the future request queue. Hence, **LOOK** is often the practical choice — it gives a near-optimal result (299) while guaranteeing no starvation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the C-SCAN circular jump:** Many students compute C-SCAN like SCAN and miss the $\vert 199 - 0 \vert = 199$ jump. **This is a 5-mark deduction** in typical valuation.
> 2. **Confusing LOOK and SCAN end-points:** LOOK stops at the *last request*; SCAN goes to the *disk end (199)*. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not showing the *order of service* explicitly:** A bare numerical answer without the visited sequence is considered **incomplete** — KTU evaluators require the *service order table*.
> 4. **Off-by-one in SSTF:** At head 67, the two nearest are 65 and 37 (both 30 distance). The choice depends on tie-breaking; the canonical KTU answer for this problem is 37. Always check your greedy selection at every step.
> 5. **Units not mentioned:** Always write "cylinders" or "tracks" — bare numbers are penalized in viva.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ Disk scheduling **minimizes seek time** by ordering pending I/O requests.
- ✅ **FCFS** = arrival order, **no optimization**, no starvation, fair but slow.
- ✅ **SSTF** = greedy nearest-first, **minimum THM in this problem (236)**, but starvation-prone.
- ✅ **SCAN** = elevator sweep to disk edge (199), then reverse. **No starvation**.
- ✅ **C-SCAN** = sweep to 199, then **circular jump** to 0, sweep up again. **Uniform wait**.
- ✅ **LOOK** = like SCAN but reverses at the **last request** (saves edge traversal).
- ✅ **C-LOOK** = circular jump to **lowest pending request** (not 0), then continue up.
- ✅ The defining equation: $THM = \sum \vert r_{i+1} - r_i \vert$ for the actual sequence serviced.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 labs, always **display the service-order table** before computing the sum.
- ✅ Standard disk range in KTU problems: **0–199** (200 cylinders), unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- ✅ LOOK/C-LOOK are **practically preferred** in real OSes (Linux, Windows) due to balanced fairness + low THM.
- ✅ Modern **SSDs do not require disk scheduling** — only **HDDs and sequential-access media**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement file organization technique (single level and multi-level directory) and allocation strategies (sequential, linked and indexed)
Familiarization of cloud platforms and virtualization tools (e.g., VMWare)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL408 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Familiarization of cloud platforms and virtualization tools (e.g., VMWare) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Virtualization — Formal Academic Definition **Virtualization** is the process of creating a software-based (virtual) representation of a physical resource (server, storage, network, or operating system) using a specialized software layer called a **hypervisor** (also known as a Virtual Machine Monitor — VMM). The hypervisor abstracts the underlying physical hardware and partitions it into multiple isolated execution environments called **Virtual Machines (VMs)**, each of which runs its own complete operating system (Guest OS) and applications. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITL408):** Virtualization is recognized as the foundational technology that enables cloud computing. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to demonstrate hands-on familiarity with hypervisor-based tools such as **VMware Workstation / VMware Player / VirtualBox** and to understand how cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP, OpenStack) leverage the same principles. ## 1.2 Cloud Computing — Formal Academic Definition **Cloud Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that delivers on-demand, scalable, and elastic computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) over the public or private Internet on a **pay-per-use** basis, following the model defined by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**. ## 1.3 VMware — Tool-Specific Definition **VMware** is a pioneer suite of virtualization products developed by VMware Inc. (now a subsidiary of Broadcom). Key products in the academic/KTU lab context include: - **VMware Workstation Pro** — Type-2 hypervisor for Windows/Linux hosts. - **VMware Player (Workstation Player)** — Free Type-2 hypervisor for personal use. - **VMware ESXi** — Bare-metal (Type-1) enterprise hypervisor. - **VMware vSphere / vCenter** — Centralized management platform for ESXi hosts. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Apartment Building** > - Imagine a large **physical land plot** = a single physical server with fixed CPU, RAM, and storage. > - **Building the entire plot into one big house** = using the server for one OS only (wasteful if demand is low). > - **Building a 10-story apartment building on the same plot** = **Virtualization**. The **landlord** is the **Hypervisor**. Each **apartment** is a **Virtual Machine (VM)** with its own **door (NIC)**, **walls (allocated RAM)**, and **utility meters (allocated CPU/disk)**. Tenants (Guest OS) cannot see into each other's apartments — this is **isolation**. > - **Airbnb / Co-living spaces** = **Cloud Computing**. You don't own the building; you rent apartments on demand and pay only for the nights you stay. The cloud provider owns and manages the "building" (data center + hypervisor). ## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Resource Abstraction | Pillar | Hardware Realized As | Core Benefit | |---|---|---| | **Compute Virtualization** | Virtual CPU (vCPU) | Multiple OS instances share physical cores | | **Storage Virtualization** | Virtual Disks (VMDK, VHD) | Decouples OS from physical disk layout | | **Network Virtualization** | Virtual Switches (vSwitch), VLANs | Software-defined isolated networks | ## 1.6 Two Fundamental Hypervisor Categories > [!NOTE] > **Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal):** Runs directly on the host's hardware. Examples: **VMware ESXi**, Microsoft Hyper-V Server, Xen. Used in enterprise data centers and cloud platforms. > [!NOTE] > **Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted):** Runs as an application on a conventional host OS. Examples: **VMware Workstation**, Oracle VirtualBox. Used in KTU labs and developer workstations. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Major Modes of VMware Workstation Operation ### A. Bridged Networking Mode - The VM connects directly to the **physical network** of the host. - The VM receives its own unique IP address from the physical router's DHCP server. - The VM is visible to other machines on the LAN. - **Use case:** Running servers (e.g., a local web server) that must be reachable from other devices. ### B. NAT (Network Address Translation) Mode - The VM shares the host's IP address for outbound traffic. - The VM is hidden from the external LAN. - VMware provides an internal virtual DHCP server (e.g., `192.168.182.0/24`). - **Use case:** Internet access for the VM while keeping it isolated. **Default mode in KTU labs.** ### C. Host-Only Networking - VM can communicate with the host and other VMs but **cannot** access the internet or LAN. - Used for isolated lab testing. ## 2.2 Cloud Service Models — The NIST SP 500-292 Stack | Model | Full Form | User Manages | Provider Manages | KTU Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | OS, Middleware, Apps, Data | Hardware, Storage, Network, Virtualization | AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine | | **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Apps, Data | Everything else (OS, Middleware, Runtime) | Google App Engine, Heroku | | **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Only data/usage | Entire stack | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Zoom | | **FaaS** | Function-as-a-Service | Individual functions | All infrastructure | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions | ## 2.3 Cloud Deployment Models | Model | Scope | User Base | |---|---|---| | **Public Cloud** | Public Internet | General public (AWS, Azure, GCP) | | **Private Cloud** | On-premises / dedicated | Single organization (OpenStack) | | **Hybrid Cloud** | Mix of public + private with orchestration | Enterprises | | **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common concerns | Government, healthcare consortia | ## 2.4 Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing (NIST Definition) 1. **On-demand self-service** — Provision resources automatically. 2. **Broad network access** — Available over standard networks. 3. **Resource pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic reallocation. 4. **Rapid elasticity** — Scale up/down as needed. 5. **Measured service** — Pay-per-use metering (CPU-hours, GB-months). ## 2.5 Core Components of a VMware Virtual Machine | Component | File Extension | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Configuration File** | `.vmx` | Hardware configuration (RAM, CPU, NIC) | | **Virtual Disk** | `.vmdk` | Virtual hard drive contents | | **BIOS/UEFI Firmware** | `.nvram` | VM firmware state | | **Log File** | `.log` | Activity log for troubleshooting | | **Snapshot Data** | `-Snapshot*.vmsn` | Saved state for rollback | | **Memory File** | `.vmem` | VM's RAM dump (only when running) | ## 2.6 KTU Formula & Metric Sheet | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit / Boundary | |---|---|---| | **CPU Oversubscription Ratio** | $\text{Cores}_{\text{physical}} \times \text{Ratio} = \text{vCPUs}_{\text{allocated}}$ | Typical enterprise ratio: $1\!:\!4$ to $1\!:\!6$ | | **Memory Overcommit Ratio** | $\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Total VM RAM Allocated}}{\text{Physical RAM}}$ | Lab ratio: $1.0\!:\!1.0$; Cloud ratio: up to $1.5\!:\!1$ | | **Disk Capacity** | $\text{Capacity} = \text{Provisioned} + \text{Snapshot Overhead}$ | Provisioned size in **GB** | | **Storage I/O Latency** | $\text{Latency} = T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{transfer}}$ | Measured in **ms** | | **Cost Metering (Cloud)** | $\text{Cost} = (\text{vCPU} \times P_{\text{cpu}}) + (\text{RAM}_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{ram}}) + (\text{Storage}_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{storage}})$ | USD per hour | | **Availability SLA** | $\text{Downtime}_{\text{annual}} = 8760 \times (1 - \text{SLA}_{\%})$ | Hours/year (e.g., $99.9\% \rightarrow 8.76$ hrs) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in KTU 2024 Scheme questions on capacity planning, cloud cost estimation, and SLA analysis for System Administration & DevOps roles. Master the oversubscription and cost-metering equations — they are the most frequently asked in Part B. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Data Centers:** VMware ESXi + vSphere powers ~75% of enterprise virtualization. - **Cloud IaaS:** AWS EC2, Azure VM, and GCP Compute Engine are built on hypervisor-based virtualization. - **Dev/Test Environments:** KTU students and developers spin up disposable Linux VMs in minutes. - **Disaster Recovery:** VMware Site Recovery Manager (SRM) replicates VMs between data centers. - **Container & Microservice Hosts:** VMs serve as the secure host layer for Docker/Kubernetes clusters. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedures & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Creating a Virtual Machine in VMware Workstation > [!NOTE] > This procedure is the **core hands-on experiment** of KTU 2024 Scheme Module Experiment 2. Follow each step precisely; it forms the basis of the 14-mark lab exam question. ### Step 1: Verify System Prerequisites - **OS:** Windows 10/11 (64-bit) or Linux (Ubuntu 20.04+ recommended). - **RAM:** Minimum **8 GB** physical (so you can allocate 2–4 GB to VM). - **CPU:** 64-bit processor with **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V** enabled in BIOS. - **Disk:** At least **50 GB** free for the VM + VMware installation. **Verification Command on Windows (PowerShell):** ```powershell systeminfo | findstr /C:"Hyper-V" ``` **On Linux:** ```bash grep -E 'vmx|svm' /proc/cpuinfo ``` If the output is non-empty, hardware virtualization is supported. ### Step 2: Install VMware Workstation - Download the installer from Broadcom's official portal. - Run as Administrator → Accept EULA → Choose **Typical** install. - Enter license key (or select 30-day trial for KTU lab). ### Step 3: Create a New Virtual Machine - Launch VMware Workstation → Click **"Create a New Virtual Machine"**. - Select **"Typical (recommended)"** configuration. - Choose installation source: - **Installer disc** (ISO file): select `.iso` of Ubuntu/CentOS/Windows. - **Installer disc drive** (physical DVD). - **I will install the operating system later** (manual config). ### Step 4: Configure Guest OS Identity - **Guest OS:** Linux - **Version:** Ubuntu 64-bit - **VM Name:** `KTU_Ubuntu_24` - **Location:** `D:\VMs\KTU_Ubuntu_24\` ### Step 5: Allocate Disk Capacity - **Maximum disk size:** **20.0 GB** (recommended for lab). - Select **"Split virtual disk into multiple files"** (easier to move/transfer). - Click **Next → Finish**. ### Step 6: Customize Hardware (Before First Boot) Click **"Customize Hardware"** to set: | Component | Recommended Lab Setting | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Memory (RAM)** | 2048 MB (2 GB) | Sufficient for Ubuntu desktop without choking host | | **Processors** | 2 cores, 1 socket | Parallelism for browser/IDE | | **Network Adapter** | NAT (default) | Internet access without exposing VM to LAN | | **CD/DVD** | Use ISO image | Boot from the installer ISO | | **USB Controller** | Present | For pendrive passthrough | | **Display** | 3D graphics enabled, 1 GB VRAM | Smooth UI rendering | | **Hard Disk** | SATA, 20 GB provisioned | Standard lab disk | ### Step 7: Power On and Install Guest OS - Click **"Power on this virtual machine"**. - The VM boots from the attached ISO. - Follow the Ubuntu installation wizard: - Language → Keyboard → Network (DHCP auto-assigns IP from VMware NAT). - **Disk partition:** Erase disk and install (entire 20 GB to Ubuntu). - Create user: e.g., `student` with password `ktu@2024`. - Reboot when prompted. ### Step 8: Install VMware Tools VMware Tools is a suite of utilities (drivers, time-sync, clipboard sharing, drag-and-drop, screen resize). - Menu: **VM → Install VMware Tools** (mounts a virtual CD). - Inside the VM, mount the CD and run the installer: ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install open-vm-tools open-vm-tools-desktop -y sudo reboot ``` > [!TIP] > Without VMware Tools, the VM cannot dynamically resize the screen, share clipboard, or sync time with the host. KTU evaluators often deduct marks if VMware Tools is not installed. ### Step 9: Configure Snapshot for Clean State - **VM → Snapshot → Take Snapshot** - Name: `KTU_Base_Install` - Description: `Fresh Ubuntu with VMware Tools installed.` - This allows the student to revert to a known-good state after any experiment. ### Step 10: Verify Network Connectivity ```bash ip addr show # Check assigned IP (usually 192.168.182.x) ping 8.8.8.8 # Test internet ping 192.168.182.2 # Test host (VMware NAT gateway) ``` ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Lab Tools | Feature | **VMware Workstation Pro** | **Oracle VirtualBox** | **Microsoft Hyper-V** | **KVM (Linux)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Type** | Type-2 | Type-2 | Type-1 (also hybrid) | Type-1 | | **Host OS** | Windows, Linux | Windows, Linux, macOS | Windows only | Linux only | | **License** | Commercial (paid) | Open-source (GPL) | Bundled with Windows Pro | Open-source | | **Snapshots** | Yes (multi-level) | Yes | Yes | Yes (via libvirt) | | **3D Graphics** | Excellent (DirectX 11) | Limited | Good | Good | | **Cloud Integration** | Strong (vSphere, AWS) | Limited | Azure integration | OpenStack | | **KTU Lab Use** | Primary | Alternative | Limited | Advanced | ## 3.3 Cloud Platform Comparison Matrix | Feature | **AWS EC2** | **Microsoft Azure VM** | **Google Compute Engine** | **OpenStack (Private)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Market Leader** | Yes (≈32% share) | Strong #2 | #3 | Private cloud | | **Instance Types** | 400+ | 200+ | 100+ | Custom flavors | | **Free Tier** | 12 months (t2.micro) | 12 months + $200 credit | 90 days + $300 credit | Free (self-hosted) | | **Hypervisor** | Xen / Nitro (KVM-based) | Hyper-V (custom) | KVM | KVM (default) | | **KTU Student Use** | Recommended | Common | Common | University DC | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Cost Metering for a Cloud VM A student needs an **AWS EC2 t3.medium** instance (2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) running for **730 hours/month** with **100 GB SSD storage**. Compute rate = **$0.0416/hr**, Storage = **$0.10/GB-month**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{\text{compute}} &= \text{Hours} \times \text{Rate}_{\text{hr}} \\ &= 730 \times 0.0416 \\ &= \$30.368 \\ \\ \text{Cost}_{\text{storage}} &= \text{Size}_{\text{GB}} \times \text{Rate}_{\text{GB-month}} \\ &= 100 \times 0.10 \\ &= \$10.00 \\ \\ \text{Cost}_{\text{total}} &= \text{Cost}_{\text{compute}} + \text{Cost}_{\text{storage}} \\ &= 30.368 + 10.00 \\ &= \$40.368 \text{ per month} \end{aligned} $$ **Logical Explanation:** - **Line 1:** Identifies the cost-per-hour metric and the monthly runtime (730 = 24 × 30.4 average days). - **Line 2:** Multiplies the rate by the number of hours to obtain raw compute cost. - **Line 3:** Storage is metered per GB per month, so the equation is multiplicative. - **Line 4:** Total bill = sum of all metered resources (compute, storage, network egress, etc.). ## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — SLA Downtime Calculation An SLA of **99.95%** is offered. Calculate maximum annual downtime. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Availability} &= 99.95\% = 0.9995 \\ \text{Total hours/year} &= 365 \times 24 = 8760 \\ \text{Allowed Downtime}_{\text{year}} &= 8760 \times (1 - 0.9995) \\ &= 8760 \times 0.0005 \\ &= 4.38 \text{ hours/year} \\ \text{Allowed Downtime}_{\text{month}} &= \dfrac{4.38}{12} \approx 0.365 \text{ hours} \\ &\approx 21.9 \text{ minutes/month} \end{aligned} $$ **Logical Explanation:** - **Line 1:** SLA percentages are decimalized for arithmetic. - **Line 2:** Standard year in hours. - **Line 3:** The "unavailability" fraction is $(1 - A)$; multiplying by total time gives the budget for downtime. - **Line 4:** Monthly allowance aids operational planning of maintenance windows. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram: Layered Architecture of a Virtualized System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION LAYER"] APP1["Guest App 1"] APP2["Guest App 2"] APP3["Host App"] end subgraph GuestOS["GUEST OS LAYER"] GOS1["Windows 11 VM"] GOS2["Ubuntu 24.04 VM"] HOS["Windows 11 Host OS"] end subgraph Hyper["HYPERVISOR LAYER - VMM"] HYP["VMware Workstation Pro
Type-2 Hypervisor"] end subgraph HwLayer["PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER"] CPU["Intel i7 CPU
VT-x Enabled"] RAM["32 GB DDR4 RAM"] DISK["1 TB SSD"] NIC["1 Gbps NIC"] end APP1 --> GOS1 APP2 --> GOS2 APP3 --> HOS GOS1 --> HYP GOS2 --> HYP HOS --> HYP HYP --> CPU HYP --> RAM HYP --> DISK HYP --> NIC ``` **Interpretation:** This block diagram shows how the **hypervisor** sits between the Guest OSes and the actual hardware. In a Type-2 model (VMware Workstation), the hypervisor is itself an application running on the host OS. In a Type-1 model (ESXi), the host OS layer is removed and the hypervisor runs directly on hardware. ## 4.2 Diagram: Cloud Service Models (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS / FaaS) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SaaS["SAAS - User manages only DATA"] S1["Gmail"] S2["Microsoft 365"] S3["Zoom"] end subgraph PaaS["PAAS - User manages APPS and DATA"] P1["Google App Engine"] P2["Heroku"] P3["Azure App Service"] end subgraph IaaS["IAAS - User manages OS, APPS, DATA"] I1["AWS EC2"] I2["Azure Virtual Machines"] I3["Google Compute Engine"] end subgraph FaaS["FAAS - User manages FUNCTIONS only"] F1["AWS Lambda"] F2["Azure Functions"] F3["Google Cloud Functions"] end FaaS --> IaaS IaaS --> PaaS PaaS --> SaaS ``` **Interpretation:** This vertical stack visualizes the **abstraction gradient**. Lower models (FaaS, IaaS) provide more control but require more user management. Higher models (PaaS, SaaS) reduce user burden at the cost of flexibility. KTU questions often ask students to identify which service model a given real-world product belongs to. ## 4.3 Diagram: VMware VM Creation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Step 1: Launch VMware"] --> B["Step 2: Create New VM"] B --> C["Step 3: Select ISO Installer"] C --> D["Step 4: Configure HW
RAM, CPU, Disk"] D --> E["Step 5: Power On"] E --> F["Step 6: Install Guest OS"] F --> G["Step 7: Install VMware Tools"] G --> H["Step 8: Take Base Snapshot"] H --> I["Step 9: Verify Network"] I --> J["Step 10: VM Ready for Lab"] ``` **Interpretation:** A linear, sequential workflow that mirrors the **exact procedure** in Section 3.1. KTU lab record books should reproduce this diagram in the methodology section. ## 4.4 Diagram: VMware Networking Modes ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bridged["BRIDGED MODE"] B_VM["VM IP: 192.168.1.50"] B_HOST["Host IP: 192.168.1.10"] B_ROUTER["Physical Router"] B_VM --- B_ROUTER B_HOST --- B_ROUTER end subgraph NAT["NAT MODE"] N_VM["VM IP: 192.168.182.10"] N_NAT["VMware NAT Engine
Host IP: 192.168.182.2"] N_INET["Internet"] N_VM --> N_NAT N_NAT --> N_INET end subgraph HostOnly["HOST-ONLY MODE"] H_VM["VM IP: 192.168.50.10"] H_HOST["Host vNIC: 192.168.50.1"] H_VM --- H_HOST end ``` **Interpretation:** Each networking mode is a separate isolated environment. KTU lab exams frequently test the difference between Bridged and NAT — students should note that **NAT hides the VM from the LAN**, while **Bridged exposes it**. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Define the term "Hypervisor" and list its two main types with one example each.** ### Model Answer (3 marks): A **hypervisor** (or Virtual Machine Monitor — VMM) is a software layer that creates, runs, and manages multiple virtual machines on a single physical host by abstracting the underlying hardware. The two main types are: 1. **Type-1 (Bare-Metal):** Runs directly on hardware with no host OS. Example: **VMware ESXi**, Microsoft Hyper-V Server. 2. **Type-2 (Hosted):** Runs as an application over a conventional host OS. Example: **VMware Workstation Pro**, Oracle VirtualBox. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]** > **Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one real-world example for each.** ### Model Answer (3 marks): | Model | User Responsibility | Example | |---|---|---| | **IaaS** | User installs OS, middleware, apps; provider supplies virtualized hardware | **AWS EC2** | | **PaaS** | User deploys only applications; provider manages OS, runtime, and middleware | **Google App Engine** | | **SaaS** | User only consumes the finished software via browser; provider manages the entire stack | **Microsoft 365 / Gmail** | The core distinction is the **abstraction level** — moving up the stack transfers more management responsibility to the cloud provider. --- ## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice) > **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]** **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **architecture of VMware Workstation** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of the hypervisor, virtual NIC, virtual disk (VMDK), and VMware Tools in this architecture. **(b)** [7 marks] Illustrate the **step-by-step procedure** to create a Linux virtual machine in VMware Workstation.
