Include snapshot creation and VMware Tools installation. ### Model Solution: **(a) Architecture of VMware Workstation (7 marks):** VMware Workstation uses a **Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor** architecture with the following layers (top to bottom): 1. **Guest OS Layer:** Independent OS instances (e.g., Windows 11 VM, Ubuntu VM) running concurrently. 2. **VMware Tools Layer:** Drivers and utilities installed inside each guest for time-sync, clipboard sharing, drag-and-drop, and dynamic screen resize. 3. **Virtual Hardware Layer:** Emulated devices presented to the guest: - **vCPU** — abstracted CPU cores. - **Virtual RAM** — portion of host's physical RAM. - **Virtual Disk (VMDK)** — file-based virtual hard drive. - **Virtual NIC (VMnet0/VMnet8)** — connected to Bridged, NAT, or Host-only network. 4. **VMware Workstation (VMM) Layer:** Core hypervisor that schedules CPU, memory, and I/O resources among VMs. 5. **Host OS Layer:** Windows/Linux operating system. 6. **Physical Hardware Layer:** CPU with VT-x, RAM, disk, and NIC. **Function of key components:** - **Hypervisor (VMM):** Allocates physical resources to VMs and enforces isolation. - **Virtual NIC:** Connects the VM to a virtual network. **VMnet0 = Bridged**, **VMnet8 = NAT**, **VMnet1 = Host-only**. - **Virtual Disk (VMDK):** A single file (or set of split files) acting as the VM's hard drive. - **VMware Tools:** Improves integration between guest and host; without it, screen resolution is fixed at 800×600 and clipboard sharing is disabled. **(b) Step-by-Step VM Creation (7 marks):** The complete procedure is as enumerated in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–10**. Key checkpoint commands and actions: - **Prerequisite check:** `systeminfo | findstr /C:"Hyper-V"` (Windows) or `grep -E 'vmx|svm' /proc/cpuinfo` (Linux). - **ISO mounting:** VM Settings → CD/DVD → Use ISO image. - **Hardware allocation:** 2 GB RAM, 2 CPU cores, 20 GB disk, NAT network. - **OS installation:** Follow Ubuntu wizard → create user `student` / `ktu@2024`. - **VMware Tools installation:** ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install open-vm-tools open-vm-tools-desktop -y sudo reboot ``` - **Snapshot creation:** VM → Snapshot → Take Snapshot → Name: `KTU_Base_Install`. - **Verification:** `ip addr show`, `ping 8.8.8.8`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Missing VMware Tools installation** — Examiners deduct **2 marks** if VMware Tools is not installed in the live demo. > 2. **Wrong networking mode** — Using Bridged when NAT is required, or vice versa, costs **1 mark** plus practical-time loss. > 3. **No snapshot** — Not taking a base snapshot before running risky operations loses **1 mark** in the record. > 4. **Confusing `.vmx` with `.vmdk`** — Examiners often ask: "Which file holds the VM configuration?" The answer is **`.vmx`**, not `.vmdk` (which is the virtual disk). > 5. **Forgetting to enable VT-x in BIOS** — VMs may fail to boot with "VT-x not available" error; this is the **#1 setup-time failure** in KTU labs. --- > **Q3B. [Alternative Module Choice, 14 Marks]** **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **NIST definition of cloud computing** and enumerate its **five essential characteristics**, **three service models**, and **four deployment models** with examples. **(b)** [7 marks] Compare the working of **Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors** with a block diagram. State two advantages of each, and give two examples of cloud platforms built on each type. ### Model Solution: **(a) NIST Definition and Classification (7 marks):** The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** defines cloud computing as: > *"A model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."* — **NIST SP 800-145** **Five Essential Characteristics:** 1. **On-demand self-service** — Resources provisioned automatically without human intervention. 2. **Broad network access** — Available over the Internet via standard protocols. 3. **Resource pooling** — Multi-tenant model with physical/virtual resources dynamically reassigned. 4. **Rapid elasticity** — Capabilities scaled up or down quickly, often automatically. 5. **Measured service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use via metering (pay-per-use). **Three Service Models:** 1. **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Raw VMs and storage. Example: **AWS EC2**. 2. **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Application deployment platform. Example: **Heroku**. 3. **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Ready-to-use applications. Example: **Gmail**. **Four Deployment Models:** 1. **Public Cloud** — Open to the public (AWS, Azure). 2. **Private Cloud** — Used by a single organization (on-prem OpenStack). 3. **Hybrid Cloud** — Combination of public and private with orchestration. 4. **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., government clouds). **(b) Type-1 vs Type-2 Hypervisors (7 marks):** | Parameter | **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | **Type-2 (Hosted)** | |---|---|---| | **Position** | Runs directly on hardware | Runs as an app on host OS | | **Performance** | High (no host OS overhead) | Lower (double scheduling) | | **Use Case** | Data centers, cloud | Labs, dev/test | | **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Hyper-V Server, Xen | VMware Workstation, VirtualBox | | **Cloud Platforms** | AWS (Nitro/Xen), Azure (Hyper-V), GCP (KVM) | KTU student laptops | **Block diagram (textual representation):** Type-1 Stack: ``` [ Guest OS 1 ] [ Guest OS 2 ] [ Guest OS 3 ] \ | / [ HYPERVISOR (ESXi) ] [ PHYSICAL HARDWARE ] ``` Type-2 Stack: ``` [ Guest OS 1 ] [ Guest OS 2 ] \ / [ VMware Workstation ] [ HOST OS ] [ PHYSICAL HARDWARE ] ``` **Two advantages of each:** - **Type-1 advantages:** (i) Higher performance and efficiency; (ii) Better isolation and security suitable for multi-tenant cloud. - **Type-2 advantages:** (i) Easy to install and use on a regular desktop; (ii) Supports rapid prototyping and dev/test. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PCITL408 — Module Experiment 2:** - [x] **Virtualization** creates multiple isolated VMs on a single physical host using a **hypervisor**. - [x] **Hypervisor (VMM)** is the software layer that abstracts hardware; comes in **Type-1 (bare-metal)** and **Type-2 (hosted)**. - [x] **VMware Workstation** is a Type-2 hypervisor used in KTU labs; **ESXi** is the Type-1 enterprise variant. - [x] A VM consists of a **`.vmx` configuration file**, a **`.vmdk` virtual disk**, **`.nvram` firmware**, and **`.log` files**. - [x] **VMware Tools** is mandatory — installs drivers, enables clipboard, drag-drop, and screen resize. - [x] **Three VMware networks:** **Bridged** (LAN-visible), **NAT** (default, hidden, internet via host), **Host-only** (no internet). - [x] **Cloud computing** is on-demand, elastic, metered, pooled, and self-serviced — per **NIST SP 800-145**. - [x] **Four service models:** **IaaS** (EC2), **PaaS** (App Engine), **SaaS** (Gmail), **FaaS** (Lambda). - [x] **Four deployment models:** **Public, Private, Hybrid, Community**. - [x] **Sliding abstraction:** FaaS → IaaS → PaaS → SaaS transfers more control to the provider. - [x] **Snapshot** captures VM state and is essential before risky lab operations. - [x] **Hardware prerequisite:** **VT-x (Intel)** or **AMD-V** must be enabled in BIOS for 64-bit VMs. - [x] **Cloud cost formula:** $\text{Cost} = \text{Hours} \times \text{Rate}_{\text{hr}} + \text{Storage}_{\text{GB}} \times \text{Rate}_{\text{GB-month}}$. - [x] **SLA downtime formula:** $\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = 8760 \times (1 - \text{SLA}_{\%})$. - [x] Always take a **base snapshot** after a clean install and VMware Tools setup — this is the rollback safety net. - [x] **Common exam trap:** Confusing `.vmx` (configuration) with `.vmdk` (disk) — remember **X = config, D = disk**.
